Q33. When creating `matrix3` using `matrix(1:9, nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)`, what will be the value in 
`matrix3[1, 2]`?
   (A) A. 1
   (B) B. 2
   (C) C. 4
   (D) D. 7
Q34. If `matrix3` was created using `matrix(1:9, nrow = 3, byrow = FALSE)` (default), what would be 
the value in `matrix3[2, 1]`?
   (A) A. 1
   (B) B. 2
   (C) C. 4
   (D) D. 7
Q35. Consider a matrix `M` with 5 rows and 3 columns. What does `M[3, ]` return?
   (A) A. The element in the 3rd row and 3rd column.
   (B) B. The entire 3rd row of the matrix.
   (C) C. The entire 3rd column of the matrix.
   (D) D. An error, as a column index is missing.
Q36. What is the outcome of `matrix1[-c(2:4), ]` if `matrix1` has 10 rows?
   (A) A. It prints rows 2, 3, and 4.
   (B) B. It prints all rows except rows 2, 3, and 4.
   (C) C. It prints an error because negative indexing is not allowed.
   (D) D. It only prints the 2nd and 4th rows.
Q37. Which of the following is NOT a type of vector discussed in the previous class, according to the 
recap?
   (A) A. Numeric vector
   (B) B. Character vector
   (C) C. Logical vector
   (D) D. Factor vector
Q38. What happens if a working directory is not set before attempting to read a CSV file using 
`read.csv()`?
   (A) A. R will automatically search the entire computer for the file.
   (B) B. R will assume the file is in the RStudio installation directory.
   (C) C. R will look for the file in its current default directory, likely leading to an error if not found there.
   (D) D. R will prompt the user to select the file location manually.
Q39. Why did the instructor call the occurrence of an error (using `matrix` instead of `matrix1`) in the 
live coding a 'good thing'?
   (A) A. Errors are necessary to learn debugging.
   (B) B. It demonstrated how red error messages appear in R.
   (C) C. It showed that the lecturer is also human.
   (D) D. Both A and B.
Q40. When creating a sequence with `length.out`, like `seq(start, end, length.out = N)`, what does `N` 
represent?
   (A) A. The step size between consecutive numbers.
   (B) B. The number of intervals between `start` and `end`.
   (C) C. The total number of elements in the resulting sequence.
   (D) D. The maximum value allowed in the sequence.
Q41. What is the typical function used to view a matrix or data frame in a spreadsheet-like format 
within RStudio?
   (A) A. `print()`
   (B) B. `summary()`
   (C) C. `VIEW()` or `View()`
   (D) D. `show()`
Q42. You have a data frame `my_df` and want to create a line plot of its `Price` column against its 
`Date` column. Assuming `Date` is suitable for the x-axis, which command would be most 
appropriate?
   (A) A. `plot(my_df$Price, type='l')`
   (B) B. `plot(my_df$Date, my_df$Price, type='l')`
   (C) C. `plot(my_df$Date, my_df$Price)`
   (D) D. `lines(my_df$Price, my_df$Date)`
Q43. When creating a matrix using `cbind()` with vectors of different lengths, what typically happens?
   (A) A. R truncates the longer vectors to match the shortest one.
   (B) B. R generates an error, requiring all vectors to be of equal length.
   (C) C. R recycles the shorter vectors to match the length of the longest one.
   (D) D. R pads the shorter vectors with `NA` values.
Q44. What is the main purpose of `row.names = FALSE` when saving a data frame to a CSV file using 
`write.csv()`?
   (A) A. To prevent R from writing the row numbers as a column in the CSV file.
   (B) B. To indicate that the data frame has no row names defined.
   (C) C. To improve file read/write performance.
   (D) D. To encrypt the row names for security.
Q45. If you are learning to code in R, why does the instructor stress 'keeping things clean' by closing 
other open files and clearing the environment?
   (A) A. To save computational resources for complex tasks.
   (B) B. To avoid confusion and focus on the current script and its output.
   (C) C. To prevent data corruption in other open files.
   (D) D. To speed up the RStudio IDE startup time.
Q46. What could be a reason for 'warning messages' appearing in R, as mentioned in the lecture?
   (A) A. They always indicate a critical error that stops execution.
   (B) B. They suggest a potential issue but allow the code to run.
   (C) C. They are simply informational messages with no impact.
   (D) D. They mean R has run out of memory.
Q47. You have a matrix `M` with 10 rows and 3 columns. What does `M[c(1, 5, 10), 2]` return?
   (A) A. The element at row 1, column 5, then row 10, column 2.
   (B) B. The elements in the 1st, 5th, and 10th rows, but only from the 2nd column.
   (C) C. The entire 1st, 5th, and 10th rows.
   (D) D. An error, as column index should be `c()` for multiple rows.
Q48. Which basic plotting types are specifically mentioned by the instructor as things students 
should 'go and search how to do it'?
   (A) A. Histogram, Heatmap, Density Plot
   (B) B. Bar Chart, Box Plot, Pie Chart
   (C) C. Violin Plot, Scatter Plot, Line Plot
   (D) D. Area Chart, Bubble Chart, Contour Plot
Q49. When `matrix(data, nrow=3)` is used with `data` containing 9 values, what can be inferred about 
the number of columns?
   (A) A. It will be 9 columns.
   (B) B. It will be 3 columns.
   (C) C. It will be 1 column.
   (D) D. It will produce an error if `ncol` is not specified.
Q50. What is the key functional difference between `cbind` and `rbind` when creating a matrix from 
vectors `x`, `y`, `z`?
   (A) A. `cbind` puts `x,y,z` as columns, `rbind` puts them as rows.
   (B) B. `cbind` converts to character, `rbind` preserves original types.
   (C) C. `cbind` requires all vectors to be numeric, `rbind` allows mixed types.
   (D) D. `cbind` works only for 2 vectors, `rbind` for any number.
Q51. If you want to view the `gh` column of `data1` (a data frame), but only the values between 6 and 
10, which code snippet uses conditional subsetting correctly to achieve this?
   (A) A. `data1$gh[data1$gh > 6 & data1$gh < 10]`
   (B) B. `data1$gh(data1$gh > 6 & data1$gh < 10)`
   (C) C. `subset(data1$gh, gh > 6 & gh < 10)`
   (D) D. `data1[data1$gh > 6 & data1$gh < 10]`
Q52. What does the `$` operator primarily allow you to do with a data frame?
   (A) A. Perform arithmetic operations on its columns.
   (B) B. Create new columns based on existing ones.
   (C) C. Access a column by its name.
   (D) D. Convert the data frame to a list.
Q53. You define `A <- 1:10`. What is the data type of `A`?
   (A) A. Double
   (B) B. Numeric
   (C) C. Integer
   (D) D. Character
Q54. Why is it important to ensure the R script file `W1S3.r` is saved before setting the working 
directory to 'source file location' in RStudio?
   (A) A. RStudio needs to know the file's content to set the directory.
   (B) B. If not saved, RStudio cannot determine the source file's location.
   (C) C. Saving the file prevents accidental overwriting of the working directory.
   (D) D. It's a symbolic step with no practical effect.
Q55. Which statement best describes the difference between how column names are treated in 
matrices versus data frames?
   (A) A. Matrices don't support column names; data frames do.
   (B) B. Both support column names, but only data frames allow direct access using the `$` operator with names.
   (C) C. Both support column names, but matrices require them to be numeric.
   (D) D. Data frames use numbers for columns; matrices use names.
Q56. What would be the dimensions of a matrix created by `rbind(vec1, vec2)` where `vec1` and 
`vec2` are both vectors of length 5?
   (A) A. 5 rows, 2 columns
   (B) B. 2 rows, 5 columns
   (C) C. 1 row, 10 columns
   (D) D. 10 rows, 1 column
Q57. If you have a matrix `my_matrix` and you write `my_matrix[2, 3]` and get 'Sachin' as the output, 
what does this tell you about the matrix?
   (A) A. The matrix is numeric.
   (B) B. The matrix is a data frame.
   (C) C. The element at row 2, column 3 is the character string 'Sachin'.
   (D) D. The matrix contains only names.
Q58. What is the primary visual difference between a data frame and a matrix when viewed using 
`View()` in RStudio, regarding the alignment of values?
   (A) A. Data frames always right-align numbers; matrices always left-align everything.
   (B) B. Data frames left-align characters and right-align numbers; matrices left-align everything if character-coerced.
   (C) C. Data frames right-align all values; matrices center-align all values.
   (D) D. There is no visual difference in alignment.
Q59. If a data set has '10 observations of 3 variables', how many individual data points (cells) does it 
contain?
   (A) A. 10
   (B) B. 3
   (C) C. 30
   (D) D. 13
Q60. What is the most flexible data type in R that other types (like numeric, integer) are coerced into 
when combined with it in a matrix, as exemplified in the lecture?
   (A) A. Numeric
   (B) B. Integer
   (C) C. Logical
   (D) D. Character
Answer Key
Q1: A
The speaker advises pressing 'Control L' to clean the console. This is a common shortcut in RStudio.
Q2: B
The lecture explicitly mentions clicking a 'small brush sign' in the global environment pane to clear it.
Q3: C
The lecture refers to a prior discussion about how character and factor vectors differ, emphasizing that factor vectors 
are for categorical data, which implies treating elements as levels rather than just raw text.
Q4: B
The lecture clearly states, 'each cell will has to continuous similar type of data. So if one cell is numeric the all other 
cells has to be numeric... matrix all the elements in a particular matrix has to be same.'
Q5: C
The lecture illustrates, 'each cell is known by its row number first and column number second. So if I want to find out 
what is the average of this particular cell this is 2, 3. 2 is the row number and 3 is the column number.'
Q6: C
The lecture explains that if you combine vectors of different types, 'because character vector is superior than them... 
they converted everything to a character and the matrix that is when created is a character matrix.' R performs type 
coercion to the most flexible type.
Q7: C
The lecture explicitly uses `cbind(a, b, c)` to combine vectors `a`, `b`, and `c` into columns of a new matrix.
Q8: B
The speaker contrasts `cbind` (column bind) with `rbind` (row bind), showing that `rbind` stacks vectors as rows, while 
`cbind` places them side-by-side as columns.
Q9: B
The lecture introduces `matrix()` for situations where 'we populate a matrix also' by providing values and dimensions 
like `n row` (number of rows).
Q10: C
The `matrix()` function uses `nrow` for the number of rows and `byrow = TRUE` for row-wise population. The `c()` is 
optional but common for sequences. Option A uses `by.row` instead of `byrow` and D uses `rows` and `by_row` which 
are incorrect arguments.
Q11: B
The lecture states, 'if I do not write anything column wise will happen and if I write byrow is equal to true then row wise 
will happen.' This indicates the default is column-wise (which corresponds to `byrow = FALSE`).
Q12: B
While not explicitly stated in a single sentence, the creation of data frames with `a` (numeric), `b` (numeric), and `c` 
(character) vectors as columns implies that data frames do not suffer from the same single-data-type constraint as 
matrices.
Q13: C
The lecture demonstrates this: `data.frame(gh = a, ij = b, kl = c)`, where 'gh', 'ij', and 'kl' become the column names.
Q14: C
The lecture states, 'In case of matrix it is 1 to, 1 colon 10, 1 colon 3. On the other hand in case of data set it is saying 10 
observations of 3 variables.'
Q15: D
Matrix subsetting uses square brackets `[row, column]`. The row number comes first, then the column number, 
separated by a comma.
Q16: D
The lecture illustrates this exactly: `matrix1[2, C 2, 3]` (where C refers to `c()` for combining values) to get the second 
row and second and third columns.
Q17: C
To select all rows, you leave the row index blank before the comma. To select specific columns, you use `c()` or a range 
after the comma.
Q18: A
To select a range of rows, you use `start_row:end_row` before the comma. To select all columns, you leave the column 
index blank after the comma.
Q19: C
The lecture states, 'I want to print all rows, other than second to fourth row, all I have to write is minus, minus 2 to 4.' 
This shows that the minus sign excludes elements.
Q20: B
The lecture explicitly states, 'data 1 dollar gh that is how you call up at this is only applicable for data frame not 
applicable for matrix. You cannot call a matrix’s column with the name of the column.'
Q21: C
The lecture demonstrates this exact syntax: `data1[ (data1$gh > 6 & data1$gh < 10), ]`. The `&` operator is for logical 
AND, and the comma after the condition with nothing specified for columns selects all columns for the matching rows.
Q22: C
The lecture clearly states, 'here I get a scatter plot, I get gh as x, ij as y and this is the scatter plot of that particular 
thing.'
Q23: C
The lecture shows, 'you all the thing that you have to do is plot data dollar GH probably type is equal to l and if I run this 
so you will get a line curve'.
Q24: C
The lecture emphasizes, 'again good practice is that you put a working directory... remember before doing write or read 
whatever you have set working directory properly.'
Q25: B
The lecture demonstrates this: `write dot CSV data 1 comma let us say data 1 dot CSV and row names is equal to false`.
Q26: C
The lecture explicitly uses `rm data 1` to remove `data1` from memory.
Q27: C
The lecture uses `data 1 is equal to read dot CSV and then within quotes data 1 dot CSV`.
Q28: C
The speaker instructs students to 'open that file in R studio' and then proceeds to run lines of code from it, indicating it's 
an R script.
Q29: C
The lecture describes `A 1 to 10` as 'particularly a integer vector which starts from 1 goes up to 10.'
Q30: B
The lecture explains `seq` as 'a sequence which starts from 2 ends at 10 and the length output is also 10... these values 
are actually broken into probably 9 equal intervals, so that you get 10 values.' This describes evenly spaced numbers.
Q31: B
The lecture's example for data frame creation is `data.frame(gh = a, ij = b, kl = c)`, where the desired column name is 
on the left of the equals sign.
Q32: A
This is standard statistical terminology. Observations are typically rows (individual data points), and variables are 
columns (characteristics measured for each observation).
Q33: B
Populating `1:9` row-wise into a 3x3 matrix means the first row is `1, 2, 3`. So, the element at row 1, column 2 is 2.
Q34: B
Populating `1:9` column-wise into a 3x3 matrix means the first column is `1, 2, 3`. So, the element at row 2, column 1 is 
2.
Q35: B
Leaving the column index blank (`M[rows, ]`) means selecting all columns for the specified rows. In this case, it selects 
the entire 3rd row.
Q36: B
The lecture explains that `minus C 2 to 4` means 'it does not print second to fourth row, it prints all other rows.'
Q37: C
The lecture explicitly mentions 'vectors, we have talked about numeric vectors, we have talked about character vector, 
we have talked about factor vector'. Logical vectors were not mentioned in this recap.
Q38: C
A working directory is the default location. If not set, R uses its current default, and if the file isn't there, it won't be found.
Q39: D
The instructor said, 'this is a good thing that the error come, so the red error it will be written.' This highlights both the 
visibility of errors and the learning opportunity.
Q40: C
The lecture explains `length.out = 10` means 'so that you get 10 values'. So `N` determines the total count of numbers 
in the sequence.
Q41: C
The lecture states, 'I can also click here or I can write capital V I E W, view within bracket matrix and that will give me a 
spreadsheet like view of the matrix.'
Q42: B
For a line plot of one variable against another, you specify `x` and `y` arguments in `plot()` and set `type='l'`. Option A 
only plots Price against its index, not Date. Option C is a scatter plot. Option D is typically for adding lines to an 
*existing* plot.
Q43: C
While not explicitly covered in *this* lecture, a common behavior in R (and implied by the `cbind`/`rbind` examples which 
expect equal lengths or multiples) is vector recycling. For matrices, unequal lengths that are not multiples often result in 
errors or warnings. However, the lecture uses equal length vectors so the question is a bit outside the transcript, but a 
plausible distractor based on general R knowledge.
Q44: A
The lecture states, 'row dot names is equal to false means I do not have any row names I only have column names.' 
This is the primary reason for preventing the default R row numbers from appearing as an unnecessary first column in 
the CSV.
Q45: B
The instructor says, 'it is very important that you keep things clean when you are learning a little bit of coding. So any 
other file if that is open here please close it... console should be clear, your global environment should be clear.' The 
emphasis is on clarity and focus.
Q46: B
The lecture explains, 'Warning messages is something if it is not something which you cannot understand, you have to 
think a little bit whether it has some meaning or it is relevant otherwise you can ignore it as well.' This implies they are 
not always critical errors.
Q47: B
The `c()` function is used to specify multiple indices. `M[c(rows), column]` extracts specific rows from a single column. 
So it will return three elements, one from each specified row, all from the second column.
Q48: B
The lecture advises, 'for example the bar chart is something that you should know, you should go and search how to do 
it... how to plot bar chart, how to plot a box plot, how to plot a Pi chart these are some of the basic things that you can 
learn.'
Q49: B
The lecture explains, 'if there are 9 values and number of rows is equal to 3 then how many columns do you want? 3 
obviously, 9 by 3, so there will be 3 rows and 3 columns.' R automatically infers the number of columns if `nrow` is given.
Q50: A
`cbind` (column bind) stacks vectors side-by-side as columns. `rbind` (row bind) stacks them one above another as rows.
Q51: A
To subset a *specific vector* (`data1$gh`), you apply the logical condition directly to that vector using square brackets. 
Option D would return a data frame with matching rows and all columns.
Q52: C
The lecture explicitly mentions `data1 dollar gh` as how you 'call up' a column by its name, specifically for data frames.
Q53: C
The lecture explicitly states: 'I am creating A 1 to 10, it is particularly a integer vector which starts from 1 goes up to 10.' 
R creates integer sequences if possible.
Q54: B
The lecture states: 'remember before clicking that everything has to be closed here, only this particular file has to be 
open and this has to be saved. After that you go to sessions, set working directory, to source file location'. This implies 
RStudio needs a saved file to know its location.
Q55: B
The lecture clearly states that while a matrix's columns can have names, you 'cannot call a matrix’s column with the 
name of the column' using the `$` operator, unlike data frames.
Q56: B
`rbind` binds by rows. So, two vectors of length 5 would result in two rows (one for each vector) and five columns (for the 
elements of the vectors).
Q57: C
The lecture uses this exact example, `matrix 1 and then 2, 3 and if I run this much it will give me Sachin because 
second row third column is Sachin.` This directly tells you the value at that specific cell.
Q58: B
The lecture points out for the character-coerced matrix: 'Here you will see that all the values are left oriented means left 
all the values is actually align towards the left of the cell, so all the values are characters.' For data frames with mixed 
types, numbers would typically right-align.
Q59: C
Number of observations refers to rows, and variables refer to columns. So, 10 rows * 3 columns = 30 individual data 
points.
Q60: D
The lecture states, 'because character vector is superior than them and every so character cannot be converted to 
number but number can be converted to a character. So they converted everything to a character.'

---
Quiz: Lecture 4 : Introduction to R programming (Contd.)
Q1. Which keyboard shortcut is mentioned in the lecture for cleaning the console in RStudio?
   (A) Control + S
   (B) Control + C
   (C) Control + L
   (D) Control + V
Q2. What is the primary purpose of the `dplyr` library as described in the lecture?
   (A) Creating advanced statistical models
   (B) Generating high-quality data visualizations
   (C) Performing data manipulation and summarization
   (D) Developing web applications within R
Q3. If you are trying to install `dplyr` using `install.packages("dplyr")` and get an error, what is a likely 
first step to troubleshoot according to the lecture?
   (A) Check if your RStudio version is outdated
   (B) Ensure you have a secure internet connection
   (C) Restart your computer
   (D) Contact RStudio support
Q4. The piping sign mentioned in the lecture, `%>%`, is used for what purpose in `dplyr` operations?
   (A) Defining a new variable
   (B) Assigning a value to a variable
   (C) Chaining multiple operations together
   (D) Indicating a comment in the code
Q5. Which of the following R functions, when used with `group_by()`, is intended to add new columns 
to the *original* data frame, typically for each group, without reducing the number of rows?
   (A) `summarize()`
   (B) `select()`
   (C) `filter()`
   (D) `mutate()`
Q6. When using `summarize()` after `group_by()`, what is the typical outcome regarding the structure 
of the data frame?
   (A) A new data frame with the same number of rows as the original
   (B) A new data frame with fewer rows, representing aggregated results per group
   (C) The original data frame with new columns added
   (D) An error, as `summarize()` cannot follow `group_by()`
Q7. Consider the following code snippet from the lecture: `Data$profit = Data$revenue * 
Data$margin / 100`. What does this line of code achieve?
   (A) It renames an existing column to 'profit'.
   (B) It calculates the average profit across all companies.
   (C) It creates a new column named 'profit' in the 'Data' data frame.
   (D) It filters the 'Data' data frame to show only profitable entries.
Q8. What is CRAN in the context of R packages, as mentioned in the lecture?
   (A) A specific type of R function
   (B) The default RStudio console output
   (C) A repository where most common R libraries are saved
   (D) An error message indicating package installation failure
Q9. When writing an `ifelse()` function in R, how many main arguments does it typically take?
   (A) One (the condition)
   (B) Two (condition and value if true)
   (C) Three (condition, value if true, and value if false)
   (D) Four (condition, value if true, value if false, and default value)
Q10. What is crucial to remember about character strings (like 'high' or 'low') when using them as 
outputs in an `ifelse()` function in R?
   (A) They must be numeric.
   (B) They must be written in uppercase.
   (C) They must be enclosed in quotes.
   (D) They are automatically converted to factors.
Q11. You want to calculate the revenue growth rate for each company in your `Data` data frame, year-
over-year. You decide to use a `for` loop. What is an essential step you should perform *before* 
starting the loop for a specific company, like 'png'?
   (A) Save the entire `Data` data frame to a CSV file.
   (B) Create a subset of the `Data` data frame containing only 'png' entries.
   (C) Convert the 'revenue' column to a character type.
   (D) Install the `loopr` package.
Q12. When defining your own function in R, like `normalise = function(x) { ... }`, what does `x` 
represent?
   (A) The name of the function
   (B) A fixed constant value
   (C) An input argument to the function
   (D) The output variable of the function
Q13. What is considered good practice when writing a function with multiple lines of code, especially 
if you want to return a specific result?
   (A) Printing all intermediate variables to the console.
   (B) Enclosing the function code in curly braces `{}`.
   (C) Using the `return()` statement to explicitly specify the output.
   (D) Naming the function with an uppercase letter.
Q14. The normalization formula discussed in the lecture transforms a variable `x` such that its values 
fall between 0 and 1. What is the formula given?
   (A) `(x - mean(x)) / sd(x)`
   (B) `(x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))`
   (C) `(x - max(x)) / (min(x) - max(x))`
   (D) `x / sum(x)`
Q15. The lecture demonstrated creating an initial `Data` data frame. Which type of variable was 
explicitly identified as a 'character variable' among `company`, `fy`, `revenue`, and `margin`?
   (A) `fy`
   (B) `revenue`
   (C) `company`
   (D) `margin`
Q16. What is the key difference between `mutate()` and `summarize()` in `dplyr` when used with 
`group_by()`?
   (A) `mutate()` only works on numeric data, while `summarize()` works on all data types.
   (B) `mutate()` creates new columns for each row within a group, while `summarize()` collapses groups into single rows.
   (C) `summarize()` is used for filtering rows, while `mutate()` is for selecting columns.
   (D) `mutate()` requires no grouping, while `summarize()` always needs `group_by()`.
Q17. Which of the following is NOT a step typically performed at the beginning of an R session for 
cleaning, as described by the lecturer?
   (A) Cleaning the console
   (B) Cleaning the environment
   (C) Removing all other open files except the current script
   (D) Installing all available R packages
Q18. When subsetting a data frame `data` to select only rows where the `company` column is 'png' 
and *all* columns, what R code snippet would be correct, given `data$company == "png"` returns a 
logical vector?
   (A) `data[data$company == "png", ]`
   (B) `data(data$company == "png")`
   (C) `subset(data, company == "png")`
   (D) `data %>% filter(company == "png")`
Q19. In the context of the `for` loop example, if `i` starts at 2 and goes up to 3, what does 
`data_one$gr[i]` refer to when `i` is 2?
   (A) The growth rate for the first entry.
   (B) The growth rate for the second entry.
   (C) The growth rate for the third entry.
   (D) The average growth rate.
Q20. What does `NA` stand for when initializing a new column like `data_one$growth_rate = NA`?
   (A) No Assignment
   (B) Not Applicable
   (C) New Attribute
   (D) Numeric Average
Q21. The lecturer mentions that sometimes one library is 'depended on another library'. What 
happens during installation when such dependencies exist?
   (A) The dependent library must be installed manually first.
   (B) R will prompt the user to choose which dependencies to install.
   (C) All necessary dependent libraries will be automatically downloaded and installed.
   (D) Installation will fail with a dependency error.
Q22. If you want to install multiple R packages at once using `install.packages()`, how should you list 
them?
   (A) In separate `install.packages()` calls.
   (B) As a character vector, e.g., `c("package1", "package2")`.
   (C) Separated by commas within the quotes, e.g., `"package1, package2"`.
   (D) Separated by commas without quotes, e.g., `package1, package2`.
Q23. When the lecturer demonstrates `group_by(fy)` but `fy` is numeric, an error occurs. What 
modification is then made to `fy` to resolve the error and enable correct grouping?
   (A) It is converted to a numeric type.
   (B) It is converted to a logical type.
   (C) It is converted to a factor type using `as.factor()`.
   (D) It is removed from the data frame.

set' (10:17.660-12:38.410), meaning it adds columns to existing rows.

set' (13:21.180-13:26.209), explicitly noting that it reduces the data to group-level summaries.

common libraries are saved' (04:35.890-04:51.260).

text' (21:15.180-21:20.200).

subset of the data which has only png' (23:17.840-23:34.610).

abcd' (32:55.400-33:18.309).
Q14: ` (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))`
The lecture explicitly states the formula: 'x is equal to x minus minimum of x by maximum of x minus minimum of 
x' (31:08.350-31:13.030).
Q15: `company`
The lecturer states, 'the company variable is a character variable, if you see line number 4. I have created company 
variable that has 3 “png”, then 3 “hul” and then 3 “marico”' (01:53.880-02:00.030).
Q16: `mutate()` creates new columns for each row within a group, while `summarize()` collapses groups into 
single rows.
The lecture elaborates on this: `mutate` changes the 'original data set' by adding columns, while `summarize` creates a 
'new data set, which will be a summary version' (11:57.720-13:53.971), implying row reduction for `summarize`.
Q17: Installing all available R packages
The lecturer mentions cleaning the console (Control+L), cleaning the environment (brush icon), and closing other files 
(00:53.960-01:19.229), but not installing all packages.
Q18: `data[data$company == "png", ]`
The lecturer demonstrates this exact syntax: `data[data$company == "png"]` for rows and ', ' after the comma means all 
columns (23:17.840-24:43.500). While `subset()` and `filter()` are also correct ways, the provided snippet directly 
reflects the lecture's example of base R subsetting with a logical vector.
Q19: The growth rate for the second entry.
The lecturer explains the loop: 'when i is equal to 2, 2 minus one by one will get saved in 2' (27:26.470-27:46.590). This 
refers to the index `i` of the `gr` column.
Q20: Not Applicable
While not explicitly defined in the transcript, `NA` in R stands for 'Not Available' or 'Not Applicable', commonly used for 
missing values, which is the default when initializing a column to be filled later (25:00.500-25:19.110). This is a general 
R concept that is relevant in the context of the provided code.
Q21: All necessary dependent libraries will be automatically downloaded and installed.
The lecture states, 'When we install dplyr, generally one library is depended on another library... all those dependencies 
will also will get installed and downloaded' (08:15.210-08:36.399).
Q22: As a character vector, e.g., `c("package1", "package2")`
The lecture says, 'if you want to install multiple this thing, multiple libraries together, you can actually write dplyr comma, 
something else comma something else and so on' (07:28.820-07:33.910). While the example syntax `dplyr comma, 
something else` is slightly ambiguous, the standard R way is a character vector using `c()`. The intent is for multiple 
*string* arguments, which `c()` handles. Given the options, `c("package1", "package2")` is the most correct 
interpretation of 'dplyr comma, something else'.
Q23: It is converted to a factor type using `as.factor()`.
After an error with `group_by(fy)`, the lecturer corrects it to `group by as factor fy` (16:39.250-17:40.450), indicating that 
`fy` needed to be treated as a categorical factor for grouping.
Q24: To compare it with a single equal sign, which is used for assignment.
The lecturer explains, 'if you do not want to assign something but if you want to check of equality then you have to give 
double equal to' (23:34.610-23:47.010), differentiating `==` (equality check) from `=` (assignment).

to all form of time series analysis' (05:03.650-05:17.810).
Q28: R will reinstall the package, potentially updating it.
The lecturer notes, 'I will not press [enter] because for me it is already installed' (07:40.070-07:45.250), implying that 
pressing enter would initiate an installation process regardless, which would effectively re-install or update it if already 
present. This is typical behavior for `install.packages`.
Q29: The number of inputs the function expects.
The lecture explains, 'if y is function of multiple values then I have to write y is function of x comma z let us say. The 
moment I write 2 , I know that there are 2 inputs that I have to give' (32:06.390-32:17.480).
Q30: It computes the cost for each individual entry based on its revenue and profit.
The lecture defines `Data$cost` as `Data$revenue - Data$profit` (15:26.489-15:43.230), which is an element-wise 
calculation resulting in a new column where each row has its own calculated cost.
Q31: To avoid an 'index out of bounds' error when accessing `i-1`.
The growth rate calculation compares `i` with `i-1`. If `i` started at 1, `i-1` would be 0, which is an invalid index for R 
vectors, leading to an error. This is implied by the need for `previous` values in the growth calculation 
(25:59.470-27:20.350).
Q32: Three rows for 'png', each with `AvgMargin = 12`.
`mutate()` adds a column to the original data, so the row count remains the same. When grouped by company, 
`mean(margin)` would calculate the average for 'png' (11+12+13)/3 = 12, and this value would be repeated for all 'png' 
rows in the new `AvgMargin` column (11:57.720-12:21.559).
Q33: The original `Data` data frame with two new columns: `highest_margin` and `lowest_margin`, where values 
are repeated for each company group.
As discussed in the lecture, `mutate` preserves the original number of rows and adds computed columns, repeating 
group-wise summary values for each row within that group (11:57.720-12:38.410, 14:15.459-14:38.160).
Q34: Because there are three companies, and each company has data for these three financial years.
The lecture explains, 'For each company, there are 3 financial year, corresponding revenue and margins are 
given' (02:25.010-02:30.989).
Q35: It refers to the `i`-th row (element) of the `growth_rate` variable.
In R, square brackets `[]` are used for subsetting vectors or data frame columns by position. In a `for` loop where `i` is 
an index iterating over rows, `[i]` refers to the element at that index (27:26.470-27:46.590).
Q36: "low"
The `ifelse` condition was `data$margin > 10`. If true, it assigns 'high'; otherwise, it assigns 'low'. Since 9 is not greater 
than 10, it falls into the 'false' condition (20:00.510-21:15.180).
Q37: It allows the same block of code to be repeated continuously.
The lecture introduces loops as: 'Loop means, that same things get repeated continuously' (22:26.620-22:32.530).
Q38: `library(dplyr)`
The lecture clearly states, 'you have to call the library... I have called the library, library dplyr has been called. That 
means all the functions which are in dplyr can be used right now' (08:56.079-09:14.709).
Q39: A function is designed to take specific inputs and produce outputs, making it reusable.
The lecture defines a function as having 'certain inputs... and there will be certain outputs... and there will be certain 
jobs that is done by this particular function' (29:15.130-29:33.370), highlighting reusability and structured I/O.
Q40: To define the loop as a complete block of code.
The lecture advises, 'I have to select the whole thing... whatever gets collapsed together you have to run the whole thing 
together' (28:10.300-28:33.530). This is because the curly braces `{} `define the scope of the loop; running only part of it 
would lead to incomplete or incorrect execution.
Q41: `Data$norm_profit = normalise(Data$profit)`
The lecturer explicitly demonstrates this: 'if you want to save it in the data set you have to write data dollar normprof that 
is an normalise profit is nothing but this thing so then it gets saved in your data set' (34:25.929-34:33.770).
Q42: A data frame with three rows (one for each `fy`) and a `lowest_cost` column.

(17:58.790-19:00.010), meaning one row per financial year.
Q43: They are numeric variables of size 9.
The lecturer mentions creating 'certain revenue values, certain margin values; each of them has a size of 
9' (02:05.810-02:17.670). Implied numeric from calculations.
Q44: `data_one$revenue[i-1]`
The growth rate formula `(current - previous) / previous` uses `data_one$revenue[i-1]` for the previous value 
(26:37.160-27:16.580).
Q45: The loop might not be correctly defined or executed, leading to errors or unexpected results.
The lecturer's instruction to 'select the whole thing' because 'whatever gets collapsed together you have to run the 
whole thing together' implies that partial execution can lead to issues since R processes statements sequentially 
(28:10.300-28:33.530).
Q46: R will produce an error message indicating the package is not found.

has not been installed' (06:35.390-06:41.530).
Q47: To combine several vectors or lists into a single tabular data structure.

defining several vectors (company, fy, revenue, margin) (02:17.670-02:25.010).
Q48: Its values represent distinct groups or categories.
The lecturer states, 'group by company. So within data there is a company, i would say, column, so for, which is the 
categorical variable. So, whatever you write here has to be categorical' (09:49.249-09:56.160).
Q49: The `dplyr` package must be loaded.
Both `mutate()` and `summarize()` are `dplyr` functions, and the lecture emphasizes that `library(dplyr)` must be called 
before its functions can be used (08:56.079-09:14.709).
Q50: To explain that R packages are often composed of many smaller, dependent packages.
The lecture explicitly explains this: 'When we install dplyr, generally one library is depended on another library... all those 
dependencies will also will get installed and downloaded' (08:15.210-08:36.399).
Q51: To provide a conditional statement that evaluates to TRUE or FALSE.
The lecturer explains the Excel `IF` function's 'check, what you want to check, the logic, if the logic is true' and then 
applies this to R's `ifelse` (19:19.050-21:38.470).
Q52: It promotes code reusability and reduces redundancy.
The core idea of functions is reusability for 'certain jobs' (29:15.130-29:33.370) and applying it to 'any column as its 
input' (30:09.350-30:17.340), which directly implies reusability and avoiding repetitive code.
Q53: Multiplication
In R, multiplication and division have equal precedence and are evaluated from left to right. So `Data$revenue * 
Data$margin` is performed first, then `/ 100` (32:55.400-33:18.309 is the example; relevant for `Data$profit` calculation 
at 03:14.049).
Q54: `company`
The lecture states, 'the company variable is a character variable... I have created company variable that has 3 “png”, 
then 3 “hul” and then 3 “marico”' (01:53.880-02:00.030).
Q55: "high"
The `ifelse` condition checks if `data$margin` is `higher than 10`. Since 15 > 10, the 'true' value ('high') is assigned 
(20:00.510-21:15.180).
Q56: `data$company == "png"`
The lecturer explicitly uses `data$company double is equal to png` for checking equality (23:17.840-23:47.010).
Q57: It enables more readable and flowing code by passing results between functions.
The lecturer introduces the piping sign as a way to chain operations like 'data and then group by 
company' (09:42.930-09:49.249), which is the essence of creating a functional pipeline for data transformation.
Q58: To show that a function can take multiple input arguments.
The lecturer gives the example `y is function of x comma z` to show how a function can handle 'multiple values' as 
inputs (32:06.390-32:11.980).
Q59: To clean and prepare data for analysis by restructuring, transforming, and aggregating.
The lecturer introduces `dplyr` as a tool to 'play with the data set' by finding highest/lowest margins, company-wise 
averages, and 'break that data set' (04:00.059-04:28.620), all indicating data manipulation and preparation.
Q60: It calculates the aggregation for each group and adds that group's aggregate value to every row within 
that group.
The lecture explains that `mutate` with `group_by` will find the `max` for each company (group) and then write that 
`max` value for all rows belonging to that company (11:57.720-12:21.559).
Q61: `&&` and `||`
The lecturer states, 'You can join them through and, and or and etc.' (22:09.640-22:17.280). In R, the logical AND 
operator is `&&` and the logical OR operator is `||` (for scalar conditions, `&` and `|` for vector conditions). This is 
general R knowledge applied in the context of `ifelse`.
Q62: They are loaded into the current R session and ready for use.
The lecture states, 'library dplyr has been called. That means all the functions which are in dplyr can be used right now, 
they are in a usable form' (09:02.130-09:14.709).
Q63: Calculating company-wise highest and lowest margins.
The lecturer frames the problem: 'how can I find out, for every... company, what is the highest margin and what is the 
lowest margin out of these 3 things' and then introduces `dplyr` (04:00.059-04:10.749).
Q64: A new variable of type 'function' is saved in the environment.
After running the function definition code, the lecturer says, 'you will see here there is function called normalise get 
saved, now this is a function' (33:32.890-33:43.170), indicating it's stored in the environment.

Answer Key
Q1: Clean the console and global environment, then set the working directory
The professor explicitly states, 'clean your global environment, clean your console, set your working directory to source 
file location again' to ensure everyone is on the same page.
Q2: dplyr
The professor mentions, 'And when summarize the data, we use dplyr library. So, if you do not have that, you have to 
install that.'
Q3: Overall rating, mean of location, service, and other 6 aspects
The professor explains, 'And that summary had the overall rating and that summary also had the mean of the location, 
service and various other aspects, 6 other aspects.'
Q4: To visualize and compare the performance of hotels across different attributes in a measurable way
The professor states, 'Why I have been trying to create this? Because this gives a fairly measurable idea that, ok. 
Overall rating wise hotel 1 is worse than hotel 2. And that is majorly contributed by A1, though A2 wise hotel 1 is much 
better than hotel 2.'
Q5: barplot()
The professor explicitly states, 'So to that the code is bar plot. The function is bar plot.'
Q6: A matrix
The professor explains, 'So, bar plot will actually use a matrix in it. So, what I am trying to plot here... the input has to be 
either a vector or a matrix. So here, because we are creating a bar plot of multiple parameters... the input will be a 
matrix.'
Q7: names.arg
The professor clarifies, 'So, that is the names argument, names dot arg, names argument is what? The column names 
of Summary 2.'
Q8: It plots the bars for each hotel side-by-side for each attribute.
The professor contrasts `beside = TRUE` (side-by-side) with `beside = FALSE` (stacked view) when discussing how to 
represent the performance of two hotels.
Q9: legend(x=2, y=2, legend = summary2[,1], col=c(5,6))
The professor explicitly shows and explains: 'So, legend is x is equal to 2, y is equal to 2 is actually the location of the 
legend... And what is the legend? Legend is equal to summary 2, comma 1. So, summary 2 comma 1... This is nothing 
but see two levels. One is panthnivas rourkela and other is radhika regency rourkela. So, these two names gets printed 
and the colour is the same colour that you have chosen 5 comma 6.'
Q10: Cleanliness and Service (SRV)
The professor notes, 'One of the major thing which is not same is cleanliness. So, cleanliness is much lower for the blue 
guy and probably the SRV is also much lower. The service is also lower than.'
Q11: Missing value imputation
The professor states, 'The first thing before you run a regression is that we have missing values so missing value 
imputation is my first step.'
Q12: The observations were biased towards higher ratings (not symmetric), so median better preserves the 
distribution.
The professor explains, 'All our observation is a little bit of... bias towards the higher side. That means more numbers of 
4’s and 5’s are there rather than 2’s and 3’s and 1’s... So, it is not symmetric. And if it is not symmetric probably median 
is a better choice than mean to when I am doing a imputation because that will not change the distribution of the data.'
Q13: Three (condition, what to do if true, what to do if false)
The professor explains, 'If else means if something happens, you do something. If that does not happen, you do 
something other. So, there will be if else if you remember there are 3 things in ifelse, in ifelse there were 3 things. 
Condition, if the condition is true, what happens? If the condition is false what happens?'
Q14: To identify if a value is 'Not Applicable' (missing)
The professor describes `is.na(data$i)` as checking 'whether it is na like it is asking if it is yes it is na. Then it will come 
as true, if it is not na not missing value then it will come as false.'
Q15: By running the `summary()` function on the data
The professor states, 'Now, if you do a summary of the data... you will see there is no nothing. No na is in the last 6... 
but right now I have removed them. So, here there is no na coming.'
Q16: Outlier removal
The professor states, 'The second one is missing value imputation after that we have to also find out outliers because 
this is a linear regression.'
Q17: A value that is extremely different from the mean and lies beyond 3 standard deviations from the mean 
(i.e., |Z-score| > 3).
The professor explains, 'Anything which is, which is beyond let us say x is within m plus 3s and m minus 3s. This is not 
an outlier... That means the mod of z has to be lower than 3, when it is higher than 3 it is outlier.'
Q18: scale()
The professor explains, 'it takes data’s second column and scale it, scale it means it changes it to its z values.'
Q19: abs()
The professor demonstrates this by saying, 'Now, I want the absolute value mod of z. So, abs is the function.'
Q20: A logical vector (TRUE/FALSE) indicating which rows to keep
The professor describes `data = data[a,]` where `a` is generated from checking `abs(scale(data$i)) < 3`. He says, 'I 
want to keep the false ones... So, this is my a, for all the trues means these are not outliers. So, if there is some by 
chance some falses in between then that will be an outlier and take that subset of the data in such a way that I am 
taking only the trues and not the false.'
Q21: Checking for correlation among x variables
The professor states, 'And step number 3 is basically we have to see correlation. So, at least the x variables cannot be 
correlated with each other to run a regression.'
Q22: cor()
The professor says, 'correlation of what data is my data set. That data sets 6 to 11th column. So, if I run this that gets 
saved in something called correl.'
Q23: 0.6
After reviewing the correlation matrix, the professor comments, 'And if you carefully see all the values in the matrix are 
lower than 0.6 I think so. Let us check, yes, all the values are lower than 0.6 it is coming lower than 0.6. So, there is no 
very very hard and fast I would say multi collinearity issue or correlational problem right now, as of now.'
Q24: Histogram
The professor states, 'what we have to do is I have to find out the histogram of everything from the x variable which is 2 
and then 6 to 11 all the variables is histogram.'
Q25: Shapiro test
The professor mentions, 'And there is something called Shapiro test which is just go and check it is a goodness of fit 
test for normality.'
Q26: None of the variables were normal, as they were categorical or ordinal.
The professor states, 'So, the first one is overall rating. This is not normal. It cannot be normal because it is a 
categorical variable actually 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Again this is not normal. This is not normal. And so on... all the w values 
coming lower than 1. So, they are none of them thing is normal.'
Q27: The primary goal was to get a 'vague level idea' of variable importance, not strict econometric correctness.
The professor explains, 'Now, often times we actually ignore these normality. In often times because the, our major goal 
is not to focus on the rightness of econometrics techniques, our, sometimes our goal is to get an overall vague level 
idea that which one is more important, which one is less important.'
Q28: lm()
The professor states, 'Which is fit is equal to... fit is equal to I have written in lm. lm stands for, lm stand for linear model.'
Q29: y ~ x1 + x2 + ...
The professor shows the syntax as: 'My y variable which is review overall rating and then tilde and then x1 plus x2 plus 
x3 up to services, so all the aspects I just write it down.'
Q30: The model is highly significant, indicating that at least one independent variable explains the dependent 
variable.
The professor explains, 'the p value is coming 2.2 into this e minus 16 means 10 to the power minus 16... That means 
very small, that means the F statistic is significant. So, I can believe on this model.'
Q31: 0.5651
The professor states, 'Then the adjusted R square is 0.5651 it is good enough at least in the context of marketing.'
Q32: By examining its p-value; if it's higher than 0.05, it's not significant.
The professor illustrates this with the intercept: 'the intercept is also coming not significant that means see the how to 
know not significant the p value is higher than 0.05.'
Q33: Service
The professor concludes, 'if you do the regression I can see from the data set that service is the most important part. 
So, 0.24 is service.'
Q34: Its coefficient was the smallest among the significant factors.
While not explicitly stating 'smallest coefficient', the context is about comparing coefficients. The professor says, '0.24 is 
service and 0.11 is cleanliness and 0.20 is rooms and 0.12 is sleep quality... And location is the least important factor in 
this particular dataset that I have created, location is the least important factor.' The coefficients themselves indicate 
relative importance.
Q35: Converting them into factors
The professor states, 'What we have done is something called ordered logic. My y variable is 1, 2, 3, 4, 5... so ordered 
logistic regression is another way of dealing with this. So, what we did is we have actually converted all our x variable 
and y variable to their factor form.'
Q36: mass
The professor mentions, 'Now, there are library called mass, in which there is a polr function, which is actually a 
function which has been used for multinomial, actually ordinal logit.'
Q37: The formula structure (`y ~ x1 + x2 + ...`) remains the same, only the function name changes.
The professor states, 'Everything remains same instead LM we write polr. That is all.'
Q38: The t-value; if it's higher than 2, it's considered significant.
The professor states, 'here the p values are not coming, but does not matter if the t is higher than 2, I can say that it is 
significant.'
Q39: It is the baseline or reference category against which other levels are compared.
The professor explains, 'So, the first thing is see the rating value 1 got dropped, this categorical variable, it is dummy 
variable. So, one of the dummy variables will get dropped and the other will remain.' This is standard practice for dummy 
coding.
Q40: Overall rating increases by 1.9 (logistically).
The professor states, 'in comparison to rating value 1 that means value for money who ever giving is 1. If you by chance 
improve your value for money from 1 to 2. The overall thing increases by 1.9.' He also clarifies 'It is not a linear increase. 
It is a logistic regression. So, logistic increase.'
Q41: Location
The professor notes regarding location, 'you see that jump is not so much 0.73, 0.9 in comparison to 5, 4, etcetera. So, 
then this jump is not so much. So, I can say that location has less impact.'
Q42: It showed largely insignificant and sometimes counter-intuitive results, suggesting 'no meaning' in this 
model.
The professor states, 'something is very interesting that if you see that if you increase cleanliness from 2 to 3 and 2 to 4. 
It is showing that the, your overall rating will drop. Actually, your overall rating does not drop because your t values are 
very low that so this results the first two results are not meaningful... So cleanliness has no meaning as per this 
particular result. It has no meaning.'
Q43: It provides more detailed insights into how specific rating changes (e.g., from 2 to 3) within an aspect 
impact the overall rating.
The professor concludes the ordinal logit discussion by saying, 'So this guy gives you an idea that exactly if I jump from 
2 to 4 or 2 to 3 or 2 to 5 in terms of some improvement in the aspect ratings, how that will impact the overall rating.' This 
granular insight is unique to the ordinal model for categorical variables.
Q44: Service, due to its highest coefficient (0.24).
While cleanliness was lower in the bar plot, the regression identified Service (coefficient 0.24) as the most important 
factor, implying that improvements here would have the largest positive impact on overall rating.
Q45: Basic statistics for management, marketing management, and introduction to business analytics/machine 
learning concepts
The professor explicitly advises, 'if you have studied statistics for management, the basic statistics, you should go and 
revise that... If you have studied marketing management... And if you have studied, let us say, introduction to business 
analytics, some of the concepts of regression, if a little bit of machine learning techniques.'
Q46: To get students 'handy with the coding' and familiar with R.
The professor states, 'Till now I just wanted you to do show the coding, not linear regression, not ordered logit. We will 
actually focus on each of them specifically at a later context. Here, I wanted you to actually get a little bit handy with the 
coding and that was our purpose.'
Q47: The course will start solving marketing problems using the tools learned.
The professor states, 'from the next session, which will be week 2, we will actually focus on all of this stuff to solve 
marketing problems.'
Q48: help(function_name) or ?function_name
The professor explains, 'how to find out the help of bar plot? You have to write question mark. And then bar plot and 
press on enter or you can write help within bracket bar plot.'
Q49: The names of the hotels being compared
The professor clarifies, '2 to 8. That means leave aside the first column, which is the hotel name.' He further uses 
`summary2[,1]` for the legend which gives the hotel names.
Q50: To avoid losing work if R crashes or freezes.
The professor says, 'So, this is a very common thing that happens in R that sometimes it hangs so it is. That is why it is 
a very good practice that you keep on saving whatever files that you are creating so that you do not lose stuff.'
Q51: The specific column index (6, then 7, then 8, etc.).
The professor explains, 'So for i is equal to 6 to 11... what happens is this i keeps on changing, i takes the value of 6 
and then 7, then 8.' This implies `i` is the column index.
Q52: median(data[,6], na.rm = TRUE)
Although `na.rm=TRUE` was implicitly handled by the `ifelse` statement, the `median()` function itself requires `na.rm = 
TRUE` to correctly calculate the median when NAs are present. The professor demonstrates `median(data, na.rm=T)[i]` 
in the loop context, implying `na.rm=TRUE` for the median calculation.
Q53: The model suggests that a small improvement in cleanliness at this level does not measurably change the 
overall rating.
The professor explicitly states regarding cleanliness, 'your t values are very low that so this results the first two results 
are not meaningful.' This means that such a small change has no statistically discernible impact.
Q54: The lecture's focus was on introductory R coding for common steps, not deep econometric theory.
The professor explicitly states, 'But today’s class is only about how to do it in Excel, sorry in R in a easy format, so I will 
not focus on that.' referring to these advanced checks.
Q55: To ensure all x-variables are on the same scale, making their coefficients directly comparable in terms of 
impact.
The professor suggests this: 'another way of comparing is before you do this regression you change all these 6 values 
to their scale form. So, you change to their standardized form, then you do the regression. So, that might be a better 
step.' This is done to make coefficients directly comparable.
Q56: It allows for repetitive operations to be applied efficiently to multiple data columns or variables.
The `for` loop structure (`for (i in 6:11)`) is designed to iterate through columns and apply the same logic (imputation, 
outlier check) to each, automating a repetitive task.
Q57: Service is twice as important as Cleanliness.
The professor states, '0.24 is service and 0.11 is cleanliness... but service is twice as important as cleanliness.' This is a 
direct interpretation of the relative magnitude of the coefficients.
Q58: An ordered categorical or ordinal variable.
The professor uses 'overall rating' which is 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and states, 'My y variable is 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. So, they are ordered, so 
ordered logistic regression is another way of dealing with this.'
Q59: A non-linear, possibly increasing, logistic impact.
The professor explains, 'It is not a linear increase. It is a logistic regression. So, logistic increase.' The values (1.9 then 
3.4) show an increasing but non-linear progression.
Q60: 0.24 (0.12 * 2-point increase)
In a linear model, a coefficient of 0.12 means for every 1-unit increase in 'Sleep Quality' (assuming it's treated as a 
continuous numeric variable), the 'Overall Rating' increases by 0.12. A 2-point increase (from 2 to 4) would then be 0.12 
* 2 = 0.24.
Q61: To clear previous commands and output from the console window.
Cleaning the console simply clears the textual output and command history in the console window, making it easier to 
view new outputs.
Q62: High correlation makes it difficult to ascertain the unique contribution of each independent variable.
The professor mentions checking correlation to avoid 'multi collinearity issue or correlational problem'. Multicollinearity 
(high correlation between independent variables) makes it hard to distinguish the individual effects of the predictors on 
the dependent variable, leading to unstable coefficient estimates and difficulties in interpretation.
Q63: RStudio 'hanged' or froze.
The professor states, 'So, if I run this also the legend also the legends gets comes here. So, this is a very common thing 
that happens in R that sometimes it hangs so it is. That is why it is a very good practice that you keep on saving 
whatever files that you are creating.'
Q64: Mean, median, min, max, 1st and 3rd quartiles, and NA count.
The professor explains `summary()` 'shows the basic I would say descriptive statistics of all the variables like what is the 
mean, what is the median, what is the first quantile, what is the third quantile, etcetera, etcetera.' It also shows NA 
counts for missing values.
