===== WEEK 7 =====
Quiz: Lecture 34 : Recommendation Engine and Retail Analytics
Q1. According to the lecture, what is the primary reason for the significant growth of e-commerce in 
recent years?
   (A) Decreased number of physical retail stores.
   (B) Increased cost of traditional advertising.
   (C) Lower cost and wider availability of internet access.
   (D) Shift in consumer preference towards luxury goods.
Q2. What does the term 'sell deep' refer to in the context of recommendation engines?
   (A) Focusing on high-value, exclusive products.
   (B) Maximizing profit margins on core products.
   (C) Monetizing the 'long tail' with surprising recommendations for incremental sales.
   (D) Engaging in aggressive sales tactics for immediate purchases.
Q3. The lecture describes an 'aaha' feeling associated with recommendations. What does this 'aaha' 
feeling signify?
   (A) A sense of relief after a difficult purchase.
   (B) A moment of surprise or serendipitous discovery.
   (C) Frustration due to irrelevant suggestions.
   (D) Satisfaction from finding a discounted item.
Q4. When a customer buys a mobile phone and is then recommended a protective cover, what type 
of selling is being utilized?
   (A) Upselling.
   (B) Downselling.
   (C) Cross-selling.
   (D) Bundling.
Q5. The lecture states, 'We are leaving the age of information and entering the age of 
recommendations.' What does this statement imply about consumer behavior?
   (A) Consumers no longer seek out information on their own.
   (B) Consumers are overwhelmed by information and increasingly rely on suggestions.
   (C) Information is becoming less important than product quality.
   (D) Recommendations are replacing traditional marketing channels.
Q6. Which of the following is NOT an example of a type of recommendation discussed in the lecture?
   (A) Frequently bought together.
   (B) Customers who bought this item also bought these items.
   (C) Customers who returned this item also bought these items.
   (D) What other items do customers buy after viewing this item?
Q7. For the recommendation type 'Frequently bought together,' what is the primary data source 
required?
   (A) Customer viewing data and buying data matched over time.
   (B) Customer basket data where items are purchased in the same transaction.
   (C) Customer ratings and reviews for individual products.
   (D) Customer demographics and psychographics.
Q8. To implement 'What other items do customers buy after viewing this item?' recommendations, 
what additional data source is crucial compared to 'Customers who bought this item also bought 
these items'?
   (A) Customer demographic data.
   (B) Customer feedback on product quality.
   (C) Customer viewing data.
   (D) Customer support interaction logs.
Q9. A good recommender should aim to recommend programming titles to a software engineer and 
baby toys to a new mother. This illustrates the importance of what principle in recommendation 
systems?
   (A) Diversity.
   (B) Novelty.
   (C) Fit.
   (D) Popularity.
Q10. Why should a recommendation engine avoid recommending items a user would 'anyway buy'?
   (A) It can confuse the customer.
   (B) It leads to reduced customer satisfaction.
   (C) It incurs unnecessary calculation costs without generating incremental sales.
   (D) It violates data privacy regulations.
Q11. Which of the following is a key challenge for recommendation engines when dealing with a 
newly launched product that has no existing ratings or purchase history?
   (A) Sparsity problem.
   (B) Cold start problem (for users).
   (C) First rater problem.
   (D) Popularity bias.
Q12. What is the 'cold start problem' in recommendation engines, as described in the lecture?
   (A) Difficulty in recommending items during off-peak seasons.
   (B) The challenge of recommending products to new users with limited historical data.
   (C) The system crashing due to sudden surges in user activity.
   (D) The inability to process recommendations in real-time.
Q13. How does the lecture describe customer data in terms of its stability for recommendation 
purposes?
   (A) Static and unchanging.
   (B) Volatile and subject to change over time due to life events.
   (C) Consistently growing in volume.
   (D) Only relevant for a short period after initial purchase.
Q14. Which of the following describes the 'sparsity problem' in collaborative filtering?
   (A) The recommendation engine runs out of server space.
   (B) Users provide insufficient feedback for quality products.
   (C) Many items remain unrated or unpurchased by most users, making it hard to find common users or items.
   (D) The algorithm is too complex to run efficiently.
Q15. What is the primary objective of 'personalization' in the context of retail analytics, as explained 
in the lecture?
   (A) To optimize supply chain logistics.
   (B) To adapt to the individual needs, interests, and preferences of each user.
   (C) To standardize product offerings across all customers.
   (D) To reduce customer service interactions.
Q16. Collaborative filtering primarily relies on which of the following to make recommendations?
   (A) The demographic profile of the user.
   (B) Maintaining a dataset of many users' ratings or purchase decisions for a variety of items.
   (C) The content features of the items themselves.
   (D) Expert opinions on product quality.
Q17. In the Shahrukh Khan movie example for collaborative filtering, if two friends are similar based 
on past movie tastes, and one has seen 'Main Hoon Na' but the other hasn't, why would the system 
recommend it?
   (A) Because 'Main Hoon Na' is a highly-rated movie by critics.
   (B) Because the two friends share similar tastes and one has rated it highly, suggesting the other might also like it.
   (C) Because it's a popular movie and needs more viewership.
   (D) Because the unseen movie has content similar to movies both have seen.
Q18. Amazon's item-to-item collaborative filtering focuses on finding similarities between what?
   (A) Customers.
   (B) Items.
   (C) Retail locations.
   (D) Purchase times.
Q19. When measuring similarity between users for recommending food items, which attribute might 
be given more weightage, according to the lecture's example?
   (A) Gender.
   (B) Age.
   (C) Native region/ethnicity.
   (D) Profession.
Q20. Which of the following similarity measures is explicitly mentioned in the lecture as being used 
in recommendation systems?
   (A) Euclidean distance.
   (B) Manhattan distance.
   (C) Pearson correlation.
   (D) Jaccard similarity.
Q21. What problem that collaborative filtering faces is specifically addressed by Content-Based 
Recommendation?
   (A) The popularity bias.
   (B) The cold start problem for new users.
   (C) The first rater problem for new items.
   (D) The real-time calculation challenge.
Q22. Content-Based Recommendation makes suggestions based on what type of information?
   (A) The ratings provided by other users.
   (B) The purchasing patterns of similar customers.
   (C) Information about the content and features of the items themselves.
   (D) The geographical location of the user.
Q23. Which of these is an advantage of Content-Based Recommendation over Collaborative Filtering?
   (A) It requires a large dataset of other users' ratings.
   (B) It can easily recommend items even if they are new and unpopular.
   (C) It primarily relies on the quality judgments of other users.
   (D) It avoids the need for defining item features.
Q24. A disadvantage of Content-Based Recommendation is its reliance on 'content that can be 
encoded as a meaningful feature.' What does this imply?
   (A) It is easier to implement for all product types.
   (B) It works best for subjective experiences like art.
   (C) Not all product attributes can be easily converted into quantitative, machine-readable data.
   (D) It only works for products with text descriptions.
Q25. Why is the ability to provide 'explanations of recommended items' considered an advantage for 
Content-Based Recommendation?
   (A) It helps users understand why a product is popular.
   (B) It allows tracking which specific features of an item contributed to its recommendation.
   (C) It informs users about the manufacturing process.
   (D) It reduces the need for customer reviews.
Q26. In the context of the lecture, what is the main difference in data utilization between a 'frequently 
bought together' recommendation and a 'customers who bought this also bought' recommendation?
   (A) The former uses demographic data, the latter uses psychographic data.
   (B) The former requires items in the same basket; the latter can use items bought in different baskets over a time 
period.
   (C) The former considers product ratings; the latter considers product views.
   (D) The former is for physical stores; the latter is for e-commerce.
Q27. The lecture suggests that for recommendation engines, the cost of calculation for one lakh 
products for one lakh customers can be problematic. What does 'problematic' primarily refer to in 
this context?
   (A) Difficulty in coding the algorithm.
   (B) Ethical concerns regarding data usage.
   (C) High computational time and resource costs.
   (D) Lack of skilled analysts to interpret results.
Q28. According to the lecture, what defines the 'long tail' in the context of retail?
   (A) A small number of popular products that generate most of the sales.
   (B) The vast number of niche products that individually sell little but collectively contribute significantly to revenue.
   (C) The extended delivery time for specialized items.
   (D) The duration a product remains relevant in the market.
Q29. What is the primary characteristic of 'unorganized retail' in India, as mentioned in the lecture?
   (A) It is primarily government-owned.
   (B) It represents a very small portion of the total retail market.
   (C) It lacks formal structure, often consisting of small, independent businesses.
   (D) It focuses exclusively on online sales.
Q30. Why is it important for recommendation results to be available in 'real-time'?
   (A) To allow for complex, offline analysis.
   (B) Because customers expect immediate suggestions while browsing or purchasing.
   (C) To reduce the overall server load.
   (D) To ensure data privacy and security.
Q31. In the context of recommendation engines, what does it mean to 'expand user's taste without 
offending or annoying him/her'?
   (A) To recommend items completely unrelated to their current preferences.
   (B) To subtly introduce new categories or items that are reasonably far from their usual choices but still relevant.
   (C) To exclusively recommend popular, mainstream items.
   (D) To suggest items from competitors' websites.
Q32. Which statement best describes the 'popularity bias' problem in recommendation engines?
   (A) The system tends to recommend only highly-rated items.
   (B) The system struggles to recommend niche products to users with unique tastes.
   (C) The system favors products from well-known brands.
   (D) The system recommends items based on seasonal trends.
Q33. What is the relationship between 'personalization' and 'recommendation engines' as explained 
in the lecture?
   (A) Personalization is a subset of recommendation engines.
   (B) Recommendation engines are a type of personalization software.
   (C) They are unrelated concepts.
   (D) Personalization only applies to physical retail, while recommendation engines are for e-commerce.
Q34. In the general workflow of a recommendation engine using collaborative filtering, after finding 
similar users, what is the next step?
   (A) Suggest items that have never been rated by anyone.
   (B) Recommend items rated highly by these similar users but not yet rated by the current user.
   (C) Provide advertisements for products from competitors.
   (D) Offer discounts on items the user has already purchased.
Q35. When comparing two users for similarity based on their purchase behavior, why might not all 
purchase behaviors be 'similarly treated'?
   (A) To avoid overwhelming the system with data.
   (B) Because certain product categories or purchase frequencies might be more indicative of taste than others.
   (C) To prioritize recent purchases over older ones.
   (D) Because different users have different budgets.
Q36. What is a potential disadvantage of Content-Based Recommendation related to 'quality 
judgments of other users'?
   (A) It overemphasizes the opinions of popular influencers.
   (B) It cannot incorporate or leverage user ratings or reviews from others.
   (C) It requires users to provide their own quality judgments.
   (D) It is susceptible to fake reviews.
Q37. The lecture mentions 'aggregator firms' that connect customer requirements and service 
providers. Which of the following is an example provided?
   (A) Flipkart.
   (B) Amazon.
   (C) Alibaba (for B2B).
   (D) Vodafone.
Q38. What did the lecturer say about the availability of internet in the past (around 10 years back) 
compared to the present?
   (A) It was more widely available but much slower.
   (B) It was more expensive and less available, especially on mobile phones.
   (C) It was primarily used for academic purposes.
   (D) The quality of connection was better due to fewer users.
Q39. When should an e-commerce firm ideally present 'Customers who bought this item also bought' 
recommendations in a customer's journey?
   (A) During the initial information search phase.
   (B) While the customer is adding items to their cart.
   (C) On the post-purchase page (e.g., after payment successful) or when they open Amazon again after a recent 
purchase.
   (D) Only for high-value items.
Q40. A key aspect of a good recommender is to avoid recommending items a user 'would never buy.' 
This directly relates to which core principle?
   (A) Novelty.
   (B) Diversity.
   (C) Fit.
   (D) Timeliness.
Q41. What is the primary challenge related to 'old customers' in recommendation systems, as 
mentioned in the lecture?
   (A) They are less likely to buy new products.
   (B) They generate too much information, making it difficult to decide which data to use.
   (C) They require more personalized marketing campaigns.
   (D) They often switch to competitor platforms.
Q42. The lecture categorizes recommendation engines as being under what broader domain of 
marketing?
   (A) Supply Chain Management (SCM).
   (B) Customer Relationship Management (CRM).

Q43. Which of the following is NOT a problem associated with Collaborative Filtering, as identified in 
the lecture?
   (A) Cold start.
   (B) Sparsity.
   (C) First rater.
   (D) Over-personalization.
Q44. In Content-Based Recommendation for movies, what are examples of 'content' features

   (B) Genre, time period, length, number of heroes/heroines, director.
   (C) Movie budget and box office collection.
   (D) Audience demographics.
Q45. What is an advantage of Content-Based Recommendation concerning 'data of other users'?
   (A) It requires extensive data from other users to establish similarity.
   (B) It relies heavily on the aggregated opinions of a large user base.

   (D) It needs data from other users only for initial setup.
Q46. The lecture mentions placing Scotch Brite or dishwashing powder near crockeries in a physical

   (D) Inventory management.
Q47. According to the lecture, what is the 'standard approach' when selecting correlated users for 
predictions in collaborative filtering?

   (B) To use a randomly selected subset of users.
   (C) To use the most similar 'n' users.
   (D) To only include users with perfect correlation.
Q48. What is meant by 'customer search items' versus 'recommendation' in the context of retail?
   (A) Customer search items are physical; recommendations are digital.
   (B) Customer search items imply active user seeking; recommendations imply passive suggestions to users.
   (C) Customer search items are free; recommendations are paid.
   (D) Customer search items are for new users; recommendations are for old users.
Q49. If a male customer from Bengal is looking for apparel, and a male customer from Odisha is also 
looking for apparel, the lecture suggests that which attribute would be more important for similarity 
in this scenario?
   (A) Their native region (Bengali vs. Odia).
   (B) Their gender (male).
   (C) Their age.
   (D) Their income level.
Q50. The lecture states that the growth rate of e-commerce is 'pretty high' even though its size might 
not be significant compared to the total retail industry. What is the implication of this observation for

   (B) Businesses should focus solely on unorganized retail.
   (C) E-commerce, despite its current size, represents a major future opportunity and area for investment.
   (D) Organized retail will soon disappear.
Q51. What kind of data would be sufficient for a 'Frequently bought together' recommendation using

   (A) User demographics and item content.
   (B) Items purchased concurrently in a single transaction (basket data).
   (C) User ratings on individual items over time.
   (D) Item browsing history.
Q52. If a recommendation engine suggests a new, niche product to a user who has very specific and 
unique tastes, and the recommendation is successful, which type of recommendation system is 
more likely at play?
   (A) Collaborative Filtering.
   (B) Content-Based Recommendation.
   (C) Rule-Based Recommendation.
   (D) Random Recommendation.
Q53. The formula for rating prediction in collaborative filtering includes a component where a user's 
average rating is used (ra). What does this 'ra' represent?
   (A) The highest rating ever given by user 'a'.
   (B) The average rating given by user 'a' to all items.
   (C) The rating given by user 'a' to a specific item being predicted.
   (D) The overall average rating of all items in the system.
Q54. Which of the following describes a key role of Marketing Analytics in the context of retail, as 
introduced in the lecture?
   (A) To eliminate the need for traditional marketing.
   (B) To exclusively focus on improving product manufacturing processes.
   (C) To help retail firms, especially e-commerce, address challenges and leverage opportunities.
   (D) To replace human decision-making with automated systems entirely.
Q55. Why is it inefficient for a recommendation engine to calculate recommendations for products a 
user will 'anyway buy'?
   (A) It makes the user experience less engaging.
   (B) It consumes valuable computing resources (time and money) without adding marginal benefit/incremental sales.
   (C) It can lead to customer dissatisfaction if the predicted item is out of stock.
   (D) It biases the system towards popular items.
Q56. The lecture mentions that 'Internet is becoming cheap' and 'Jio is giving this much offer.' What 
impact does this trend have on the retail industry?
   (A) It reduces the need for physical retail stores.
   (B) It primarily benefits multinational e-commerce giants.
   (C) It fuels the growth of e-commerce by making online access ubiquitous and affordable.
   (D) It increases the production costs for internet-enabled devices.
Q57. What is the implied core difference between 'user-based' and 'item-based' collaborative filtering, 
based on Amazon's approach discussed in the lecture?
   (A) User-based focuses on similar customers, while item-based focuses on similar items.
   (B) User-based is for B2C, item-based is for B2B.
   (C) User-based is older technology, item-based is newer.
   (D) User-based relies on explicit ratings, item-based on implicit feedback.
Q58. A woman's 'life period' is highlighted as a very interesting case for marketers in the customer 
segmentation video (referenced in the lecture). Why is this so?
   (A) Women tend to be more influenced by advertising.
   (B) Women's purchasing power increases significantly over time.
   (C) Women's lifestyle and needs change drastically over different life stages (e.g., adolescent, mom).
   (D) Women are generally more loyal to brands than men.
Q59. According to the lecture, what is the impact of recommending a product (e.g., a phone cover) if 
only 10% of customers actually convert on that recommendation?
   (A) It is a significant failure for the recommendation engine.
   (B) It indicates poor targeting by the system.
   (C) Even a small percentage of conversion, when scaled across millions of sales, can generate substantial additional 
revenue.
   (D) It suggests that recommendations should only be made for high-value items.
Q60. When is 'Collaborative filtering' considered the most common approach for recommendation 
engines?
   (A) Only for new users with no history.
   (B) When item content cannot be easily encoded.
   (C) Almost all existing commercial recommenders use this approach.
   (D) When real-time results are not necessary.
Q61. In the context of recommendation engine challenges, what does the 'glut of information' refer to 
regarding old customers?
   (A) Customers deleting their old purchase data.
   (B) The overwhelming volume of historical data available for long-term customers, making it hard to prioritize relevant 
information.
   (C) Old customers being less receptive to new recommendations.
   (D) The difficulty in acquiring new data for established customers.
Answer Key
Q1: Lower cost and wider availability of internet access.
The lecturer explicitly states that 'Internet is becoming cheap' and 'people who are in rural areas also can access 
Internet because availability of internet is also there means the connection is also good,' attributing the rise of e-
commerce to this trend (1:08-1:31).
Q2: Monetizing the 'long tail' with surprising recommendations for incremental sales.
The lecture defines 'sell deep' as 'monetize the long tail with surprising recommendations,' emphasizing the importance 
of incremental sales for products beyond the initially sought items (3:20-3:31).
Q3: A moment of surprise or serendipitous discovery.
The lecturer mentions that recommendations create an 'aaha' feeling, which is described as 'a surprise that comes into 
your mind' (6:58-7:01).
Q4: Cross-selling.
The example of buying a mobile phone and being recommended a cover is explicitly given as a case of 'cross-
selling' (5:07-5:33).
Q5: Consumers are overwhelmed by information and increasingly rely on suggestions.
This statement suggests a shift in how consumers interact with products, moving from active information seeking to 
passively receiving and trusting recommendations to guide their choices (7:06-7:13).
Q6: Customers who returned this item also bought these items.
The lecture explicitly discusses 'frequently bought together,' 'customer who bought this item also bought these items,' 
and 'what other items do customers buy after viewing this item?' as common recommendation types. Returns are not 
mentioned in this context (11:05-12:09).
Q7: Customer basket data where items are purchased in the same transaction.
The lecture clarifies that for 'frequently bought together,' 'both items should be in the same basket' (12:16-12:26) and 
'only customer basket data in the same basket will be matched' (14:36-14:44).
Q8: Customer viewing data.
The lecture states that for 'What other items do customers buy after viewing this item?', 'Customer viewing data should 
also be collected because customers who have seen this then they bought this one.' (14:24-14:32).
Q9: Fit.
The lecturer uses this exact example to illustrate that 'there has to be a fit' between the recommendation and the 
customer's requirements (15:19-16:08).
Q10: It incurs unnecessary calculation costs without generating incremental sales.
The lecture explains that such recommendations are costly in terms of 'time and resources' for calculation, and since 
the customer would buy it anyway, it yields 'no result' or 'no incremental benefit,' leading to a 'loss' (17:33-19:51).
Q11: First rater problem.
The 'first rater' problem specifically refers to the inability to recommend an item that has not been previously rated or 
purchased, which applies to new products (32:03-32:21).
Q12: The challenge of recommending products to new users with limited historical data.
The cold start problem is defined as the situation where 'New customers have limited information' and 'to get that 
information you have to have be old customer' (21:04-21:31) and further described as 'there needs to be enough other 
users already in the system to find a match' (31:40-31:48).
Q13: Volatile and subject to change over time due to life events.
The lecture highlights that 'A customer data is volatile' and 'Customer change is overtime,' giving examples of personal 
life changes like childbirth or marriage impacting preferences (21:42-22:29).
Q14: Many items remain unrated or unpurchased by most users, making it hard to find common users or items.
Sparsity is described as a problem where 'If there are many items to be recommended, even if there are many users, 
the user rating matrix is sparse and it is hard to find users that are rated same items' (31:50-32:00).
Q15: To adapt to the individual needs, interests, and preferences of each user.
The lecture defines personalization as 'adapting the individual needs, interests and preferences of each 
user' (23:52-24:05).
Q16: Maintaining a dataset of many users' ratings or purchase decisions for a variety of items.
The lecture states that 'Collaborative filtering requires maintaining a dataset of many users' ratings of variety items, 
sometimes ratings sometimes purchased decisions' (24:33-24:44).
Q17: Because the two friends share similar tastes and one has rated it highly, suggesting the other might also 
like it.
This example illustrates collaborative filtering, where if two users are similar in their past ratings/purchases (e.g., other 
SRK movies), an item enjoyed by one but not yet experienced by the other would be recommended (25:11-25:54).
Q18: Items.
The lecture specifically mentions, 'the Amazon's item to item collaborative filtering is focusing on finding similar instead 
of similar items instead of similar customers' (23:22-23:28).
Q19: Native region/ethnicity.
The lecturer uses an example comparing gender vs. native region for food, concluding that 'if it is food item, probably 
our food habits match based on the native. So native will get more importance' (28:30-28:48).
Q20: Pearson correlation.
The lecture states, 'One is correlation. This is a very simple formula of correlation. So Pearson correlation you can find 
out.' (29:38-29:46). Cosine is also mentioned but Pearson is explicitly named as a type of correlation used.
Q21: The first rater problem for new items.
The lecturer introduces content-based recommendation by stating, 'To solve the first problem so to solve this problem 
where the first rater, sometimes we do not believe on customer's purchased data or rating data. We believe on the 
content of the product.' (32:35-32:46).
Q22: Information about the content and features of the items themselves.
The lecture defines content-based recommendations as being 'based on information of the content of items rather than 
other's opinions' (33:32-33:38).
Q23: It can easily recommend items even if they are new and unpopular.
One of the listed advantages of content-based recommendation is that it is 'Able to recommend new and unpopular 
items also' because it doesn't need prior ratings (34:21-34:26).
Q24: Not all product attributes can be easily converted into quantitative, machine-readable data.
The lecture explains this disadvantage by stating, 'not all content is can be converted to a so it is a mathematical 
calculation. It has to be quantitative. So not all content can be converted to quantitative values' (34:45-34:55).
Q25: It allows tracking which specific features of an item contributed to its recommendation.
The lecture states this advantage means, 'Which particular feature is actually contributing to this recommendation of 
this product to you can also be tracked' (34:26-34:39).
Q26: The former requires items in the same basket; the latter can use items bought in different baskets over a 
time period.
The lecture differentiates: 'both items should be in the same basket' for the first case, versus 'both items in past history 
might be in different baskets but both items has to be bought' for the second (12:16-12:31).
Q27: High computational time and resource costs.
The lecturer emphasizes that a 'very difficult calculation' for a large number of products and customers leads to 'very 
time taking calculation, heavy job,' and that 'time and resources cost money' (16:50-17:38).
Q28: The vast number of niche products that individually sell little but collectively contribute significantly to 
revenue.
The 'long tail' is described as the area where 'whatever sells happen in this area which is the minute sells or which is 
the incremental sells... you can do incremental sells, incremental sells slowly slowly as much as possible' and 'when you 
do it in a large scale, that contributes to a revenue in a very huge matter' (3:42-4:05).
Q29: It lacks formal structure, often consisting of small, independent businesses.
The lecture states, 'Still in India, I would say that majorly Indian retail is unorganized, probably majorly the whole world's 
retail is unorganized.' This implies a lack of formal structure, though the direct definition isn't given, it's contrasted with 
organized and e-commerce (2:23-2:38).
Q30: Because customers expect immediate suggestions while browsing or purchasing.
The lecturer emphasizes this by saying, 'I will, let us say I am seeing a website in Amazon and you give me 
recommendation probably half an hour later that does not work. You have to give me recommendation then and 
there' (20:44-20:56).
Q31: To subtly introduce new categories or items that are reasonably far from their usual choices but still 
relevant.
The lecture advises to 'expand user's taste without offending or annoying him her' and then elaborates that this means 
'not recommend something which is very far from the fit which has to be reasonably far' (19:56-20:15).
Q32: The system struggles to recommend niche products to users with unique tastes.
Popularity bias is defined as 'cannot recommend items to someone with unique tastes' or 'certain niche products you 
cannot recommend' (32:25-32:33).
Q33: Recommendation engines are a type of personalization software.
The lecture states, 'Recommendations are instances of personalization software' (23:39-23:47).
Q34: Recommend items rated highly by these similar users but not yet rated by the current user.
The process described is: 'From a given user find out similar users through those ratings. So you have to find out which 
two users are similar and recommend items rated highly by these similar users but not rated by the current 
user' (24:44-25:02).
Q35: Because certain product categories or purchase frequencies might be more indicative of taste than others.
The lecturer asks, 'do you think that all purchase behavior should be similarly treated?' and gives an example of a 
movie series where one user watched all seasons and another only five, implying that depth of engagement might 
matter more (27:01-27:39).
Q36: It cannot incorporate or leverage user ratings or reviews from others.
A stated disadvantage is being 'unable to exploit quality judgements of other users' and that 'the judgement the ratings 
is not considered here' (35:01-35:10).
Q37: Alibaba (for B2B).
The lecturer mentions Alibaba in the context of 'other in the B2B sector also there are Alibaba and let us say there is 
Blackbuck and there are various kinds of aggregator firms that is coming up' (1:42-1:55).
Q38: It was more expensive and less available, especially on mobile phones.
The lecturer contrasts the past with the present, stating 'At our time when let us say around 10 years back when we 
were youngsters our the Internet was not very much available and the cost of having Internet in your mobile phone was 
very high' (1:10-1:21).
Q39: On the post-purchase page (e.g., after payment successful) or when they open Amazon again after a 
recent purchase.
The lecture suggests this type of recommendation is 'more applicable when you went to the purchase you got the 
purchase order number and etcetera, payment successful, that particular page' or 'when you open up your Amazon. You 
say that okay, so that is the last purchase that you have made' (13:31-14:00).
Q40: Fit.
The lecture explains that 'Feed is important because you will never, otherwise you will never buy' and uses the example 
of not recommending programming titles to a mother unless it fits her parental role (18:03-19:17).
Q41: They generate too much information, making it difficult to decide which data to use.
The lecture states, 'Old customers can have a glut of information. So old customer have sometimes have huge 
information, you do not know which one to use, which one not to use' (21:34-21:40).
Q42: Customer Relationship Management (CRM).
The lecture states, 'In the broad domain of marketing, it comes under CRM' (24:09-24:15).
Q43: Over-personalization.
The lecture lists 'cold start,' 'sparsity,' 'first rater,' and 'popularity bias' as problems for collaborative filtering. Over-
personalization is not mentioned (31:40-32:33).
Q44: Genre, time period, length, number of heroes/heroines, director.
The lecturer explains, 'content can be the genre of the movie, can be the time period of the movie, length of the movie I 
would say, can be the how many heroes, how many heroines, who is the major figure, who is the director' (33:10-33:27).
Q45: It does not require data of other users.
One of the listed advantages of content-based recommendation is 'No need of data of other users' (34:05-34:10).
Q46: In-store recommendations (similar to cross-selling).
The lecturer explicitly states that in brick-and-mortar stores 'recommendation engine works' by placing related products 
together like 'Scotch bite or the powder, washing powder, dish washing powder will be there, just beside the 
crockeries' (9:03-9:25). This is an analogy for cross-selling in physical retail.
Q47: To use the most similar 'n' users.
The lecture states, 'Standard approach is to use the most similar n users' (30:42-30:51).
Q48: Customer search items imply active user seeking; recommendations imply passive suggestions to users.
The lecture contrasts these by saying, 'users search items... you find out the user and you see that what kind of items 
are good for him' vs. 'in case of recommendations you find out the items and you suggest that the items are suggested 
to the users rather than users are searching the items' (10:32-10:51).
Q49: Their gender (male).
The lecture provides this exact example: 'if it is apparel then probably the gender matching is more important... so the 
male of Bengal and male of Odisha will probably wear similar kind of products in comparison to male of Bengal and 
female of Bengal' (28:56-29:10).
Q50: E-commerce, despite its current size, represents a major future opportunity and area for investment.
The lecturer highlights that 'the growth rate of e-commerce is pretty high' and though 'not significant if we compare with 
the total retail industry, the growth rate is something which is very much higher for e-commerce firms,' indicating its 
strategic importance (2:41-2:52).
Q51: Items purchased concurrently in a single transaction (basket data).
As stated at 14:36, 'in this case only customer basket data in the same basket will be matched.'
Q52: Content-Based Recommendation.
One of the advantages of Content-Based Recommendation listed is 'Able to recommend to users with unique 
tastes' (34:14-34:18) and 'Able to recommend new and unpopular items also' (34:21-34:26), which directly addresses 
this scenario.
Q53: The average rating given by user 'a' to all items.
While the exact definition of 'ra' isn't explicitly stated as 'average rating given by user a to all items' in text, the prediction 
formula `ra + weighted sum ...` (31:14-31:28) implies `ra` is a baseline, typically the user's average rating, from which 
deviations are predicted. This is a standard interpretation of such formulas in collaborative filtering.
Q54: To help retail firms, especially e-commerce, address challenges and leverage opportunities.
The lecture states, 'Given that as a background we have to focus on how Marketing Analytics can help retail firms as 
well and to be specific e-commerce firms because that is where the application is pretty high' (2:52-3:04).
Q55: It consumes valuable computing resources (time and money) without adding marginal benefit/incremental 
sales.
The lecture explicitly states, 'If you anyway buy it, so I will not recommend anything which you will anyway buy' because 
it would incur 'some cost' in 'resource, in your time allocation' and 'it will be a basically a loss for you' (17:53-19:51).
Q56: It fuels the growth of e-commerce by making online access ubiquitous and affordable.
The reduction in internet cost and widespread offers are directly cited as reasons for the 'coming up in the whole world 
actually in a quite a big way' for e-commerce (0:56-1:33).
Q57: User-based focuses on similar customers, while item-based focuses on similar items.
The lecture mentions 'Amazon's item to item collaborative filtering is focusing on finding similar instead of similar items 
instead of similar customers' (23:22-23:28). This directly contrasts the two approaches, with the implicit understanding 
that traditional collaborative filtering often focuses on users.
Q58: Women's lifestyle and needs change drastically over different life stages (e.g., adolescent, mom).
The lecture refers to a previous segmentation video, stating 'women life changes and that is why woman's lifestyle not 
lifestyle say woman's life period, life span is a very interesting case for marketers because that changes overtime like 
anything' (22:08-22:16), with examples like 'adolescent versus who is in let us say in her youth age and then after a 
mom and probably after the second child' (22:16-22:27).
Q59: Even a small percentage of conversion, when scaled across millions of sales, can generate substantial 
additional revenue.
The lecture explains that even if '90 % case it is a failure, only 10 % case that recommendation leads to some 
successful conversion,' if 10 million mobile phones are sold, 1 million covers at 500 rupees each generates '500 million 
extra revenue,' making it a 'very big number' (5:54-6:19).
Q60: Almost all existing commercial recommenders use this approach.
The lecture states, 'Almost all existing commercial recommender using this approach. So this is the most common 
approach' (25:56-26:05).
Q61: The overwhelming volume of historical data available for long-term customers, making it hard to prioritize 
relevant information.
The lecture mentions that 'Old customers can have a glut of information. So old customer have sometimes have huge 
information, you do not know which one to use, which one not to use' (21:34-21:40).

---
Quiz: Lecture 35 : Recommendation Engine and Retail Analytics 
(Contd.)
Q1. In collaborative filtering, what is the primary method for creating a similarity matrix when dealing 
with rating-based data (e.g., 1 to 5 point scale)?
   (A) Euclidean distance of ratings
   (B) Correlation of the ratings
   (C) Jaccard similarity of purchased items
   (D) Manhattan distance of ratings
Q2. For collaborative filtering based on purchase decisions, how is a customer typically represented, 
and what similarity measure is then used?
   (A) As a scalar value of total purchases; Euclidean distance
   (B) As an N-dimensional vector of items; Cosine similarity
   (C) As a binary vector of demographics; Pearson correlation
   (D) As a time-series of purchase events; Dynamic Time Warping
Q3. When using cosine similarity in collaborative filtering, what does a higher cosine value indicate?
   (A) Lower similarity between entities
   (B) Higher similarity between entities
   (C) No relationship between entities
   (D) Inverse relationship between entities
Q4. What is a major challenge for user-based collaborative filtering when dealing with very large 
datasets, such as 10 million customers and 1 million items?
   (A) Inability to handle sparse data
   (B) Difficulty in interpreting recommendations
   (C) Severe performance and scaling issues
   (D) Over-reliance on historical data
Q5. What is one potential consequence of applying dimensionality reduction techniques to a user-
based collaborative filtering model?
   (A) Increased computation time
   (B) Improved real-time performance without quality impact
   (C) Reduced quality of the recommendation
   (D) Elimination of the cold-start problem
Q6. How do cluster models primarily aim to improve the scalability of recommendation engines 
compared to traditional collaborative filtering?
   (A) By eliminating the need for similarity calculations
   (B) By dividing the customer base into segments and processing within those segments
   (C) By requiring real-time online segmentation for every user
   (D) By focusing solely on content-based features
Q7. What is a key drawback of cluster models regarding recommendation quality?
   (A) They are unable to handle diverse customer preferences.
   (B) The recommendations are less relevant because the similar customers found by clusters might not be the most 
similar.
   (C) They require constant re-clustering, leading to instability.
   (D) They only work with binary purchase data, not ratings.
Q8. To improve the quality of cluster models, what technique is mentioned, and why is it often 
impractical?
   (A) Increasing the number of items in the catalog; due to data storage limits.
   (B) Implementing online segmentation; because it's almost as expensive as collaborative filtering.
   (C) Reducing the number of clusters; because it oversimplifies user behavior.
   (D) Using content-based filtering; because it ignores user preferences.
Q9. In the context of the provided matrix example for item-item and user-user collaborative filtering, 
what does performing a 'column wise calculation' yield?
   (A) User-to-user similarity
   (B) Item-to-item similarity
   (C) Product popularity score
   (D) Customer purchase frequency
Q10. Using the same matrix example, what does performing a 'row wise' calculation allow you to 
determine?
   (A) The similarity between different items
   (B) The total number of purchases for each item
   (C) How similar customer A and customer B are
   (D) The average rating for each item
Q11. What is a primary advantage of item-to-item collaborative filtering for e-commerce firms, as 
highlighted in the lecture?
   (A) It requires no offline computation.
   (B) It scales better because the number of items is generally less than the number of customers.
   (C) It is immune to the cold-start problem.
   (D) It provides more diverse recommendations.
Q12. Item-to-item collaborative filtering typically requires what kind of computation to handle its 
'expensive' similar items table?
   (A) Real-time, on-demand calculation
   (B) Online, continuous streaming analysis
   (C) Offline, batch processing beforehand
   (D) Client-side, browser-based computation
Q13. According to the MovieLens dataset results cited, which recommendation approach achieved 
the 'best overall results' in terms of both computation time and mean absolute error?
   (A) User-based collaborative filtering with dimensionality reduction
   (B) Cluster models with online segmentation
   (C) Item-to-item collaborative filtering
   (D) A purely content-based approach
Q14. For item-to-item collaborative filtering, what happens during the 'online recommendation' phase 
after the similar item table is built?
   (A) It re-calculates all item similarities in real-time.
   (B) It finds items similar to the user's purchased/rated items, aggregates them, and recommends popular correlated 
items.
   (C) It asks the user for explicit feedback on new items.
   (D) It performs a full re-clustering of all users.
Q15. What is a characteristic advantage of item-to-item collaborative filtering's online component?
   (A) It is heavily dependent on the number of customers.
   (B) It scales independently of the catalog size.
   (C) It scales independently of the catalog size or the number of customers.
   (D) It requires constant human intervention for accuracy.
Q16. Pandora's music recommendation system is described as being based on what unique data 
source?
   (A) User listening history from social media
   (B) Real-time popularity trends on streaming platforms
   (C) The Music Genome Project, assigning 400 attributes per song
   (D) Demographic information of its users
Q17. A benefit of Pandora's music recommendation method, as described, is that it:
   (A) Needs lots of user information
   (B) Is highly scalable for any library size
   (C) Provides accurate recommendations without needing much user information
   (D) Allows for unlimited expansion of its music library
Q18. Netflix's movie recommendation system is characterized as a 'hybrid model' because it 
combines what different types of techniques?
   (A) Demographic and random walk
   (B) Collaborative, content-based, knowledge-based, and demographic
   (C) Only user-based and item-based collaborative filtering
   (D) Purely content-based analysis of movie genres
Q19. What is the primary motivation for companies like Google YouTube to provide effective video 
recommendations?
   (A) To reduce server load and bandwidth usage
   (B) To increase user engagement, leading to more ad revenue
   (C) To diversify their content library by identifying gaps
   (D) To comply with regulatory requirements for content diversity
Q20. In 2010, Google YouTube shifted its recommendation system from a 'random walk' approach to 
one based on what specific method?
   (A) User-based collaborative filtering
   (B) Content-based similarity of video metadata
   (C) Amazon's item-to-item collaborative filtering
   (D) Demographic segmentation and targeting
Q21. What is a core principle behind ensemble methods in recommendation systems, such as the 
one described for Netflix?
   (A) To rely on a single, highly optimized algorithm for all recommendations.
   (B) To simplify the recommendation process by reducing computational complexity.
   (C) To combine multiple techniques to overcome the shortcomings of individual techniques.
   (D) To exclusively use content features, ignoring user behavior.
Q22. When representing a customer as an N-dimensional vector of items for purchase decision-
based collaborative filtering, what values might represent 'purchased' and 'not purchased'?
   (A) Only 1 (purchased) and 0 (not purchased)
   (B) Only positive values for purchased items
   (C) 1 (purchased), 0 (not purchased), or -1 (negatively rated/not purchased)
   (D) Any arbitrary real numbers
Q23. After finding similar customers in user-based collaborative filtering, how are recommendations 
generated?
   (A) By recommending the most recently added items to the catalog.
   (B) By randomly selecting items from the entire catalog.
   (C) By ranking each item based on how many similar customers have purchased it.
   (D) By showing items that the target user has already purchased.
Q24. What is the computational complexity concern mentioned when dealing with 'm number of 
customers and n number of items' in collaborative filtering?
   (A) Memory overflow for storing item attributes.
   (B) The need to create a matrix which is m into n sizes.
   (C) Difficulty in parallel processing of customer data.
   (D) Slow network transfer speeds for large datasets.
Q25. In cluster models, once a user is assigned to a segment, how are recommendations generated 
for that user?
   (A) Based on the most popular items across all segments.
   (B) Based on the purchase and ratings of customers within that specific segment.
   (C) By using content features of items the user has previously ignored.
   (D) Through a random selection process to ensure diversity.
Q26. What is the relationship between the number of items and the number of customers in a typical 
established e-commerce firm, and what implication does this have for collaborative filtering?
   (A) Number of items is much higher than customers; favors user-based CF.
   (B) Number of customers is much lower than items; favors content-based CF.
   (C) Number of items is generally lesser than customers; favors item-to-item CF.
   (D) Number of items and customers are roughly equal; favors hybrid models.
Q27. When comparing user-based collaborative filtering, cluster models, and item-to-item 
collaborative filtering, which one is stated to have excellent recommendation quality due to 
recommending highly correlated similar items?
   (A) User-based collaborative filtering (with dimensionality reduction)
   (B) Cluster models (with online segmentation)
   (C) Item-to-item collaborative filtering
   (D) All three have equally excellent quality.
Q28. What is the main reason user-based collaborative filtering is considered 'impractical for large 
datasets' in terms of scalability and computation?
   (A) It requires no offline computation, which means all work must be done online.
   (B) It inherently reduces recommendation quality with larger datasets.
   (C) It often relies on proprietary algorithms that don't scale well.
   (D) Its accuracy decreases significantly with increasing user numbers.
Q29. Cluster models are noted to 'perform much on a computation offline' but have what drawback?
   (A) They are unable to handle new users without re-clustering.
   (B) Recommended quality is relatively poor.
   (C) They are not suitable for real-time applications.
   (D) They require extensive computational resources during online use.
Q30. What analogy is used to describe the function of cluster models in breaking down customers?
   (A) A neural network for pattern recognition.
   (B) A decision tree for classification.
   (C) K nearest neighbor model / K means clustering model.
   (D) A regression model for predicting future behavior.
Q31. What is the approach for an item-to-item collaborative filtering system to generate 
recommendations for a user?
   (A) Match the user's demographic profile to item attributes.
   (B) Build a similar items table, then match the user's purchased/rated items to similar items.
   (C) Randomly select items that have high overall popularity.
   (D) Identify users with similar purchasing patterns and recommend what they bought.
Q32. In item-to-item collaborative filtering, how is the similarity between two items typically 
measured?
   (A) Based on the number of shared categories.
   (B) Using the cosine measure between their customer vectors.
   (C) By calculating their price difference.
   (D) Through a hierarchical clustering algorithm.
Q33. What is a potential disadvantage of Pandora's recommendation system, despite its accuracy?
   (A) It scales very well, but sacrifices accuracy.
   (B) It requires frequent user intervention to tune preferences.
   (C) It does not scale very well and can feel limited in library size.
   (D) It relies heavily on demographic data which can be biased.
Q34. Netflix's recommendation system incorporates a wide range of algorithms, including an 
'ensemble method' of how many different algorithmic approaches?
   (A) 5 to 10
   (B) Approximately 50
   (C) 107
   (D) Over 1000
Q35. What is meant by 'batch processing beforehand' in the context of recommendation engines?
   (A) Processing user requests in batches for faster response times.
   (B) Performing computationally intensive calculations offline before real-time requests.
   (C) Grouping similar items into batches for easier management.
   (D) Storing all user data in batches for security reasons.
Q36. Which type of collaborative filtering was found to be particularly effective for video 
recommendations, as adopted by YouTube?
   (A) User-based collaborative filtering
   (B) Cluster-based recommendation engines
   (C) Item-to-item collaborative filtering
   (D) Content-based filtering using video transcripts
Q37. What is the purpose of a 'similarity matrix' in collaborative filtering?
   (A) To store user demographic data.
   (B) To map items to categories.
   (C) To quantify how alike users or items are to each other.
   (D) To track the purchase history of all users.
Q38. When considering the vector representation of items for item-to-item collaborative filtering, 
what does each dimension of the vector correspond to?
   (A) A specific feature or attribute of the item.
   (B) A customer who has purchased that item.
   (C) A rating score given by a user.
   (D) A unique item category.
Q39. What is the main distinction between user-based and item-based collaborative filtering?
   (A) User-based focuses on content attributes, item-based on user ratings.
   (B) User-based finds similarities between users, item-based finds similarities between items.
   (C) User-based requires offline computation, item-based requires real-time processing.
   (D) User-based is always more accurate, item-based is always faster.
Q40. Why might 'sampling customers' be used in collaborative filtering, and what is a potential trade-
off?
   (A) To increase the diversity of recommendations, but it might slow down the system.
   (B) To reduce runtime, potentially with a little reduction of similarity.
   (C) To focus on high-value customers, at the cost of ignoring new users.
   (D) To improve data security by anonymizing customer identities.
Q41. What is a 'cold-start problem' (not explicitly defined, but implied by context of needing user/item 
data)?
   (A) Difficulty in recommending items to new users or new items without sufficient data.
   (B) Problems caused by recommendations during cold weather.
   (C) A bug in the recommendation engine that causes it to freeze.
   (D) The challenge of starting a new recommendation system from scratch.
Q42. In the comparison of scalability and quality, what is a consistent finding for methods that aim to 
'reduce the time taking' for computations?
   (A) They invariably improve recommendation quality.
   (B) Their quality generally gets reduced.
   (C) They only work for small datasets.
   (D) They become more expensive to implement.
Q43. What type of data are the 'values' in the illustrative matrix (e.g., 17, 0, 5, 000, 44444) mentioned 
in the lecture section about item-item similarity?
   (A) Item price points
   (B) Customer demographic scores
   (C) Number of purchases made
   (D) Item inventory levels
Q44. The concept 'if two items are bought by similar type of people then they are similar' is the 
underlying principle for which collaborative filtering type?
   (A) Content-based filtering
   (B) Item-to-item collaborative filtering
   (C) Demographic filtering
   (D) Knowledge-based filtering
Q45. What are the two main performance metrics discussed for comparing recommendation engine 
types?
   (A) Storage capacity and power consumption
   (B) Accuracy and precision
   (C) Scalability and quality
   (D) Latency and throughput
Q46. What is one characteristic of users in an e-commerce platform that affects collaborative filtering 
efficiency, as mentioned in the lecture?
   (A) Most customers have a very large number of purchases.
   (B) Most customers have very few purchases.
   (C) Customer purchase patterns are generally random.
   (D) All customers have similar purchase frequencies.
Q47. When defining the vector for purchase decision-based similarity, a negative value (-1) could 
represent what?
   (A) A highly rated item
   (B) An item that was not purchased or negatively rated
   (C) An item bought at a discount
   (D) An item with low inventory
Q48. The lecture refers to collaborative filtering as a discussion for 'recommendation engine and 
retail analytics.' What is the broader field this topic falls under?
   (A) Supply Chain Management
   (B) Financial Accounting
   (C) Marketing Analytics
   (D) Human Resources Management
Q49. Why is 'dimensionally reduction' sometimes applied in collaborative filtering, despite its 
potential drawbacks?
   (A) To increase the quality of recommendations.
   (B) To reduce the need for batch processing.
   (C) To increase performance (e.g., speed up computations).
   (D) To ensure data privacy for customers.
Q50. In the context of cluster models, why might the 'similarity' found by the clustering method be 
different from actual purchased data similarity?
   (A) Clustering methods always prioritize speed over accuracy.
   (B) The clustering method might use different criteria or data for forming clusters than just purchased data.
   (C) Cluster models intentionally create dissimilar groups to encourage exploration.
   (D) The 'similarity' in clusters is based on user demographics only.
Q51. What is the meaning of 'MAE' (Mean Absolute Error) in the context of the MovieLens dataset 
results?
   (A) The average time taken for a recommendation to be generated.
   (B) A measure of how far off the recommended ratings were from actual user ratings.
   (C) The total number of errors encountered during processing.
   (D) The average percentage of items correctly predicted.
Q52. What kind of systems are Pandora, Netflix, and Google YouTube given as examples of?
   (A) Content Management Systems
   (B) Customer Relationship Management platforms
   (C) Recommendation Systems
   (D) Payment Processing Gateways
Q53. What is a 'correlation matrix' typically used for in collaborative filtering, especially with rating-
based data?
   (A) To identify popular items among all users.
   (B) To show the statistical relationship between different users' or items' ratings.
   (C) To visualize the distribution of item prices.
   (D) To store the metadata of various products.
Q54. If a recommendation engine is built around the idea that 'whoever has seen item X, if they have 
also seen item Y, then X and Y are similar products,' this is characteristic of which approach?
   (A) User-based filtering
   (B) Content-based filtering
   (C) Item-based filtering
   (D) Demographic filtering
Q55. What is the primary objective of 'online segmentation' mentioned in the context of cluster 
models?
   (A) To update cluster centroids in real-time.
   (B) To improve the quality of recommendations from cluster models.
   (C) To reduce the number of initial clusters.
   (D) To classify new items into existing categories.
Q56. The phrase 'Cos 0 is 1. Sin 0 is 0. Cos 0 is 1 means specifically, the higher the cosine value the 
higher is the similarity' illustrates which mathematical property?
   (A) The inverse relationship between angle and cosine value.
   (B) The direct relationship between angle and cosine value.
   (C) That a smaller angle (closer to 0) results in a cosine value closer to 1 (max similarity).
   (D) That a larger angle (closer to 90) results in a cosine value closer to 1 (max similarity).
Q57. What does 'OMN' refer to in the statement 'In practice, it is closer to OMN that means as most 
customers have very few purchases'?
   (A) Order of Magnitude for Network traffic
   (B) Optimal Model Normalization
   (C) A computational complexity notation related to sparse matrices (often O(m*n) for dense, but optimized for sparse)
   (D) Online Marketing Niche
Q58. The lecture describes 'content based method' as one of the primary features discussed. What 
does a content-based recommendation system primarily rely on?
   (A) Similarities between users' purchase histories.
   (B) Attributes or descriptions of the items themselves.
   (C) Feedback from other users on item quality.
   (D) Real-time market trends.
Q59. What was the total number of users and movies in the MovieLens dataset cited for performance 
comparison?
   (A) 1 million users and 6040 movies
   (B) 6040 users and 3090 movies
   (C) 3090 users and 1 million movies
   (D) 10 million users and 1 million movies
Answer Key
Q1: Correlation of the ratings
The transcript states, 'In the rating based, we create the similarity based on the correlation of the ratings. Because 
ratings are often given in 1 to 5 point scale. We can find out correlations of that.'
Q2: As an N-dimensional vector of items; Cosine similarity
The lecture explains, 'approach is represent a customer as a N-dimensional vector of items...and then we find out 
cosine matrix between the customers.'
Q3: Higher similarity between entities
The speaker clarifies, 'Cos 0 is 1 means specifically, the higher the cosine value the higher is the similarity. Because the 
theta, the angle is smaller.'
Q4: Severe performance and scaling issues
The transcript mentions, 'So for very large data sets, such as 10 million customers and 1 million items, the algorithm 
encounters severe performance and scaling issues.'
Q5: Reduced quality of the recommendation
The lecture states, 'dimensionally reduction is that is why is an important factor, can increase the performance but can 
also reduce the quality of the recommendation.'
Q6: By dividing the customer base into segments and processing within those segments
Cluster models 'divide the customer base in many segments' so you 'do not deal with M number of customers, you deal 
with small m number of clusters.'
Q7: The recommendations are less relevant because the similar customers found by clusters might not be the 
most similar.
The lecture states, 'the recommendations are less relevant because the similar customers that the clusters find are not 
the similar, not the most similar customers.'
Q8: Implementing online segmentation; because it's almost as expensive as collaborative filtering.
To improve quality, 'it needs online segmentation, which is almost as expensive as finding similar customers using 
collaborative filtering.'
Q9: Item-to-item similarity
The speaker explains, 'if I do a column wise calculation...I will get the similarity of item 1 with item 2 with item 3 and so 
on.'
Q10: How similar customer A and customer B are
The lecture states, 'On the other hand, same thing if I do the row wise, I will find out how customer A and customer B 
are similar.'
Q11: It scales better because the number of items is generally less than the number of customers.
The advantage is that 'the number of items in e-commerce firm is generally lesser than the number of customers... it is 
better to do item to item collaborative filtering because that takes less time.'
Q12: Offline, batch processing beforehand
The lecture notes, 'Offline computation has to be done because builds a similar item table that calculation will trigger 
because lots of time it is extremely time intensive.' And later, 'achieved by creating the expensive similar items table 
offline.'
Q13: Item-to-item collaborative filtering
The lecture explicitly states, 'The best overall results are reached by item to item collaborative filtering.'
Q14: It finds items similar to the user's purchased/rated items, aggregates them, and recommends popular 
correlated items.
The online recommendation process is described as: 'give a similar item tables, the algorithm finds items similar to each 
of the user’s purchased and ratings. Aggregate those items and then recommends the most popular correlated items.'
Q15: It scales independently of the catalog size or the number of customers.
The transcript states, 'An online component looking up similar item scales independently of the catalog size or the 
number of customers.'
Q16: The Music Genome Project, assigning 400 attributes per song
Pandora 'is based its recommendation on data for Music Genome Project. Assigns 400 attributes for each song.'
Q17: Provides accurate recommendations without needing much user information
The benefit listed is: 'Accurate method do not need lots of user information.'
Q18: Collaborative, content-based, knowledge-based, and demographic
Netflix uses 'both the purchase behavior of the customer and the content, so create a hybrid model. Collaborative, 
content based, and knowledge based and demographic techniques serve as the basis of the recommendation system.'
Q19: To increase user engagement, leading to more ad revenue
The lecture states Google's motto is to 'Increase the number of videos, increase the length of time and maximize the 
enjoyment... Ultimately, Google can increase revenue by showing more ads.'
Q20: Amazon's item-to-item collaborative filtering
The transcript notes, 'Give up the old recommendation system based on random walk. Change to a new one based on 
Amazon’s item to item collaborative filtering.'
Q21: To combine multiple techniques to overcome the shortcomings of individual techniques.
The benefit of ensemble methods is: 'Each of these techniques have some short comings, use multiple techniques 
together to overcome, so one shortcoming of one technique is overcome by the advantage of another technique.'
Q22: 1 (purchased), 0 (not purchased), or -1 (negatively rated/not purchased)
The lecture explains, 'The vector is positive for purchased or positively rated items and negative for negatively 
purchased or negatively rated items. So the vector is either 1, 0 or 1, minus 1 or something like that.'
Q23: By ranking each item based on how many similar customers have purchased it.
The process is described as: 'generates recommendations based on a few customers who are most similar... and then 
rank each item according to the how many similar customers have purchased it.'
Q24: The need to create a matrix which is m into n sizes.
The speaker mentions, 'Computationally expensive because if there are m number of customers and n number of items, 
so you have to create a matrix which is m into n sizes.'
Q25: Based on the purchase and ratings of customers within that specific segment.
The lecture states, 'And uses the purchase and ratings of the customers in the segment to generate 
recommendations... I will focus on only those customer who are in the same segment and then we will do the 
calculations.'
Q26: Number of items is generally lesser than customers; favors item-to-item CF.
The speaker notes, 'the number of items in e-commerce firm is generally lesser than the number of customers... it is 
better to do item to item collaborative filtering because that takes less time.'
Q27: Item-to-item collaborative filtering
In the comparison section, it's stated for item-to-item CF: 'Recommendation quality is excellent since it recommends 
high correlated similar items.'
Q28: It requires no offline computation, which means all work must be done online.
The comparison table notes for user-based CF: 'requires no offline computation, impractical or large data sets because 
it takes lots of time.' The 'no offline computation' means all that 'lots of time' is on the real-time path.
Q29: Recommended quality is relatively poor.
For cluster models, the transcript states: 'perform much on a computation offline but recommended quality is relatively 
poor.'
Q30: K nearest neighbor model / K means clustering model.
The speaker says, 'So it is like, like probably K nearest neighbor model. K means clustering model.'
Q31: Build a similar items table, then match the user's purchased/rated items to similar items.
Item-to-item works by: 'Build a similar items table... Matches each of the user’s purchased and rated items to similar 
items.'
Q32: Using the cosine measure between their customer vectors.
The lecture explains, 'The similarity between two items use the cosine measure. The same thing, each vector 
corresponding to item rather than a customer. So it corresponds to an item and vector of M dimensions corresponding 
to customers who have purchased that item.'
Q33: It does not scale very well and can feel limited in library size.
A listed problem is: 'Do not scale very well and often feels that Pandora’s library is somewhat limited.'
Q34: 107
The lecture mentions Netflix uses 'an ensemble method of 107 different algorithm approaches.'
Q35: Performing computationally intensive calculations offline before real-time requests.
Regarding scalability issues, the speaker notes, 'sometimes you have to do batch processing beforehand. You cannot 
do it in real time. You have to do it beforehand.' This refers to pre-calculating similarities or tables.
Q36: Item-to-item collaborative filtering
The lecture states, 'Amazon’s item to item collaborative filtering appears to be the best for video recommendation' and 
that YouTube changed to this method.
Q37: To quantify how alike users or items are to each other.
The core idea is 'You have to find out a similarity matrix between the customers' or items.
Q38: A customer who has purchased that item.
The lecture states, 'each vector corresponding to item rather than a customer. So it corresponds to an item and vector 
of M dimensions corresponding to customers who have purchased that item.'
Q39: User-based finds similarities between users, item-based finds similarities between items.
The speaker clarifies: 'In one case you find out the similarity between the users and in other case similarity between the 
items.'
Q40: To reduce runtime, potentially with a little reduction of similarity.
The transcript mentions, 'sampling customers can also reduce runtime even further with a little reduction of similarity.'
Q41: Difficulty in recommending items to new users or new items without sufficient data.
While not explicitly defined, the cold-start problem is a well-known issue in recommendation systems that relates to the 
need for data about users or items to make effective recommendations, a core challenge for collaborative filtering 
methods.
Q42: Their quality generally gets reduced.
The speaker concludes, 'The moment you will try to reduce the time taking, its quality gets reduced and in general, 
quality is low.'
Q43: Number of purchases made
The lecture clarifies: 'these values that you are seeing here all these values are basically the number of purchases that 
has been made.'
Q44: Item-to-item collaborative filtering
This statement directly follows the discussion of item-to-item similarity, leading to 'whoever has seen 17, if they have 
also seen 19 then 17 and 19 are similar products.'
Q45: Scalability and quality
The speaker introduces the comparison section by stating, 'Now if I compare these 3 basic measures in terms of the 
scalability and quality, basically these are the two issues whether it is time taking, whether it is the quality that you are 
getting.'
Q46: Most customers have very few purchases.
The lecture states, 'In practice, it is closer to OMN that means as most customers have very few purchases.'
Q47: An item that was not purchased or negatively rated
The transcript states, 'Vector is positive for purchased or positively rated items and negative for negatively purchased or 
negatively rated items. So the vector is either 1, 0 or 1, minus 1 or something like that.'
Q48: Marketing Analytics
The lecture begins: 'Hello everybody, welcome to Marketing Analytics course.'
Q49: To increase performance (e.g., speed up computations).
The lecture states, 'dimensionally reduction is that is why is an important factor, can increase the performance but can 
also reduce the quality of the recommendation.'
Q50: The clustering method might use different criteria or data for forming clusters than just purchased data.
The lecture mentions, 'the method that this clustering method using to create the clusters are not the purchased data. 
So that is why the similarity might be different.'
Q51: A measure of how far off the recommended ratings were from actual user ratings.
The speaker clarifies MAE as 'mean absolute radar means whatever the recommended people actually bought it or not.' 
While a bit colloquial, in the context of ratings, it means the average absolute difference between predicted and actual 
ratings.
Q52: Recommendation Systems
These are all discussed under the 'related applications' for recommendation engines.
Q53: To show the statistical relationship between different users' or items' ratings.
The transcript mentions 'correlation of the ratings' and 'column wise correlation matrix' to get similarity.
Q54: Item-based filtering
This is the precise explanation given for how item-to-item similarity is determined in the lecture.
Q55: To improve the quality of recommendations from cluster models.
The lecture states, 'To improve quality, it needs online segmentation.'
Q56: That a smaller angle (closer to 0) results in a cosine value closer to 1 (max similarity).
A cosine value of 1 (Cos 0) indicates vectors pointing in the exact same direction, meaning maximum similarity. As the 
angle increases, cosine decreases.
Q57: A computational complexity notation related to sparse matrices (often O(m*n) for dense, but optimized for 
sparse)
While 'OMN' isn't explicitly defined as 'O(m*n)' in the transcript, the context 'it is closer to OMN that means as most 
customers have very few purchases and sampling customers can also reduce runtime' strongly implies it's a 
computational complexity term for sparse data (sparse matrix multiplication or iteration), where 'm' is customers and 'n' 
is items. If purchases are few, the effective complexity is less than dense O(M*N).
Q58: Attributes or descriptions of the items themselves.
Though briefly mentioned at the end, 'content based method' refers to recommendations based on item attributes, 
distinct from collaborative filtering's reliance on user-item interactions.
Q59: 6040 users and 3090 movies
The lecture states: 'The MovieLens dataset 1 million ratings of 6040 users and 3090 movies.'
