Quiz: Lecture 36 : Recommendation Engine and Retail Analytics 
(Contd.)
Q1. What is the primary purpose of the 'easy.r' file mentioned at the beginning of the hands-on 
session?
   (A) To provide a more complex coding example for advanced users.
   (B) To simplify existing R code for better understanding by newcomers.
   (C) To directly download data from the internet.
   (D) To generate a new dataset for recommendation engines.
Q2. What is the typical structure of the input data matrix for the item-to-item collaborative filtering 
discussed?
   (A) Rows represent items, columns represent users, values are ratings (1-5).
   (B) Rows represent users, columns represent items, values are ratings (1-5).
   (C) Rows represent users, columns represent items, values are 0 or 1 indicating 'seen/not seen'.
   (D) Rows represent items, columns represent users, values are 0 or 1 indicating 'seen/not seen'.
Q3. Why does the instructor use a smaller dataset (19 users, 13 variables) for the hands-on 
demonstration instead of a larger one?
   (A) The larger dataset was corrupted and unusable.
   (B) To avoid long computation times during the video lecture.
   (C) The small dataset is more accurate for initial learning.
   (D) The larger dataset requires different R packages not covered.
Q4. What is the 'holder' matrix 'A' initialized with before populating similarity values?
   (A) Random numbers between 0 and 1.
   (B) All ones (1s).
   (C) All zeros (0s).
   (D) The original data matrix values.
Q5. In the item-to-item collaborative filtering approach, what kind of matrix is created to store 
similarities between items?
   (A) A user-user similarity matrix (m x m).
   (B) An item-item similarity matrix (n x n).
   (C) A user-item preference matrix (m x n).
   (D) A recommendation score matrix (m x n).
Q6. Why are the column names of the 'A' similarity matrix set to `data.Germany`'s column names 
minus the first entry?
   (A) The first column contains missing values.
   (B) The first column represents user IDs, not items.
   (C) The first column is a timestamp, irrelevant for similarity.
   (D) To reduce the matrix size for efficiency.
Q7. What characteristic property does the item-item similarity matrix exhibit?
   (A) It is always a diagonal matrix.
   (B) It is an asymmetric matrix.
   (C) It is a symmetric matrix (upper and lower triangles are mirrored).
   (D) All its values are identical.
Q8. What are the values on the diagonal elements of the item-item similarity matrix (e.g., similarity of 
item X with item X)?
   (A) 0, as an item cannot be similar to itself.
   (B) 0.5, representing average similarity.
   (C) 1, indicating perfect similarity with itself.
   (D) Undefined, as it's not calculated.
Q9. The lecture discusses using 'correlation' to calculate similarity between items. What is the typical 
range of correlation values before normalization?
   (A) 0 to 1
   (B) -1 to 1
   (C) -inf to +inf
   (D) 0 to 100
Q10. What is the primary reason for normalizing the correlation values in the similarity matrix?
   (A) To increase the speed of calculations.
   (B) To convert the values into a 0 to 1 range.
   (C) To remove negative correlation values entirely.
   (D) To make the matrix asymmetric.
Q11. Which formula is used for normalization, converting values 'x' from a range [min_x, max_x] to 
[0, 1]?
   (A) (x - max_x) / (max_x - min_x)
   (B) (x - min_x) / (max_x - min_x)
   (C) x / max_x
   (D) (x + min_x) / (max_x + min_x)
Q12. What does the recommendation matrix 'r' represent after initialization?
   (A) A matrix of all ones, ready for recommendations.
   (B) A copy of the original user-item purchase data.
   (C) A blank user-item matrix where recommendation scores will be calculated.
   (D) A similarity matrix between users.
Q13. According to the lecture, under what specific condition will an item *never* be recommended to 
a user?
   (A) If the item has a low similarity score with other items.
   (B) If the user has already seen/purchased that item.
   (C) If the item is new and has no similarity data.
   (D) If no other users have seen that item.
Q14. When calculating a recommendation score for a user for an *unseen* item, what is the first step 
after confirming the user hasn't seen it?
   (A) Calculate the average rating of that item across all users.
   (B) Find the top 'k' items most similar to the unseen item.
   (C) Check the user's demographic information.
   (D) Randomly assign a recommendation score.
Q15. What analogy is used to explain the logic of item-to-item recommendation?
   (A) Recommending books based on genre.
   (B) Recommending music based on artist.
   (C) Recommending 'Main Hoon Na' (a movie) based on similar movies seen.
   (D) Recommending restaurants based on location.
Q16. In the calculation of a recommendation score for an item to a user, if the user has *not* seen 
any of the top 'k' similar items, what will be the resulting score for the target item?
   (A) A very high score, indicating novelty.
   (B) An average score.
   (C) A score of 0.
   (D) A score based on global popularity.
Q17. What R function is used to identify the top similar items to a target item by sorting its similarity 
scores?
   (A) max()
   (B) mean()
   (C) sort()
   (D) cor()
Q18. Why does the instructor choose to take the similarity scores from the 2nd to 6th elements after 
sorting, instead of starting from the 1st?
   (A) The first element is always 0.
   (B) The first element represents the item's similarity with itself, which is not useful for recommendation.
   (C) To exclude outliers from the similarity calculation.
   (D) To ensure an even number of similar items.
Q19. What is the final output of the entire item-to-item recommendation process described in the 
lecture?
   (A) A list of all users and their original purchase history.
   (B) A matrix of similarity scores between all items.
   (C) A recommendation matrix showing top 5 recommended items (by name) for each user.
   (D) A single score indicating the overall effectiveness of the engine.
Q20. What are the two primary reasons why a recommendation score for a particular user-item pair 
might be 0 in the final 'r' matrix?
   (A) The user has already seen the item OR the item is too popular.
   (B) The user has already seen the item OR the user has seen no similar movies to the item.
   (C) The item has no similarity data OR the item is too expensive.
   (D) The user has not seen any movies at all OR the item is rated very low by others.
Q21. What R code snippet is used to select all columns except the first one from the `data.Germany` 
dataset when performing column operations?
   (A) `data.Germany[, 1]`
   (B) `data.Germany[, -1]`
   (C) `data.Germany[, 2:ncol(data.Germany)]`
   (D) `data.Germany[, c(1)]`
Q22. If the `data.Germany` dataset has 13 variables and the first is 'user', how many items are being 
considered for similarity calculations in the small dataset?
   (A) 19
   (B) 13
   (C) 12
   (D) 1
Q23. What does the variable `l` store after sorting and ordering similarity scores for a specific item 
`j`?
   (A) The actual similarity scores of the top similar items.
   (B) The original values (0s or 1s) from the `data.Germany` matrix.
   (C) The column indices (serial numbers) of the top similar items.
   (D) The user IDs who have seen item `j`.
Q24. When accessing `data.germany[i, (j + 1)]`, why is `j + 1` used for the column index?
   (A) To skip the last item in the dataset.
   (B) To account for R's 0-based indexing.
   (C) Because the first column of `data.Germany` is a user ID, and item columns start from the second index.
   (D) It's a typo; `j` should be used directly.
Q25. If a user has seen some, but not all, of the top 5 similar movies to a target movie, how is the 
recommendation score for the target movie calculated?
   (A) It's simply the sum of similarities of all top 5 movies.
   (B) It's the sum of similarities of *only* the top similar movies that the user *has* seen.
   (C) It's the average of similarities of all top 5 movies.
   (D) It's 0 because the user hasn't seen all of them.
Q26. What is the total number of observations (users) and variables (columns) in the larger 'last FM 
matrix Germany' dataset mentioned?
   (A) 19 observations, 13 variables
   (B) 1,257 observations, 286 variables
   (C) 286 observations, 1,257 variables
   (D) 1,000 observations, 500 variables
Q27. What does the instructor mean by 'cosine matrix' when briefly discussing it alongside 
correlation?
   (A) A matrix storing angles between item vectors.
   (B) A matrix calculated using cosine similarity as an alternative to correlation for measuring item similarity.
   (C) A matrix used for dimensional reduction.
   (D) A matrix representing the user's past ratings.
Q28. Why is it stated that if a huge dataset is used, saving the similarity matrix ('A') is a good idea?
   (A) To share it with other users more easily.
   (B) To prevent data corruption during calculations.
   (C) To avoid recalculating it every time, saving computation.
   (D) To visualize the matrix more effectively.
Q29. When creating the final recommendation output, how many recommendations are typically 
shown for each user in the example?
   (A) As many as there are items.
   (B) 1 (the single best recommendation).
   (C) 5 (top 5 recommendations).
   (D) A variable number, depending on the user.
Q30. What is the role of the `order()` function when determining similar items?
   (A) It sorts the similarity scores in ascending order.
   (B) It returns the actual similarity values in descending order.
   (C) It returns the *indices* (positions) of items in a sorted order, typically for identifying the most similar items.
   (D) It calculates the average similarity.

   (A) Recommending items based on similarities between users.
   (B) Recommending items based on the direct characteristics (features) of the items.
   (C) Recommending items based on similarities found between the items themselves.
   (D) Recommending items based on the popularity of items in general.
Q32. If a user 'X' has seen movies 'A' and 'B', and movie 'C' is very similar to 'A' but not to 'B', and 
movie 'D' is very similar to 'B' but not to 'A'. If user 'X' hasn't seen 'C' or 'D', how would an item-to-
item engine likely approach recommending 'C' to 'X'?
   (A) It would only recommend 'C' if 'X' has seen *both* 'A' and 'B'.
   (B) It would consider the similarity between 'C' and 'A' (which 'X' has seen) as a strong indicator.
   (C) It would recommend 'C' only if 'C' is popular among all users.
   (D) It would ignore 'A' and 'B' and look for other users similar to 'X'.

   (D) The algorithm failed to find enough relevant suggestions.

   (D) It will require a different algorithm entirely.

not the most efficient way, but acceptable for a small dataset?
   (A) It's a typo, should be `for i = 1 to 11`.
   (B) It recalculates symmetric values twice (e.g., correlation of X-Y and Y-X) and self-similarity, which isn't strictly 
necessary.

Q37. What is the implication of 'the new item new value is x minus minimum of x where old value is x.

   (B) Recommendations would be more diverse.
   (C) Recommendations would likely be less robust and potentially of lower quality due to reliance on a single, possibly 
noisy, data point.
   (D) It would not affect quality, only computation time.
Answer Key
Q1: To simplify existing R code for better understanding by newcomers.
The speaker mentions changing a difficult coding from the internet into an 'easier form' in 'easy.r' for newcomers.
Q2: Rows represent users, columns represent items, values are 0 or 1 indicating 'seen/not seen'.
The transcript states, 'The user in the left side. The items in the column and 0 and 1 is saying that whether something is 
seen or not, seen by a group of users.'
Q3: To avoid long computation times during the video lecture.
The instructor explains, 'because I have a time issue, it takes probably 3-4 minutes, 5 minutes in the calculations if I do 
it in the bigger file. So, if I do it with bigger file, it will have... I cannot sit idle in a video for 1 minute, 2 minute.'
Q4: All zeros (0s).
The instructor states, 'A is a matrix where all the entries are 0.'
Q5: An item-item similarity matrix (n x n).
The instructor says, 'This will create n number of products here and n number of products here and this will be a 
similarity matrix. So n into n similarity matrix gets created.'
Q6: The first column represents user IDs, not items.
The instructor clarifies, 'column names of data dot Germany, why minus 1? Because first entry is user that I will not 
take.'
Q7: It is a symmetric matrix (upper and lower triangles are mirrored).
The instructor explains, 'Whatever is value here, comes here. Whatever value here, comes here and so on. So upper 
side and lower side is symmetric.'
Q8: 1, indicating perfect similarity with itself.
The instructor states, 'What is the similarity of the diagonal elements? It will be exactly 1. Because I am absolutely fully 
similar to me.'
Q9: -1 to 1
The instructor explicitly mentions, 'these correlations are between correlation values. Correlation values are between 
minus 1 to plus 1.'
Q10: To convert the values into a 0 to 1 range.
The instructor explains, 'I am trying to make sure that all of them are scaled properly so that remain from 0 to 1.'
Q11: (x - min_x) / (max_x - min_x)
The instructor details the normalization as 'x minus minimum of x by maximum of x by minimum of x'. This is the 
standard min-max scaling formula.
Q12: A blank user-item matrix where recommendation scores will be calculated.
The instructor initializes 'r' by copying the data structure and then setting all relevant item entries to 0, stating, 'currently 
all these items are 0. So I will find out the recommendation scores for these items.'
Q13: If the user has already seen/purchased that item.
The instructor explicitly states, 'If this is the purchase history, the purchase 3, 5 is 1. That means if I have already 
purchased it, I will never recommend.'
Q14: Find the top 'k' items most similar to the unseen item.
The instructor explains, 'I simply find out that, okay, item number m if this is the item, which are the top similar items?'
Q15: Recommending 'Main Hoon Na' (a movie) based on similar movies seen.
The instructor uses the 'Main Hoon Na' movie example to illustrate the recommendation logic.
Q16: A score of 0.
The instructor shows an example where 'history is 0 0 0 0 0. So he has not seen any of these movies. Then how much 
will be the probability? 0'.
Q17: sort()
The instructor states, 'So that is what I am doing, sorting. I am sorting it out and it comes to be the first item to be 1... 
The next 5 items' similarity is like this, fair enough.'
Q18: The first element represents the item's similarity with itself, which is not useful for recommendation.
The instructor mentions, 'I am most similar to myself but it has no meaning.' when discussing `k is equal to sort a, j 2 to 
6`.
Q19: A recommendation matrix showing top 5 recommended items (by name) for each user.
The instructor concludes, 'So these are my suggestions. So now if I run it for all of these guys, that populates 
suggestions for each of these people.' and shows a matrix with 'Reco1, Reco2,...Reco5'.
Q20: The user has already seen the item OR the user has seen no similar movies to the item.
The instructor clarifies, '0 can come for two reasons, one is this guy has already seen the movie before that is why it is 
coming 0 or this guy has seen no similar movie.'
Q21: `data.Germany[, -1]`
While not explicitly shown as a single line for *selection*, the concept of `-1` for excluding the first column (user ID) is 
consistently applied when setting up column names for the similarity matrix.
Q22: 12
The instructor states, 'Because there are 13 variables. Now out of these 13 variables the first variable is user name, so 
that is why we are not using. The rest there are 12 items, that is why 12 by 12 matrix I am creating.'
Q23: The column indices (serial numbers) of the top similar items.
The instructor says, 'So that 2 to 6 if I put it in l, this gives my identity.' and 'l stores the similar items.'
Q24: Because the first column of `data.Germany` is a user ID, and item columns start from the second index.
The instructor explains, 'Why plus 1? Because the first column is the user column.' This is repeatedly emphasized when 
referencing item columns in `data.Germany`.
Q25: It's the sum of similarities of *only* the top similar movies that the user *has* seen.
In the example, the instructor refers to `history into the similarity score` where `history` is 0 or 1 for seen/not seen. If an 
item in the similar list is seen (1), its similarity contributes; otherwise (0), it does not.
Q26: 1,257 observations, 286 variables
The instructor states, 'you see there are 1,257 observations of 286 variables.'
Q27: A matrix calculated using cosine similarity as an alternative to correlation for measuring item similarity.
The instructor says, 'Now I will use the cosine matrix to do my calculation. So cosine is cosine of 3, 10 is basically the 
formula is summation of x y... that is what I am trying to create as my cosine matrix'. This implies it's an alternative 
similarity measure.
Q28: To avoid recalculating it every time, saving computation.
The instructor notes, 'If it is a huge dataset it is better to save it.' This is a common practice to save intermediate results 
that are computationally intensive.
Q29: 5 (top 5 recommendations).
The instructor explains, 'Why 5 columns? Because top 5 recommendation will come. In normal case in Netflix and 
etcetera 5 recommendations come so top 5.'
Q30: It returns the *indices* (positions) of items in a sorted order, typically for identifying the most similar 
items.
The instructor says, 'if I want to find out order, order means the serial number. Serial number of minus a, j will give me 
the order.' This indicates it returns the positions for sorting.
Q31: Recommending items based on similarities found between the items themselves.
The core concept is finding similarities between items (e.g., how similar is movie A to movie B) to make 
recommendations.
Q32: It would consider the similarity between 'C' and 'A' (which 'X' has seen) as a strong indicator.
The 'Main Hoon Na' analogy highlights this: 'I will recommend Main hoon na to one of my friend only if I have seen that 
my friend has also seen similar movies like Main hoon na.'
Q33: They are placeholder items because only the first three had significant positive scores.
The instructor says, 'The last two suggestions are random, the first three suggestions in this case because there were 
some positive value, I am getting some suggestions.' This implies that when there aren't enough strong 
recommendations, less relevant items fill the slots.
Q34: It will produce results but take considerably more time.
The instructor explicitly states that using the larger file 'will take some time... but you will get a result for that.'
Q35: User `user_id` has seen or purchased `item_id`.
The instructor states, '0 and 1 means whether one user has listened to one particular thing or not.' and 'something is 
seen or not, seen by a group of users.'
Q36: It recalculates symmetric values twice (e.g., correlation of X-Y and Y-X) and self-similarity, which isn't 
strictly necessary.
The instructor states, 'I should not have calculated 1 to 12. It would have been better if I have calculated from i is equal 
to 1 to i rather than 1 to 12, j is equal to 1 to i because see it is a matrix which is lower triangular matrix or upper 
triangular matrix. Other things are same, it is a symmetric.'
Q37: It maps values proportionally within a new range (0 to 1).
This is the formula for Min-Max scaling, which scales data to a fixed range, typically 0 to 1. The instructor also 
confirmed, 'So it is now covered between 0 and 1'.
Q38: Courses taken from Professor X and Professor Y.
The instructor uses 'Let us say the item 3 is professor X and item 10 is professor Y and these are the students and the 
students has rated professor X as or in this case purchased so let us say student, various student taken the courses of 
professor X and professor Y.'
Q39: By the percentage of students who took *both* professors' courses, or *neither*.
The instructor counts 'matching' instances where students either took both classes or neither, which indicates similarity 
in student preferences for those two professors.
Q40: The user has not seen any of those 5 similar items.
The instructor explains, 'The history is says h is 0 0 0 0 0. So he has not seen any of these movies.'
Q41: To ensure the 'r' matrix has the same dimensions and structure as the original data, but with all data-
specific values reset to 0.
The instructor says, 'By running these two lines simply what I created is 19, these are my users, these are my items. 
Okay, sorry, this is something that I probably have done wrong... 2 to 13 is equal to 0 that is the right thing to do.' This 
means preparing a blank matrix with the correct dimensions and labels.
Q42: It assigns the actual item names (from `data.Germany`'s columns) to the top 5 recommended slots for 
user `i`.
The instructor states, 'These are the column names, alexonfire, ac.dc, a perfect circle... these has been taken, these are 
all 0s, so this has been taken and all these.' And 'will give me the corresponding movie names.'
Q43: Recommendation Engine.
The entire lecture is titled 'Recommendation Engine and Retail Analytics (Contd.)' and focuses on building a 
recommendation engine.
Q44: The normalized value would be ( -0.5 - (-0.73) ) / (1 - (-0.73)) which is (0.23 / 1.73) or approximately 0.13.
Applying the formula (x - min_x) / (max_x - min_x) with x = -0.5, min_x = -0.73, and max_x = 1 yields ( -0.5 - (-0.73) ) / 
(1 - (-0.73)) = (0.23) / (1.73).
Q45: It would likely increase computation time and potentially introduce less relevant items into the score 
calculation.
While not explicitly stated for 'k', increasing the number of items considered in any calculation generally increases 
computation and might dilute the quality if less relevant items are included.
Q46: The actual formula for cosine similarity is the dot product of two vectors divided by the product of their 
magnitudes.
The instructor's verbal explanation is a bit convoluted ('summation of h into k by summation of k'), but the standard 
cosine similarity is `(X . Y) / (||X|| * ||Y||)`. The mention of 'summation of x y' is the start of a dot product.
Q47: Users prefer items that are similar to items they have liked or interacted with in the past.
The core logic is to find items similar to what a user has already shown interest in, and recommend those unseen 
similar items.
Q48: It indicates the data is in a comma-separated values format, a common tabular data structure.
`.csv` stands for comma-separated values, a standard file format for tabular data.
Q49: 3 professors, 8 students.
The instructor uses 'item 3 is professor X and item 10 is professor Y... and in professor 11 that is the another guy' for 
professors, and then shows 8 rows representing students' interactions with these professors.
Q50: `x` (no change needed)
If the range is already 0 to 1, then min_x = 0 and max_x = 1. The formula (x - 0) / (1 - 0) simplifies to x / 1 = x. No 
change is needed.
Q51: It's the instructor's affiliation.
The instructor introduces himself as 'Dr. Swagato Chatterjee from VGSOM IIT, Kharagpur who is taking this course.'
Q52: Item-to-item uses similarity between products, while user-to-user uses similarity between users.
The conclusion explicitly states, 'I will come back with user to user or user based collaborative filtering.' The lecture's 
focus on item similarity versus user similarity is the fundamental difference.
Q53: Because there are 12 items (excluding the user ID column) in the small dataset.
The instructor states, 'Because there are 13 variables. Now out of these 13 variables the first variable is user name, so 
that is why we are not using. The rest there are 12 items, that is why 12 by 12 matrix I am creating.'
Q54: A numeric matrix.
The `data.Germany` matrix itself contains numbers (0s and 1s), and the operations performed (setting to 0, calculating 
correlations, adding, dividing) all result in numeric values. Thus, `r` remains numeric.
Q55: The recommendation score calculation would not proceed as there's no new item to recommend.
The logic is designed to recommend *unseen* items. If a user has seen all the *most similar* items, the system would 
need to look further down the similarity list or conclude there are no strong new recommendations based on this 
method.
Q56: A matrix.
The instructor says, 'We are naming it as A. A is a matrix where all the entries are 0.'
Q57: Marketing Analytics course.
The instructor begins with, 'Hello everybody. Welcome to Marketing Analytics course.'
Q58: It represents data collected specifically from users in Germany.
The instructor refers to 'last FM matrix Germany' as a bigger file, implying the data source is related to Germany.
Q59: It's a matrix where both rows and columns represent the same set of items, for item-item similarity.
The instructor states, 'the column names of this matrix will be similar to these values, the column names of this matrix 
and the row names will also be the same. So the row names and column names both will be similar to the column 
names of the first original raw dataset.' This confirms it's an item-by-item comparison.
Q60: Recommendations would likely be less robust and potentially of lower quality due to reliance on a single, 
possibly noisy, data point.
Using a single data point makes the model very sensitive to noise or an outlier. Averaging across multiple similar items 
(like 5 or more) usually leads to more robust recommendations.

Quiz: Lecture 37 : Recommendation Engine and Retail Analytics 
(Contd.)
Q1. Which type of collaborative filtering is the primary focus of this lecture?
   (A) Content-based filtering
   (B) Item-to-item collaborative filtering
   (C) User-to-user collaborative filtering
   (D) Hybrid recommendation system
Q2. What is the initial size of the dataset used in this lecture before any filtering?
   (A) 19 observations and 13 variables
   (B) 13 observations and 19 variables
   (C) 15 observations and 12 variables
   (D) 12 observations and 15 variables
Q3. What does a '0' in the dataset represent regarding user activity?
   (A) The user has rated the item poorly
   (B) The user has not seen or listened to the item
   (C) The item is not available to the user
   (D) The user has seen the item multiple times
Q4. Why are users with a `rowSum` of zero (for item columns) filtered out from the dataset?
   (A) They are spammers or bots.
   (B) They have no historical data for recommendations.
   (C) Their data is corrupted or incomplete.
   (D) They are new users who will be handled by a separate system.
Q5. After filtering out users with no activity, how many observations (users) remain in the dataset?
   (A) 19
   (B) 13
   (C) 15
   (D) 12
Q6. Which similarity metric is most prominently used in the real world for collaborative filtering, 
according to the lecturer?
   (A) Correlation
   (B) Euclidean distance
   (C) Cosine similarity
   (D) Manhattan distance
Q7. Why is the dataset transposed (`T` function) before calculating correlations for user-based 
collaborative filtering?
   (A) To optimize memory usage.
   (B) To align users as rows for correlation calculation.
   (C) To prepare data for singular value decomposition.
   (D) To remove redundant features.
Q8. What is the purpose of normalizing the correlation matrix 'a' using the formula `(x - min_x) / 
(max_x - min_x)`?
   (A) To increase the range of similarity scores.
   (B) To convert all similarity scores into negative values.
   (C) To bind all similarity scores between 0 and 1.
   (D) To reduce the computational complexity.
Q9. In the recommendation loop, why is `r[i, j+1]` kept as 0 if `data.Germany[i, j+1]` is 1?
   (A) To prevent over-recommendation.
   (B) Because the user has already consumed that item.
   (C) It indicates a system error.
   (D) The item is out of stock.
Q10. How is the recommendation score for an item `j` to user `i` calculated when user `i` hasn't seen 
item `j`?
   (A) Simple average of all users' ratings for item `j`.
   (B) Sum of historical purchases of item `j` by similar users.
   (C) Weighted average of historical purchases of item `j` by similar users, weighted by their similarity scores.
   (D) Randomly assigned score based on item popularity.
Q11. What is a 'cold start' problem in collaborative filtering?
   (A) Difficulty in recommending items during winter seasons.
   (B) Inability to make recommendations for new users or new items due to lack of historical data.
   (C) A bug that causes the recommendation engine to crash upon startup.
   (D) A situation where the recommendation engine provides too many recommendations.
Q12. Before populating the recommendation matrix `r`, what values does it initially contain for all 
item-related columns?
   (A) Random numbers
   (B) Zeros
   (C) Ones
   (D) Null
Q13. If a user has a `rowSum` of zero, what does this imply about their activity in the given dataset?
   (A) They are highly active.
   (B) They have purchased all items.
   (C) They have not interacted with any of the listed items.
   (D) Their data is aggregated.
Q14. What function is used in R to calculate the correlation between different users?
   (A) SUM
   (B) MEAN
   (C) COR
   (D) TRANSPOSE

Answer Key
Q1: User-to-user collaborative filtering
The lecturer explicitly states at 00:32.710 that 'In this particular video, I will also talk about user to user collaborative 
filtering' and contrasts it with previous discussions on item-to-item.
Q2: 19 observations and 13 variables
At 01:35.470, the lecturer states, 'which has 19 observations and 13 variables.'
Q3: The user has not seen or listened to the item
At 02:08.960, the lecturer clarifies, 'these 0’s and 1’s means 0 means that he has not seen the video and 1 means that 
he has seen the video or in this case probably the he has listened to the song.'
Q4: They have no historical data for recommendations.
At 03:15.750, the lecturer explains, 'if you do not have historical data and in this context, we do not also have the 
demographic data of the person. So, if I have no data about that person, I cannot recommend him anything.'
Q5: 15
At 05:57.310, the lecturer states, 'I got 15 observations from 19 observations it got dropped to 15 observations.'
Q6: Cosine similarity
At 06:22.039, the lecturer mentions, 'but in the real world, cosine is the most prominently used matrix for finding out the 
similarity between two groups or in this case two vectors.'
Q7: To align users as rows for correlation calculation.
At 06:36.300, the lecturer asks, 'why I have taken transpose? Because now I am taking row wise.' This implies treating 
each user's item consumption as a vector (row) to find similarity between user vectors.
Q8: To bind all similarity scores between 0 and 1.
At 12:53.760, the lecturer states, 'what does it do? It actually binds the normalized x into 0 and 1, so the minimum value 
comes to be 0 and the maximum value comes to be 1.'
Q9: Because the user has already consumed that item.
At 16:50.190, the lecturer explains, 'If that value is 1, that means ith person has already seen j plus 1th movie, then I will 
not recommend him, then corresponding value will remain 0 in my r matrix.'
Q10: Weighted average of historical purchases of item `j` by similar users, weighted by their similarity scores.
At 28:02.919, the formula `r i comma j plus 1 is basically sum of h into k. h stands for the historical purchase history of 
users who were close to you. k is basically the similarity score by sum of k` describes a weighted average.
Q11: Inability to make recommendations for new users or new items due to lack of historical data.
At 03:52.299, the lecturer explicitly defines the cold start problem: 'collaborative filtering will not work when the data is 
new, when the user is new or the movie or whichever the product ID is new.'
Q12: Zeros
At 14:10.110, the lecturer says, 'r is equal to r minus 1 means whatever entry there was in r you actually remove them, 
all of them and then r dollar user is equal to data Germany dollar user that means basically, it looks like this: So, all 
these values are zero'. This implies initializing with zeros.
Q13: They have not interacted with any of the listed items.
At 02:51.710, the lecturer explains, 'Now, if all the values are zero then this guy has not seen any of the movies in this 
particular list.'
Q14: COR
At 09:10.950, the lecturer mentions, 'If I just try to find out correlation using the function called COR C, O, R which is 
available in the base package, so, COR will be the correlation function.'
Q15: To make the matrix more interpretable and visually clear.
At 12:08.339, the lecturer says, 'So whatever it is just to make sure that I can visualize it properly, I can see it properly. 
And I can understand that.'
Q16: Weighted average
At 26:25.960, the lecturer asks, 'So this is my actual observation, this is the weightages so, create a weighted average.'
Q17: They are dropped from the dataset as no recommendations can be made for them using collaborative 
filtering.
At 04:56.680, the lecturer states, 'So that means that I have to drop them, before I go ahead with doing any kind of 
modeling.'
Q18: 12
At 01:49.390, the lecturer states, 'Out of them first column is the user column itself. And from then, this column till the 
last column, this all these columns are actually whether a particular the movie or a particular in this case a song has 
been consumed.' If there are 13 total variables and one is for the user, 12 remain for items.
Q19: They always have a value of 1, showing a user's perfect correlation with themselves.
At 09:57.180, the lecturer mentions, 'correlation between 2 and 2 is 1, 3 and 3 is 1, 4 and 4 is 1, 5 and 5 is 1 and so on.' 
This refers to the correlation of a user with themselves.
Q20: The actual column index of the item/movie, accounting for the user ID column.
At 16:34.850, the lecturer clarifies, 'why j plus 1? Because first column in data dot Germany is the user column. So, j 
plus 1th column is actually talking about the jth movie.'
Q21: User-based focuses on user-to-user similarity, item-based focuses on item-to-item similarity.
At 32:19.870, the lecturer summarizes, 'it is item to item similarity that we are checking in item based collaborative 
filtering, this user to user similarity that we are checking in user base in similarity matrix.'
Q22: Movie 'X' will be recommended to user 'A' with a high score.
The weighted average calculation `sum(h * k) / sum(k)` implies that if a highly similar user (`k` is high) has seen the 
movie (`h` is 1), it significantly contributes to a higher recommendation score for the target user.
Q23: Data frame
At 10:33.220, the lecturer states, 'Now, I am converting this a to a data frame because that helps me in doing further 
some, some amount of analysis.'
Q24: 5
At 19:18.120, the lecturer says, 'I will take 2 comma 6, the next 5 based so, these are the top five similarity scores for ith 
customer with its neighbors.'
Q25: The recommendation score for movie `j` to user `i` will be 0.
If `h` (historical purchase data of similar users for that movie) is all zeros, then `sum(h * k)` will be zero, resulting in a 
recommendation score of zero. The lecturer also mentions at 28:29.520, 'zeroes mean either nobody who is close to me 
has seen these movies.'
Q26: The minimum value becomes 0 and the maximum value becomes 1.
At 12:53.760, the lecturer explains, 'so the minimum value comes to be 0 and the maximum value comes to be 1.'
Q27: A recommendation matrix (`r`) containing scores for movies not yet seen by users.
The entire process culminates in populating the `r` matrix with scores for each user and each movie they haven't seen, 
which are then used to generate final recommendations.
Q28: The process might take more time to run and reveal more nuances.
At 32:39.600, the lecturer suggests, 'I will strongly suggest that you should go ahead and check and run these codes 
once more with the bigger dataset. It might take some time but you will find out more nuances.'
Q29: Normalization removes all negative values by binding the range to 0 and 1, effectively treating them as 
dissimilar but not inversely correlated.
At 13:41.710, the lecturer states, 'So, if you see this, you will see that all the values have changed, there is no negative 
value anymore.' This means an initially negative correlation (e.g., -0.5) would become a value closer to 0 after 
normalization, indicating low similarity rather than inverse behavior.
Q30: Sorting the scores for user `i` and selecting the top N (e.g., 5) highest-scoring movies.
At 29:59.029, the lecturer describes sorting the row for user `i` to identify the highest scores: 'Now, if I sort it up, this is 
how it looks like... I will recommend? These five basically I will recommend, the last five.'
Q31: The historical purchase data (0 or 1) of the `l`-th similar user for movie `j`.
At 21:57.220, the lecturer explains, 'h is the historical purchase data of this lth person... whether these guys have seen 
this j plus 1th movie or not.' And at 24:39.090, 'I will get this data from my data dot Germany, see data dot Germany l, l 
is the IDs, customer IDs, l row number, and j plus 1 column number.'
Q32: Recommendations would become less diverse and potentially too narrow.
Considering only one neighbor would limit the 'collective intelligence' aspect of collaborative filtering, making the 
recommendations heavily dependent on that single user's preferences, thus reducing diversity. The goal of 'top 5' is to 
capture a broader neighborhood of tastes.
Q33: 12
`rowSum` is the summation of all '1's (items seen) in a row. So 5 movies + 7 songs = 12 items seen.
Q34: Correlation between user `i` and user `i` itself (which is always 1).
The diagonal elements of a correlation matrix represent the correlation of a variable with itself, which is always 1 
(perfect correlation). The lecturer states this at 09:57.180.
Q35: It removes the first column of the dataset.
At 08:14.280, the lecturer says, 'transpose of data dot Germany comma minus 1, means first column you drop, first 
column gets dropped, that means this particular column gets dropped.'
Q36: Jaccard index
At 07:44.490, the lecturer lists 'correlation, it can be measured by cosine, it can be also measured by let us say 
Standardized Euclidean distance.' Jaccard index is not mentioned.
Q37: To get the indices of the sorted recommendation scores.
At 30:44.900, the lecturer states, 'So what I have done, I am doing an order, instead of sorting up I am doing an order. 
An order is giving me the basically the position of this lowest to highest and etcetera and I am using that position to find 
out the column names.'
Q38: User X and user Z are more similar than user X and user Y.
A higher normalized similarity score (closer to 1) indicates greater similarity, while a lower score (closer to 0) indicates 
less similarity.
Q39: To ensure that each user has a corresponding row for recommendations and each item a corresponding 
column.
At 14:03.430, the lecturer says, 'So, I am creating a recommendation matrix called r, r is equal to data Germany, why? 
Because then the dimensions remain same.' This structure allows direct mapping of recommendation scores to users 
and items.
Q40: A numeric vector, matrix, or data frame.
At 09:25.940, the lecturer states, 'it is saying that okay, if you give the whole dataset in COR, you can give x whichever, 
x is what? x is a numeric vector, matrix or data frame.'
Q41: Transposing `data.Germany` after dropping the user ID column.
At 08:29.241, after `transpose of data dot Germany comma minus 1`, the lecturer explains what it 'looks like': 'So, the 
column, the movie names came here, and this is the customer guys.' This sets up the data where each row represents 
a user's consumption vector across items.
Q42: Cold start problem (for new users)
The lecture directly addresses this at 03:52.299, stating that collaborative filtering 'will not work when... the user is new... 
you will not get historical data of that person.'
Q43: The user has already seen the movie, or no similar users have seen it.
At 28:29.520, the lecturer explains, 'zeroes mean either nobody who is close to me has seen these movies. Remember 
in the item to item, whether I have seen Shahrukh Khan’s others movies... or I have already seen that particular movie, 
that is why it is coming zero.'
Q44: The similarity scores between the target user and their top 'N' neighbors.
At 26:17.250, the lecturer describes `k` as 'the weightages' or 'similarity scores' and at 28:02.919 states, 'k is basically 
the similarity score'. It is derived from the `a` matrix after finding the top similar users.
Q45: It is a normalization factor to keep the recommendation score within a certain range.
The `sum(k)` in the denominator normalizes the weighted sum of `h*k`, effectively turning it into an average where 
higher similarity scores contribute more to the average. This helps keep the recommendation score bounded (e.g., 
between 0 and 1 if h is 0/1 and k is 0-1).
Q46: Zero.
If none of the similar users (`h`) have seen the movie, then all `h` values will be 0. Thus, `sum(h * k)` will be 0, making 
the recommendation score 0. The lecturer explains this at 28:29.520.
Q47: To assign meaningful user IDs for easier interpretation.
The lecturer explicitly states at 12:08.339, 'just to make sure that I can visualize it properly, I can see it properly. And I 
can understand that.' Using actual user IDs helps identify who is similar to whom.
Q48: 1 to 15
At 15:49.019, the lecturer states, 'for i is equal to 1 to 15, why 1 to 15? Because there are 15 users at this moment.'
Q49: Binary values (0 or 1)
At 02:08.960, the lecturer says, 'these 0’s and 1’s means 0 means that he has not seen the video and 1 means that he 
has seen the video'.
Q50: User IDs are not relevant for calculating item consumption similarity.
Correlation is a statistical measure between numeric variables. User IDs, while numeric in this case, represent 
identifiers, not features contributing to similarity based on consumption patterns. The correlation should be computed 
only on the item consumption data.
Q51: They would effectively be considered dissimilar, and their preferences would contribute minimally after 
normalization.
As explained, normalization binds values to 0-1, meaning negative correlations become small positive values. These 
low values (`k` near 0) would result in a very small contribution to the `sum(h*k)` part of the weighted average, 
effectively making these neighbors less influential in the recommendation.
Q52: Data frames
At 10:41.920, the lecturer says, 'some extra advantage data frame has over matrices, we have discussed about that in a 
different class.'
Q53: 'Reco 1' has a higher calculated recommendation score than 'Reco 5'.
The process involves sorting by recommendation scores from highest to lowest. Therefore, 'Reco 1' would correspond 
to the item with the highest calculated score for that user among the unconsumed items, and 'Reco 5' would be the fifth 
highest.
Q54: All item/movie columns.
At 02:31.640, the lecturer says, 'rowSum of data Germany 2 to 13 has to be greater than 0.' Since column 1 is the user 
ID, columns 2 to 13 represent all the item consumption data.
Q55: To suggest items that users might like but have not yet encountered.
Recommendation engines aim to predict preferences for unknown items. The process explicitly avoids recommending 
items already seen (0 score if `data.Germany[i,j+1] == 1`).
Q56: It would suffer from a severe cold start problem and fail to make meaningful recommendations.
Without historical user data, there are no 'similar users' to find, which is the core of user-based CF. This is a classic cold 
start scenario as discussed at 03:58.390.
Q57: 0.7
Only the neighbor who has seen the movie (`h=1`) contributes to the sum. So, (1 * 0.7) + (0 * 0.6) + (0 * 0.5) + (0 * 0.4) 
+ (0 * 0.3) = 0.7.
Q58: A table (`Reco matrix`) where each row contains the top N recommendations for a specific user.
At 32:03.240, the lecturer says, 'The recommendation table is populated now. The first one is the top five based on the r 
matrix. The second one is the top five based on the second row of r matrix and so on.' This indicates a per-user 
recommendation list.
Q59: Correlation would focus on linear relationship, while cosine would focus on the angle between vectors (co-
occurrence pattern).
Correlation (specifically Pearson) measures linear relationships and can range from -1 to 1. Cosine similarity measures 
the cosine of the angle between two vectors, indicating directional similarity, and is often preferred for sparse binary 
data as it focuses on shared presence (1s) rather than inverse relationships (0s) or absence. The lecture mentions 
correlation is easier to understand, but cosine is more common, hinting at these nuances.
Q60: Because a user is trivially perfectly similar to themselves and cannot inform novel recommendations for 
themselves.
At 19:08.010, the lecturer clarifies, 'Now, I will not take 1 because this is corresponding to himself'. This self-correlation 
is uninformative for finding *other* users to base recommendations on.
Q61: By enhancing customer experience, increasing engagement, and driving sales through personalized 
suggestions.
Recommendation engines are a core tool in marketing analytics for personalization, aiming to improve user satisfaction 
and encourage further purchases by showing relevant items. This directly impacts customer engagement and sales.
Q62: A mix of user-item and item-item correlations, with the user ID column also correlated.
If `COR` is applied to a data frame, it calculates the correlation between all pairs of columns. This would include 
correlations between the user ID column and item columns, and between different item columns. It would not directly 
give a user-user similarity matrix as intended for user-based CF.
Q63: The original row numbers (indices) of the top `k` similar users.
At 20:58.120, the lecturer says, 'l is the position' (referring to the position/index of the similar users in the original 
`data.Germany` after sorting by similarity scores). And at 24:49.419, 'l is the IDs, customer IDs, l row number'.
Q64: They are transformed into small positive values between 0 and 1.
The normalization formula `(x - min_x) / (max_x - min_x)` maps the entire range [min_x, max_x] to [0, 1]. If `min_x` is a 
negative correlation value (e.g., -0.5), then applying this formula would convert it to a small positive value, not 
necessarily 0 or an inverted positive.
Q65: It can be sensitive to items that both users haven't seen, potentially leading to misleading similarity.
Pearson correlation measures linear relationships. For binary data, if two users both haven't seen many items (both 0), 
this contributes positively to their correlation, even if their actual 'tastes' (based on items they *did* see) are very 
different. Cosine similarity often handles sparse binary data better by focusing on co-occurrence of '1's.
Q66: The five items with the highest recommendation scores for that user.
The lecturer explicitly states at 31:23.360, 'And I am taking the top five, because I will recommend only five.' These are 
the items with the highest calculated scores in the `r` matrix for each user, among those they haven't seen.

Answer Key
Q1: recommenderlab
The lecture explicitly states using the 'Recommender lab library' for recommendation engine tasks.
Q2: Sparse dataset/matrix
The speaker mentions converting the MovieLense data to a 'sparse datasets' and later building a 'sparse matrix' from it.
Q3: User ID, Item ID, and Rating
The lecture clarifies that in a sparse matrix, 'i is equal to user ID, j is equal to item ID and x is equal to user rating'.
Q4: It stores only the non-zero (valid) ratings, saving memory and processing time.
The speaker explains that if zeros were put in all blank spaces, it would create a 'huge matrix'. Sparse matrices store 'i, j 
and value' for only the valid elements, which is more efficient for large datasets with many unrated items.
Q5: Cosine similarity
The speaker explicitly states, 'I am creating a similarity ratings method is equal to cosine'.
Q6: Stronger similarity
The speaker explains, 'the more red it is, the more red it is the stronger is the similarity between two people'.
Q7: 80 percent
The lecture states, 'the evaluation parameter is the split method with training data 80 percent rest data with 20 percent'.
Q8: User-Based Collaborative Filtering
The speaker explicitly introduces 'UBCF that means user based collaborative filtering'.
Q9: Normalization
The speaker mentions, 'the parameter is normalized towards the center'.
Q10: RMSE
The lecture details calculating the 'RMSE of this particular model' and then comparing RMSE scores.
Q11: 1.02
The speaker states, 'RMSE is coming 1.02' for the UBCF model.
Q12: Item-Based Collaborative Filtering
The speaker uses 'IBCF' as an abbreviation for Item-Based Collaborative Filtering when discussing the comparison with 
UBCF.
Q13: There are more movies (items) than users in the dataset.
The speaker explains, 'because there are more number of movies and very less number of users so, user to user 
similarity 25 users is okay. But movie to movie similarity you have to take more movies.'
Q14: 350
The speaker initially sets 'k is equal to 350' for IBCF, noting it 'will take some time to do it 350 by 350'.
Q15: User-Based Collaborative Filtering (UBCF)
The speaker states, 'here it is 1.35 [for IBCF] which is higher so, user to user is something that matches better than item 
to item'. UBCF had an RMSE of 1.02.
Q16: To build and evaluate recommendation systems efficiently.
The speaker promotes `recommenderlab` because 'recommender lab is fast, much faster than you running your loop' 
and is used to 'use the recommender lab, recommender lab I am suggesting'.
Q17: The number of known items in the test set to use for prediction (not explicitly explained, but standard for 
'given').
While not explicitly explained in the transcript, `given` in `recommenderlab` when negative refers to the number of 
*randomly selected* items from the test set to be 'given' to the model to predict the remaining items. This is a common 
practice in recommendation system evaluation. The other options are incorrect interpretations.
Q18: Contains explicit user demographics (age, gender)
The speaker describes the MovieLense data as having 'various movies, their years, and etcetera and the genres'. User 
demographics are not mentioned as part of the data used.
Q19: 1,569,152
The speaker explicitly calculates this: '943 into 1664, will create 156915 to this many elements'.
Q20: 99,392
The speaker states, 'There are 99,392 reviews, unique reviews' in contrast to the potential number of elements.
Q21: It is much faster and optimized for these tasks.
The speaker emphasizes that 'recommender lab is fast, much faster than you running your loop'.
Q22: Changing the dataset to a sparse dataset.
The speaker states, 'So, what I am doing here is that the pre processing first so, MovieLense, I am changing it to a 
sparse datasets'.
Q23: To evaluate the accuracy of predictions against actual values.
The speaker mentions 'calculating the prediction accuracy, RMSE' and explains it by 'checking that whatever 
recommendations I am giving and whatever this guy has actually seen are same or not'.
Q24: Create a new dataset from other sources and try to replicate the work.
The speaker advises, 'Best practice is create a new dataset from some other sources from let us say a source that is 
available in Kaggle or you get your own dataset from somewhere or create a small matrix on your own, and then try to 
replicate whatever work...'.
Q25: To reduce the computation time during the live demonstration.
The speaker reduces `k` to 35 for IBCF, saying 'Let us do it for 35 then we can actually see the result so, 35 by 35 so, it 
has to be actually 350. It has to be big, but I am doing it because the time otherwise will be very high.'
Q26: User1 to User943 and Movie1 to Movie1664.
The speaker describes the sparse matrix: 'these are the dimension names you want to, user1 to user 943 and this is 
movie1 to movie1664'.
Q27: A list of 'best movies' recommended for that user.
The speaker shows the output for user 610 and states, 'the best movies are m831, m100, m574, m623 and m1502'.
Q28: Only a small fraction of possible user-item interactions (ratings) are present.
The speaker highlights the massive difference between total possible ratings (1.5 million) and actual unique reviews 
(99,392), indicating 'most of the places of blank'. This directly defines sparsity in this context.
Q29: UBCF finds similar users, while IBCF finds similar items.
UBCF stands for User-Based Collaborative Filtering (comparing users to users), and IBCF stands for Item-Based 
Collaborative Filtering (comparing items to items). The lecture demonstrates this by showing 'similarity of users' for 
UBCF and later specifying 'movie to movie similarity' for IBCF.
Q30: A sparse ratings matrix.
The code `real ratings is equal to new real rating matrix, data is equal to sparse ratings` clearly indicates `real_ratings` 
is populated from the previously created `sparse_ratings` matrix.
Q31: The labels for rows (users) and columns (movies).
The speaker states, 'these are the dimension names you want to, user1 to user 943 and this is movie1 to movie1664', 
identifying the rows as users and columns as movies.
Q32: Significantly longer processing times.
The speaker advises against custom loops, stating `recommenderlab` is 'much faster than you running your loop'.
Q33: To partition the dataset into training and testing subsets.
The speaker describes the 'split method with training data 80 percent rest data with 20 percent', which is standard 
practice for evaluating model generalization.
Q34: Movies recommended by the model as 'best' for the user.
After running the model, the speaker says, 'the best movies are m831, m100, m574, m623 and m1502' for user 610, 
indicating these are model-generated recommendations.
Q35: The model is less accurate (predictions deviate more from actuals).
RMSE measures the average magnitude of the errors. A higher RMSE indicates a larger difference between predicted 
and actual values, implying lower accuracy. This is implicitly understood when the speaker notes '1.35 which is higher 
so, user to user is something that matches better than item to item' (meaning UBCF with 1.02 RMSE was better).
Q36: Be prepared to change code and 'fight a little bit' for data pre-processing.
The speaker says, 'if the data is bad then the code has to be changed and you have to actually fight a little bit to find out 
those codes'.
Q37: An inbuilt dataset in the `recommenderlab` package.
The speaker states, 'The data in this case is inbuilt called MovieLense' and 'MovieLense dataset already available with 
us'.
Q38: 25
For UBCF, the parameter was 'nearest neighbor is 25'.
Q39: User ID, Item ID, and Rating.
The speaker explicitly states, 'where i is equal to user ID, j is equal to item ID and x is equal to user rating'.
Q40: Inefficient storage and processing due to many empty (zero) entries.
The speaker explains that if zeros were put in all blank spaces, it would create a 'huge matrix' which implies inefficiency 
due to the vast number of empty cells.
Q41: Users/items that are identical or perfectly similar.
The speaker states, 'the more red it is, the more red it is the stronger is the similarity between two people'. A solid red 
would indicate maximum similarity, typically self-similarity (a user with themselves) or perfect similarity between two 
distinct entities.
Q42: Unique identifiers for rows and columns.
The dimension names are given as 'user1 to user 943' and 'movie1 to movie1664', serving as unique identifiers for each 
user (row) and movie (column) in the matrix.
Q43: A significant delay in processing.
The speaker commented, 'it will take some time to do it 350 by 350, you might want to probably run it in the background 
because it might take some time'.
Q44: Subtracting the user's average rating from their individual ratings.
While not explicitly defined in the transcript, 'normalized towards the center' (or 'center normalization') in 
recommendation systems refers to adjusting ratings by subtracting the average rating of a user. This helps account for 
individual rating biases. The lecturer mentions it as a parameter for UBCF.
Q45: To prevent overfitting and assess how well the model generalizes to new data.
The speaker explains the evaluation as 'checking that whatever recommendations I am giving and whatever this guy 
has actually seen are same or not' on the 20% test data, which is the standard purpose of a test set in machine 
learning to assess generalization.
Q46: Movie genres and years.
The speaker states, 'MovieLense data has basically again the various movies, their years, and etcetera and the genres, 
remember this is something that we have created in the last video and MovieLense is the matrix which is like looks like 
this so, this is something that is there in MovieLense dataset already available with us.'
Q47: Significant memory allocation and computational overhead.
The speaker mentions, 'if I put zeros there and etcetera that will create a huge matrix, that much matrix, the large matrix 
I might not need because so, all I would rather know that okay, this is a matrix...'. This implies that storing all zeros leads 
to inefficiency in memory and computation.
Q48: The transcript doesn't state an initial 'k' other than 25 for UBCF.
The speaker only mentions `nearest neighbor is 25` for UBCF. The change in `k` was for IBCF (from 350 to 35).
Q49: Generating recommendations for a specific user.
The speaker introduces this by saying, 'And the moral obligation is what? So, let us see for 6, item number 610 I will be 
creating that whether somebody should see it or not.' This leads to generating recommendations for user 610.
Q50: To modify and adapt the code, potentially involving significant effort.
The speaker advises, 'if the data is bad then the code has to be changed and you have to actually fight a little bit to find 
out those codes.'
Q51: 1.35
The speaker states, 'So, here it is 1.35 which is higher' (referring to IBCF's RMSE compared to UBCF's 1.02).
Q52: It optimizes the data for mathematical operations and storage in matrices.
The speaker states, 'Now, I am changing user to numeric and item to numeric data so both are numeric, fair enough.' 
This is a standard practice for preparing data for computational models, which primarily work with numerical inputs, 
especially when constructing matrices or performing distance/similarity calculations.

Answer Key
Q1: Recency
'R' in RFM stands for Recency, which measures how recently a customer made a purchase.
Q2: Frequency
'F' in RFM stands for Frequency, indicating how often a customer makes purchases within a given period.
Q3: Monetary
'M' in RFM stands for Monetary, which represents the total expenditure a customer has made.
Q4: A profitable customer
A high RFM score indicates a customer who has purchased recently, frequently, and spent a significant amount, making 
them a profitable customer.
Q5: They are a customer who needs to be reactivated.
A low RFM score suggests the customer hasn't purchased recently, infrequently, or spent little, implying they might be 
dormant and need reactivation.
Q6: read.table()
The lecture explicitly uses `read.table()` to load data from a URL where the data is formatted like a notepad file.
Q7: header
The `header = TRUE` argument in `read.table()` tells R that the first row of the data contains the variable names.
Q8: Integer
The transcript states that checking the structure of the dataset showed the date column as an integer before conversion.
Q9: Convert it to `as.character()`
The lecture explains converting the integer column to character first (`as.character(DF2)`) before converting to date 
using `as.Date()`.
Q10: %Y
The lecture specifies that `%Y` is for a four-digit year (e.g., 1997), while `%y` is for a two-digit year (e.g., 97).
Q11: %m
As explained, `%m` is used for the numeric representation of the month.
Q12: %B
The lecture clarifies that `%B` is for the full month name (e.g., January), while `%b` is for the abbreviated form (e.g., 
Jan).
Q13: Removing duplicate data
The lecturer explicitly states, "I will remove the duplicate data first using the duplicated function."
Q14: System date - Purchase date
The lecture describes `UID$timedist = sys.date - UID$date`, meaning the current system date minus the customer's 
purchase date.
Q15: More preferred
A lower `timedist` means the purchase was more recent, which is more preferred for recency.
Q16: More preferred
Higher frequency (more purchases) and higher monetary value (more spending) are always preferred.
Q17: tidyverse
The lecture introduces and installs the `tidyverse` library, noting that `dplyr` (which contains `mutate` and `ntile`) is part 
of it.
Q18: To break a numeric column into equal-sized groups (deciles, quartiles)
The lecture states `ntile` "breaks a numeric column into deciles or quartiles or n number of tiles basically."
Q19: 1 to 10
The lecture explicitly demonstrates and discusses that `ntile(..., 10)` returns values from 1 to 10, not 0 to 9, which 
necessitates `ntile - 1` or `10 - ntile` adjustments.
Q20: `10 - ntile`
The lecture demonstrates `recency = 10 - ntile(time_distance, 10)`. This flips the scoring so that the lowest `timedist` 
(ntile 1) gets a score of 9 (10-1), and the highest `timedist` (ntile 10) gets a score of 0 (10-10).
Q21: `ntile - 1`
The lecture shows `frequency = ntile(number_of_purchases, 10) - 1` and `monetary = ntile(dollar_value, 10) - 1`. This 
converts the 1-10 range to 0-9, where higher values (higher ntile) correctly map to higher scores.
Q22: To add new columns or modify existing ones in a dataframe
The lecturer states, "mutate means it will change the data set and add certain columns on that."
Q23: 100*R + 10*F + M
The combined RFM score is explicitly calculated as `100 * recency + 10 * frequency + monetary`, giving more weight to 
recency, then frequency, then monetary.
Q24: To emphasize the recency of purchase as a primary indicator of customer engagement.
Giving recency a higher multiplier prioritizes recent engagement, making customers who purchased recently stand out 
more in the combined score.
Q25: They were highly skewed.
The lecturer points out that the histograms for dollar value and number of CDs (purchases) were "very skewed," unlike 
the 'time distance' distribution.
Q26: `ntile` forces an equal number of people into each group, which may not reflect the underlying 
distribution; use custom intervals instead.
The lecture explains that `ntile` forces an equal number of observations into each decile, which can distort the score 
representation if the original data is highly skewed. Custom intervals allow for more meaningful breakpoints based on 
the actual distribution.
Q27: It allows for a more even distribution of scores across groups, reflecting the original data's actual spread.
By manually defining intervals (e.g., 0-10, 10-30, etc.), the scores better reflect the non-uniform distribution of the 
underlying data, leading to a "better distribution of monetary."
Q28: Behavioral segmentation of customers
The lecture states, "this is an analysis technique which is used to do behavioral segmentation of customers based on 
their purchase data."
Q29: Customer ID
The lecture explicitly mentions, "I have got customer ID, the date, the date is given as..."
Q30: Recency score is 0
Since the score is 54, and the multiplier for Recency is 100, if R were 1 or more, the score would be 100 or higher. A 
score of 54 implies Recency is 0, leaving 5 for Frequency and 4 for Monetary (10*5 + 4 = 54).
Q31: sys.date()
The lecture explicitly uses `sys.date()` to get the current date of recording.
Q32: RStudio
Although not explicitly stated as 'RStudio', the interface shown with code and output is characteristic of RStudio, the 
common IDE for R.
Q33: In the NPTEL portal
The lecturer says, "here in week 8s 2 dotR file, if you open so, you will find out it is in the in your in your file link in the in 
the in the in NPTEL portal."
Q34: Around 23,000
The lecture states, "Now I have 23,570 observations of 4 variables," after duplicate removal.
Q35: str()
The lecturer uses `str(df)` to check the data types and structure of the dataframe.
Q36: %d-%m-%Y
This format string matches the day-month-year order with hyphens as separators.
Q37: Breaking them into intervals
The lecture explicitly demonstrates creating scores by defining specific intervals for the dollar value (e.g., "Below 10 it is 
1, 10 to 30 it is 2").
Q38: Excel and SQL
The lecturer mentions that RFM analysis is often done in Excel or SQL, especially when dealing with databases.
Q39: 0
`1 - 1 = 0`. This is used for Frequency and Monetary to get scores from 0-9 where higher raw values get higher scores.
Q40: 9
`10 - 1 = 9`. This is used for Recency to ensure that the most recent purchases (lowest `timedist`, highest `ntile` in 
reverse order for recency) get the highest score.
Q41: The one with 10 days
Lower `timedist` means more recent. The `10 - ntile` transformation ensures that more recent customers (lower 
`timedist`) get higher recency scores.
Q42: The internal structure, including class and data types of columns
`str()` (structure) shows the internal structure of an R object, including the class of each column.
Q43: To identify and mark duplicate rows in the dataset
The `duplicated()` function returns a logical vector indicating which rows are duplicates. This is then used to filter them 
out.
Q44: summary()
The lecturer uses `summary(UID)` to inspect the range of the newly created RFM scores.
Q45: Histogram
The lecturer specifically discusses and displays histograms to assess the distribution of the variables.
Q46: Marketing Analytics
The lecture begins with, "Hello everybody, welcome to Marketing Analytics course".
Q47: Days
When you subtract two date objects in R, the result is typically a `difftime` object, which by default represents the 
difference in days. The numerical values shown (e.g., 8427, 8416) further confirm this.
Q48: The specific business context and the raw data's distribution
The lecturer notes that for highly skewed data, it might not be a good idea to break into 10 equal deciles and suggests 
custom intervals based on the histogram's shape ("based on this picture that I got"), implying a data-driven and context-
specific approach.
Q49: Low Recency, Low Frequency, Low Monetary
These customers are defined as having a low RFM score, indicating they are dormant and need reactivation.
Q50: Doctor Swagato Chatterjee
The lecture starts with, "Hello everybody, welcome to Marketing Analytics course, this is Doctor Swagato Chatterjee".
