   (B) B) It generates hypotheses that need to be tested by predictive analysis.
   (C) C) It replaces the need for recommendation engines.
   (D) D) It offers a complete picture without further investigation.
Q23. One limitation mentioned is the exponential growth of complexity. What factors contribute to 
this exponential growth?
   (A) A) Increasing number of customers and sales staff.
   (B) B) Increasing number of products and transaction rows.
   (C) C) Increasing marketing budget and advertisement channels.
   (D) D) Increasing delivery network and store locations.
Q24. What is one of the key benefits of Market Basket Analysis mentioned in the lecture?
   (A) A) High predictive accuracy.
   (B) B) Simple computation.
   (C) C) Eliminates the need for data collection.
   (D) D) Provides instant, real-time recommendations.
Q25. The lecture states that MBA can be 'undirected.' What does this mean?
   (A) A) It lacks clear objectives.
   (B) B) It does not require prior hypotheses before analysis.
   (C) C) It provides results that are difficult to interpret.
   (D) D) It cannot be used for targeted marketing.
Q26. In the rule 'If a basket contains apple and cheese, then it also contains beer,' what is the 'if' part 
called in terms of measuring its occurrence?
   (A) A) Confidence
   (B) B) Lift
   (C) C) Coverage or Support
   (D) D) Accuracy
Q27. For the rule 'If a basket contains beer and cheese, then it also contains honey,' if 2 out of 20 
records contain both beer and cheese, what is the coverage?
   (A) A) 20%
   (B) B) 10%
   (C) C) 50%
   (D) D) 2%
Q28. What does 'confidence' measure in the context of an association rule 'If A, then B'?
   (A) A) The probability of A occurring.
   (B) B) The probability of B occurring given that A has occurred.
   (C) C) The total number of times A and B occur together.
   (D) D) The likelihood of A and B being independent.
Q29. If, for the rule 'If a basket contains beer and cheese, then it also contains honey,' 2 out of 20 
records contain beer and cheese, and only 1 of those 2 also contains honey, what is the confidence?
   (A) A) 10%
   (B) B) 20%
   (C) C) 50%
   (D) D) 1%
Q30. What characteristic makes a rule 'interesting' according to the lecture's statistical definition?
   (A) A) It has very high confidence.
   (B) B) It happens significantly more than you would expect by chance.
   (C) C) It involves expensive products.
   (D) D) It has a very low coverage.
Q31. If bread is in 10% of baskets and washing powder in 4% of baskets, and they are independent, 
what percentage of baskets would be expected to contain both?
   (A) A) 14%
   (B) B) 40%
   (C) C) 0.4%
   (D) D) 4%
Q32. The lecture describes a situation where 4 in 1000 baskets are expected to contain both bread 
and washing powder, but 20 in 1000 actually do. What does this indicate?
   (A) A) The products are completely unrelated.
   (B) B) There's a surprising connection between bread and washing powder purchases.
   (C) C) The data is flawed.
   (D) D) Customers are buying based on promotions.
Q33. Why is it important for a 'surprising' rule to also have a 'suitable level of coverage'?
   (A) A) To ensure the rule is computationally simple.
   (B) B) To confirm the rule is actionable for marketing.
   (C) C) To avoid it being dismissed as a statistical blip.
   (D) D) To increase the confidence of the rule.
Q34. What problem arises when trying to find all possible 'surprising rules' in a large database (e.g., 
20 million transactions, 1000 products)?
   (A) A) There are too few rules to analyze.
   (B) B) The rules are all obvious.
   (C) C) The number of distinct possible rules can be in the hundreds of millions.
   (D) D) The rules change too frequently to be useful.
Q35. What is the main purpose of the Apriori algorithm in Market Basket Analysis?
   (A) A) To predict future sales volumes.
   (B) B) To efficiently find interesting association rules.
   (C) C) To classify customers into demographic segments.
   (D) D) To determine optimal product pricing.
Q36. The lecture mentions that Apriori is 'simple, fast and very good.' What specific type of data is it 
particularly effective for?
   (A) A) Sequential purchase data
   (B) B) Customer demographic data
   (C) C) Basket or other transaction data
   (D) D) Time-series data
Q37. When using Apriori, what concept is focused on instead of directly talking about rules for 
efficiency?
   (A) A) Customer segments
   (B) B) Product categories
   (C) C) Item sets
   (D) D) Transaction volumes
Q38. What is the relationship between the 'coverage of a rule' and the 'support of an item set' as 
stated in the lecture?
   (A) A) They are inversely related.
   (B) B) They are unrelated concepts.
   (C) C) The coverage of a rule is the same as the support of that item set.
   (D) D) Coverage is only for item sets, support is only for rules.
Q39. Which statement best describes the inherent nature of the Apriori algorithm as presented?
   (A) A) It is a complex, cutting-edge machine learning model.
   (B) B) It is a fundamental, efficient algorithm for finding patterns in transaction data.
   (C) C) It is primarily used for predictive modeling.
   (D) D) It requires advanced statistical knowledge to implement.
Q40. Consider a 'home handyman' purchase profile. Which of the following product combinations 
would most likely be found together in a basket?
   (A) A) Nail polish, cologne, hair dye.
   (B) B) Hammer, nails, saw.
   (C) C) Paracetamol, digestive items, pain relief.
   (D) D) Steak, potatoes, beer.
Q41. If a retail store discovers that customers buying 'bread' frequently also buy 'butter' based on 
MBA, and they want to increase sales of other dairy products, what strategy could they employ?
   (A) A) Place bread and butter at opposite ends of the store.
   (B) B) Offer a discount only on butter.
   (C) C) Place new dairy products near bread and butter.
   (D) D) Stop selling bread and butter.
Q42. Why might the purchasing pattern 'milk and soda' occurring together be considered 'noise' or 
require further investigation, as per the lecture?
   (A) A) They are too expensive to buy together.
   (B) B) There's no obvious logical connection for simultaneous purchase.
   (C) C) They are usually consumed at different times of day.
   (D) D) One is a liquid and the other is a solid.
Q43. The lecturer uses the analogy of 'puppy' data analysis in the classroom versus 'dinosaurs' in 
corporate. What does this analogy highlight about real-world MBA?
   (A) A) Real-world problems are simpler than academic ones.
   (B) B) Corporate MBA only uses animal-related products.
   (C) C) Real-world MBA involves significantly larger and more complex datasets.
   (D) D) Academic MBA is outdated.
Q44. Which of the following is NOT an advantage of Market Basket Analysis over a recommendation 
engine?
   (A) A) It is simpler.
   (B) B) It is faster.
   (C) C) It is easier to use.
   (D) D) It provides more personalized, individual recommendations.
Q45. The lecture describes a scenario where customers buy one item at a time from e-commerce 
platforms with minimal delivery charges. What is the consequence for Market Basket Analysis in 
such a scenario?
   (A) A) MBA becomes more complex due to diverse individual purchases.
   (B) B) MBA is still highly effective as it tracks individual item popularity.
   (C) C) The 'basket' is not created, making traditional MBA less applicable.
   (D) D) It leads to stronger sequential purchase patterns.
Q46. What term did the lecture use to describe the phenomenon when customers buy goods based 
on a sudden decision at the store rather than a pre-planned list?
   (A) A) Strategic buying
   (B) B) Impulse buying
   (C) C) Rational buying
   (D) D) Loyalty buying
Q47. Why might seasonal factors (e.g., specific offers, weather) influence the effectiveness of Market 
Basket Analysis if not accounted for?
   (A) A) They increase the computational complexity.
   (B) B) They make data collection more difficult.
   (C) C) They can alter transaction patterns, making older data irrelevant.
   (D) D) They reduce the number of available products.
Q48. The lecture mentions 'Affinity Positioning' as an application. Which example best illustrates this 
concept?
   (A) A) Promoting unrelated products together.
   (B) B) Placing coffee and coffee makers in close proximity.
   (C) C) Offering a discount on a single, popular item.
   (D) D) Analyzing customer demographics.
Q49. If MBA reveals a strong association between a particular type of beverage and a snack, and the 
beverage is a high-margin product, how could a retailer use this information?
   (A) A) Discount the beverage to attract more customers.
   (B) B) Bundle the beverage and snack together at a premium.
   (C) C) Remove the snack to push only the beverage.
   (D) D) Place the beverage in a remote corner of the store.
Q50. What specific type of 'family' purchase profile was mentioned as buying 'illness over the 
counter' products?
   (A) A) Single adult
   (B) B) New family
   (C) C) Elderly couple
   (D) D) Young professionals
Q51. What is meant by 'scanner data' in the context of Market Basket Analysis?
   (A) A) Data from scanning customer loyalty cards.
   (B) B) Data generated when items are scanned at the billing counter.
   (C) C) Data collected by scanning product barcodes for inventory.
   (D) D) Data from scanning market research surveys.
Q52. Which of the following is an example of an 'obvious' association mentioned in the lecture?
   (A) A) Beer and milk
   (B) B) Bread and butter
   (C) C) Milk and soda
   (D) D) Beer and potato chips
Q53. What is the primary difference between how Market Basket Analysis is used in e-commerce 
versus brick-and-mortar stores today, according to the lecture?
   (A) A) E-commerce only uses it for sequential purchases, while brick-and-mortar uses it for simultaneous purchases.
   (B) B) E-commerce is decreasing its use of MBA due to single-item purchases, while brick-and-mortar still finds it very 
relevant.
   (C) C) Brick-and-mortar stores only use MBA for product placement, e-commerce for personalized recommendations.
   (D) D) E-commerce can handle much larger datasets than brick-and-mortar for MBA.
Q54. What happens to the level of 'motivation' or 'engagement' in e-commerce forms compared to 
brick-and-mortar retail stores that can affect Market Basket Analysis?
   (A) A) It is much higher in e-commerce, leading to larger baskets.
   (B) B) It is not so much visible in e-commerce, leading to smaller or single-item baskets.
   (C) C) It is irrelevant to Market Basket Analysis.
   (D) D) It only impacts luxury good purchases.
Q55. The lecture suggests reducing the number of products (columns) in the transaction data matrix. 
How is this achieved?
   (A) A) By analyzing only the most expensive products.
   (B) B) By reducing the number of product categories.
   (C) C) By removing products with low sales volume.
   (D) D) By merging similar product names into one.
Q56. Which type of 'item set' is Apriori designed to find efficiently?
   (A) A) Only item sets with very high prices.
   (B) B) Item sets that include only one product.
   (C) C) Item sets on a specific kind in baskets or other transaction data.
   (D) D) Item sets that are rarely purchased together.
Answer Key
Q1: B) Retail
The lecturer explicitly states at 0:47-0:48, 'Market Basket Analysis is something that is used only in retail'.
Q2: C) Scanner data
The lecture states at 1:00-1:06, 'the data used in this particular analysis is also generated from the scanner data'.
Q3: C) RFM is used broadly, MBA is primarily for retail.
At 0:37-0:48, the lecturer explains, 'RFM analysis... is mainly used in retail but also used in other context. On the other 
hand, Market Basket Analysis is something that is used only in retail'.
Q4: C) To find out what kinds of products go together well.
The lecturer notes at 1:45-1:48 that the analysis aims 'to find out that, what kind of products goes together well'.
Q5: B) Customer ID, loyalty card number, date, time, and counter person's name.
At 1:28-1:39, the lecture states, 'along with your customer ID probably the loyalty card number, etc. the date, time etc. 
the person's name who is there in the counter etc. gets stored in the ERP.'
Q6: C) Transaction data
At 2:08-2:08, the lecture mentions, 'A very common type of data is basket data, which is often also called transaction 
data.'
Q7: C) A transaction ID
The lecturer clarifies at 7:09-7:14, 'each row here is a transaction ID'.
Q8: C) Segment transactions by time (e.g., month/week) and reduce product categories.
At 8:26-9:08, the lecture suggests, 'We generally club it up... we do it for only dairies dairy products, only food products 
or only apparel products and then also transactions which happened in this month only... And then we reduce the 
number of products also the number of columns in that way by reducing the number of categories'.
Q9: B) Customers often buy only one or two items per transaction due to minimal delivery charges.
The lecture explains at 5:45-5:54 that 'where the delivery charges are minimal in those kinds of situations people 
actually buy one item, two item or often they buy one item at a go. So, there the basket is not created.'
Q10: B) By '1' for purchased and 'blank' for not purchased.
At 7:29-7:33, the lecture states, 'blank means that that part, in that particular thing that is not been purchased and one 
means it has been purchased.'
Q11: D) What is the average spending of a customer per month?
The lecture lists the core questions at 9:46-11:16 as 'What items tend to be purchased together?', 'What items or 
purchase sequentially?', and 'What items tend to be purchased by season?' Average spending is not mentioned as a 
primary question for MBA.
Q12: B) Furniture
At 10:24-10:31, the lecturer states, 'if you buy a house then you will buy a furniture obviously, there is a sequence.'
Q13: B) Customer purchase profiles
At 11:27-11:43, the lecture emphasizes identifying 'actionable information' and then explicitly mentions, 'we create 
purchase profiles that is an actionable information'.
Q14: C) By strategically placing associated products in distant areas to increase customer travel and impulse 
buying.
The lecture gives an example (28:30-29:03) of placing 'powder and bread at opposite ends of the supermarket' so the 
customer 'will travel more within the supermarket, the more he travels, the more he buys, so that kind of strategy people 
take to make sure that the revenue goes up.'
Q15: B) Offering product bundles based on 'whoever purchased this also purchased that.'
At 14:11-14:21, the lecturer mentions, 'if you see in Amazon, you are seeing one particular product and they are in the 
bottom it has a bundle is given that whoever purchased this also purchase have that'.
Q16: C) Kids fashion
At 19:53-20:01, the lecturer says, 'For example, kids fashion is a high margin purchase profile 15.24 dollar is something 
that I make up of it from each transaction, so I will push there.'
Q17: B) Telecommunications
At 15:19-15:23, the lecture lists, 'it is also used in telecommunications, it is also used in banks, it is also used in TV 
bundles.'
Q18: B) To cater to customer's personal needs and perceived value for specific product types.
From 13:06-13:35, the lecturer explains that 'people who use to buy crockery which is high end they also buy 
something... which they feel that is good for their hand, good for the product... So they buy a high end, more pricey, 
more quality giving or more value giving product which is let us say the gel version of dishwashing products.' This 
shows tailoring to specific customer needs and perceived value.
Q19: B) Due to a delivery fee for each purchase transaction.
At 4:36-4:46, the lecture states, 'there was a delivery fee and that is why because there was a delivery fee for each item 
sorry for each purchase transaction. That is why what we used to do is that we used to buy lots of products together in a 
basket.'
Q20: C) Personal needs
At 16:12-16:18, the lecturer highlights, 'customer shop on personal needs, not on product grouping, so this is also that 
is a important factor.'
Q21: B) It requires over 18 months for full implementation.
At 21:36-21:37, the lecture states, 'it takes over 18 months to implement that is one of the major.'
Q22: B) It generates hypotheses that need to be tested by predictive analysis.
The lecture states at 21:48-22:11, 'Market Basket Analysis only identifies hypotheses which need to be tested... but this 
hypothesis generation is also important.'
Q23: B) Increasing number of products and transaction rows.
From 22:27-22:39, the lecture explains, 'complexity grows exponentially as the data goes up, because it is again you 
have to deal with the whole matrix. If you have to deal with the whole metrics, is the cost column numbers goes on the 
row numbers goes up, you are in a dilemma, you are increasing the complexity exponentially there'.
Q24: B) Simple computation.
The lecture lists the benefits at 22:43-22:45, starting with 'simple computation.'
Q25: B) It does not require prior hypotheses before analysis.
At 22:51-22:53, the lecturer clarifies, 'Can be undirected that means do not have to have hypothesis before analysis'.
Q26: C) Coverage or Support
The lecture defines at 25:03-25:05, 'coverage or support that means, how much of the database contents the if part?'
Q27: B) 10%
At 25:37-25:43, the lecture calculates, '2 by 20 records contain both beer and cheese... so coverage is 10 percent.'
Q28: B) The probability of B occurring given that A has occurred.
At 24:39-24:45, the lecture defines confidence as, 'how often is then be true. So, given that if is true, how often the then 
is true.'
Q29: C) 50%
At 25:43-25:50, the lecture calculates, 'only out of these 2 only 1 contains honey, so that is why confidence is 50 
percent.'
Q30: B) It happens significantly more than you would expect by chance.
At 26:01-26:04, the lecture states, 'anything that is interesting is something that happens significantly more than you 
would expect by chance.'
Q31: C) 0.4%
At 27:25-27:38, the lecture calculates, 'Ideally that should be further lower 0.1 into 0.04 if they are independent to each 
other, so 0.004. Now, what is we would expect 0.4 percentage of baskets to contain both bread and washing powder in 
case of independence.'
Q32: B) There's a surprising connection between bread and washing powder purchases.
At 28:04-28:24, the lecturer explains, 'if we investigate and discover that 20 in 1000 baskets are actually having bread 
and washing powder together then that is a surprise element. It tells us something is going on consumers mind that 
bread and washing powder are connected in some way'.
Q33: C) To avoid it being dismissed as a statistical blip.
At 29:22-29:33, the lecture states, 'But it also has to be have a suitable level of coverage... or else it may be just a 
statistical blip and not an expert level.'
Q34: C) The number of distinct possible rules can be in the hundreds of millions.
At 29:46-29:57, the lecture highlights, 'there may be around 500 million distinct possible rules, if there are 2 million 
transactions, 20 million transactions of 1000 products'.
Q35: B) To efficiently find interesting association rules.
At 30:34-30:42, the lecture describes Apriori as 'very good at finding interesting rules on a specific kind in baskets or 
other transaction data.'
Q36: C) Basket or other transaction data
At 30:34-30:42, the lecture states Apriori is 'very good at finding interesting rules on a specific kind in baskets or other 
transaction data.'
Q37: C) Item sets
At 30:55-30:57, the lecturer says, 'But note that we will now talk about item sets instead of rules'.
Q38: C) The coverage of a rule is the same as the support of that item set.
At 30:57-31:09, the lecture clarifies, 'the coverage of a rule is same as the support of that item set. So, a coverage 
means, how many times the if part happens, there is also similar to the support of the item set.'
Q39: B) It is a fundamental, efficient algorithm for finding patterns in transaction data.
At 30:31-30:42, the lecture notes, 'There is nothing very special or clever about Apriori, but it is simple, fast and very 
good at finding interesting rules on a specific kind in baskets or other transaction data.'
Q40: B) Hammer, nails, saw.
The lecture lists 'home handyman products' as a purchase profile (18:51-18:55). Products like hammer, nails, and saw 
directly relate to DIY and home repair, fitting this profile.
Q41: C) Place new dairy products near bread and butter.
This is an application of 'Affinity Positioning' and cross-selling (20:47-20:55). By placing related items like new dairy 
products near the strong 'bread and butter' association, the store leverages known purchasing patterns to encourage 
additional sales.
Q42: B) There's no obvious logical connection for simultaneous purchase.
At 17:25-17:38, the lecturer questions, 'milk and soda happening together probably there is no underlying meaning, why 
milk and soda will come together? I do not know... so probably a noise.'
Q43: C) Real-world MBA involves significantly larger and more complex datasets.
From 23:35-23:47, the lecture describes, 'the data analysis that we show in the classroom are basically a puppy and the 
data analysis that you actually do in the corporate are basically dinosaurs or very big monsters'. This directly implies 
greater scale and complexity.
Q44: D) It provides more personalized, individual recommendations.
At 21:23-21:32, the lecture mentions MBA is 'more fast and easy than recommendation engine'. However, 
recommendation engines are typically designed for more personalized, individual recommendations, while MBA 
focuses on group patterns. The lecture also notes limitations of MBA can be handled by recommendation engines.
Q45: C) The 'basket' is not created, making traditional MBA less applicable.
At 5:54-6:12, the lecture states, 'So, there the basket is not created. So, if the basket is not created, then there is 
another way you have to deal with the recommendations or the market baskets, so cannot get a basket there, because 
people do not buy multiple items in one transaction.'
Q46: B) Impulse buying
At 4:08-4:13, the lecturer mentions, 'sometimes you do not purchase and purchase something else, which is not there 
in the list which you purchase based on the impulse buying'.
Q47: C) They can alter transaction patterns, making older data irrelevant.
At 8:44-8:52, the lecture explains, 'month versus month the transaction pattern can change depending on what offers 
are going on, depending on what kind of atmosphere, what kind of season it is, various other things might impact.' This 
means older patterns might not hold.
Q48: B) Placing coffee and coffee makers in close proximity.
At 20:47-20:48, the lecture directly states, 'Affinity Positioning like coffee and coffee makers is close proximity it can be 
put'.
Q49: B) Bundle the beverage and snack together at a premium.
The lecture discusses making 'product bundles' (1:51-1:56) and leveraging the profitability of purchase profiles 
(19:49-20:00). Bundling the associated items, especially when one is high-margin, at a premium would maximize 
revenue.
Q50: B) New family
At 18:05-18:08, the lecture lists 'new family, illness over a counter' products together.
Q51: B) Data generated when items are scanned at the billing counter.
From 1:06-1:14, the lecture defines 'scanner data' as 'The data that is generated when you scan some item in the billing 
counter'.
Q52: B) Bread and butter
At 10:03-10:09, the lecture explicitly states, 'it is bread and butter. These guys are bought together always when you buy 
bread you jam or butter something you buy.'
Q53: B) E-commerce is decreasing its use of MBA due to single-item purchases, while brick-and-mortar still 
finds it very relevant.
At 5:54-6:12 and 6:40-6:53, the lecture describes that with minimal delivery fees, e-commerce customers often buy 
single items, meaning 'the basket is not created,' and that 'ecommerce firm used to use to follow Market Basket 
Analysis quite a bit, but slowly it is going down but still it is an important topic'. In contrast, it's very relevant for brick-and-
mortar.
Q54: B) It is not so much visible in e-commerce, leading to smaller or single-item baskets.
At 6:25-6:37, the lecture states, 'often times the level of motivation level of engagement that you can see in a brick and 
mortar retail store is not so much visible in ecommerce form.' This impacts the formation of multi-item baskets.
Q55: B) By reducing the number of product categories.
At 9:00-9:08, the lecture states, 'And then we reduce the number of products also the number of columns in that way by 
reducing the number of categories'.
Q56: C) Item sets on a specific kind in baskets or other transaction data.
At 30:34-30:42, the lecture mentions Apriori is 'very good at finding interesting rules on a specific kind in baskets or 
other transaction data.'

---
Quiz: Lecture 43 : RFM and Market Basket Analysis (Contd.)
Q1. What is the primary motivation for using the Apriori algorithm instead of simpler Market Basket 
Analysis versions?
   (A) It provides more visually appealing results.
   (B) It can handle larger and more complex datasets efficiently.
   (C) It is easier to implement for novice users.
   (D) It generates a fixed number of rules regardless of data size.
Q2. According to the lecture, what is the first fundamental step in the Apriori algorithm for finding 
association rules?
   (A) Generate all possible combinations of items.
   (B) Identify all items with a specified minimal support.
   (C) Calculate the confidence of all potential rules.
   (D) Create marketing campaigns based on initial item pairs.
Q3. What does a 'k-itemset' refer to in the context of Market Basket Analysis?
   (A) A transaction containing exactly k items.
   (B) A set of k rules generated from the data.
   (C) A set of k distinct items.
   (D) The k-th most frequent item in the dataset.
Q4. In the Apriori algorithm, what does the 'support' of an itemset represent?
   (A) The number of times the itemset appears in a single transaction.
   (B) The percentage of transactions in the dataset that contain the itemset.
   (C) The confidence level of a rule involving the itemset.
   (D) The total revenue generated by items in the itemset.
Q5. What is the role of 'minimum support' in the Apriori algorithm?
   (A) To determine the maximum number of items in an itemset.
   (B) To set a lower bound for the frequency an itemset must meet to be considered significant.
   (C) To define the confidence threshold for association rules.
   (D) To restrict the algorithm to only one-itemsets.
Q6. How is a 'large item set' defined in the context of the Apriori algorithm?
   (A) An itemset that contains many individual items.
   (B) An itemset whose support is at least the specified minimum support.
   (C) An itemset that appears in the majority of transactions.
   (D) An itemset that has a high confidence value.
Q7. What is the primary difference between L_k and C_k in the Apriori algorithm?
   (A) L_k represents 1-itemsets, while C_k represents k-itemsets.
   (B) L_k is the set of *actual* large k-itemsets, while C_k is a set of *candidate* k-itemsets that *might* be large.
   (C) L_k is the set of all k-itemsets, while C_k is a subset.
   (D) L_k is used for generating rules, C_k is used for pruning.
Q8. Which of the following best describes the Apriori principle (or Apriori property) as explained in 
the lecture?
   (A) All subsets of a large itemset must also be large.
   (B) If an itemset is not large, then none of its supersets can be large.
   (C) Large itemsets must always contain more than two items.
   (D) The support of an itemset is always higher than the support of its subsets.
Q9. If a database contains 20 million transactions and the minimum support is set to 0.01%, how 
many times must an itemset occur to be considered 'large'?
   (A) 200 times
   (B) 2,000 times
   (C) 20,000 times
   (D) 200,000 times
Q10. What is the very first step in the iterative process of the Apriori algorithm?
   (A) Find all large k-itemsets (L_k) for k=2.
   (B) Generate all candidate 1-itemsets (C_1).
   (C) Find all large one-itemsets (L_1).
   (D) Calculate the support for all possible item combinations.
Q11. The Apriori algorithm proceeds iteratively. What condition must be met for the algorithm to 
continue increasing 'k' (the size of the itemset)?
   (A) The confidence of the rules generated from L_k-1 is above a threshold.
   (B) The set of large (k-1)-itemsets (L_k-1) is non-empty.
   (C) The number of candidate k-itemsets (C_k) exceeds a certain value.
   (D) All k-itemsets have a support of 100%.
Q12. If an item 'b' occurs 6 times out of 20 transactions (30% support) and the minimum support is 
10%, what can be said about 'b'?
   (A) 'b' is a small itemset.
   (B) 'b' is a large itemset.
   (C) 'b' cannot be used in further analysis.
   (D) Its support is insufficient for rule generation.
Q13. Which statement accurately describes the relationship between the support of an itemset and 
its subsets, according to the Apriori principle?
   (A) The support of a subset is always less than or equal to the support of its superset.
   (B) The support of an itemset is always less than or equal to the support of any of its subsets.
   (C) The support of an itemset and its subsets are always equal.
   (D) The support of an itemset is independent of the support of its subsets.
Q14. When generating candidate (k+1)-itemsets from k-itemsets, what specific method is suggested 
to manage complexity and reduce candidates?
   (A) Generating all possible combinations and then filtering.
   (B) Combining k-itemsets that differ only in their last item, arranged lexicographically.
   (C) Randomly selecting k-itemsets to form (k+1)-itemsets.
   (D) Only considering k-itemsets with 100% support.
Q15. Why is the pruning step important in the Apriori algorithm, even with advanced candidate 
generation methods?
   (A) To add more itemsets for further analysis.
   (B) Because the generation method still produces some candidates that do not have enough support.
   (C) To increase the overall execution time of the algorithm.
   (D) To convert itemsets into association rules directly.
Q16. What happens if the set of large (k-1)-itemsets (L_k-1) is found to be empty during the Apriori 
algorithm's iteration?
   (A) The algorithm proceeds to generate k-itemsets anyway.
   (B) The algorithm stops, as no larger itemsets can be generated.
   (C) The minimum support threshold is automatically adjusted.
   (D) All previous L sets are discarded.
Q17. What does C_k specifically represent in the Apriori algorithm's notation?
   (A) The final set of large k-itemsets.
   (B) A set of k-itemsets that are confirmed to be frequent.
   (C) A set of candidate k-itemsets that *might* be frequent.
   (D) The count of all k-itemsets in the database.
Q18. If an itemset {A, B} has a support of 8% and the minimum support threshold is 10%, what can be 
concluded about any itemset containing both A and B, such as {A, B, C}?
   (A) {A, B, C} will have a support greater than 10%.
   (B) {A, B, C} will have a support of exactly 8%.
   (C) {A, B, C} cannot be a large itemset.
   (D) {A, B, C} will definitely be a large itemset.
Q19. The lecture notes that after 'stage one,' possibilities are 'considerably narrowed down.' What is 
the primary benefit of this narrowing?
   (A) It allows for manual inspection of all remaining itemsets.
   (B) It enables reasonably fast processing on large itemsets to generate candidate rules.
   (C) It guarantees that all generated rules will have 100% confidence.
   (D) It eliminates the need for any further computation.
Q20. What is the definition of an 'itemset' according to the lecture?
   (A) A specific product category.
   (B) A single item within a transaction.
   (C) A specific set of items that may occur together.
   (D) A list of all items sold in a store.
Q21. In the example where 'apples and cheese' is an itemset and 'honey' also occurs with them, what 
would 'apples and cheese' be considered as a part of the rule 'apples and cheese -> honey'?
   (A) The consequent.
   (B) The confidence.
   (C) The antecedent (or itemset for the rule).
   (D) The support count.
Q22. If item 2 occurs only 5% of the time, and the minimum support is 10%, what can be said about 
any itemset containing item 2, such as {2, 4}?
   (A) It must also have 5% support.
   (B) It can have more than 5% support.
   (C) It cannot be a large itemset.
   (D) It will have 10% support.
Q23. What is the main objective of the Apriori algorithm as stated in the lecture?
   (A) To find all possible item combinations in a database.
   (B) To find all large itemsets efficiently in big databases.
   (C) To predict future purchasing behavior directly.
   (D) To calculate the total sales volume of all products.
Q24. The Apriori algorithm's iterative process is described as finding L_1, then L_2, and so on. What 
condition terminates this loop?
   (A) When the support of k-itemsets becomes 0.
   (B) When L_k-1 (the set of large (k-1)-itemsets) becomes empty.
   (C) When the number of itemsets exceeds a predefined limit.
   (D) After a fixed number of iterations, regardless of L_k-1.
Q25. What does the phrase 'you do not create a rule, you just break based on certain conditions' 
refer to in the initial stages of Apriori?
   (A) It means that rules are only generated at the very end of the process, after frequent itemsets are found.
   (B) It refers to the process of pruning itemsets that do not meet the minimum support threshold.
   (C) It implies that the algorithm is only for generating itemsets, not rules.
   (D) It describes a method for manually removing irrelevant rules.
Q26. If the minimum support is 10%, and a 3-itemset {a, b, e} is NOT found to be a large itemset (i.e., 
its support is < 10%), what can be said about a 4-itemset {a, b, c, e}?
   (A) It could still have 10% support or more.
   (B) It cannot have 10% support or more.
   (C) Its support will be exactly the same as {a, b, e}.
   (D) The algorithm will prioritize checking {a, b, c, e} first.
Q27. What is the purpose of arranging itemsets lexicographically when generating candidate k+1 
itemsets?
   (A) To make the output easier for humans to read.
   (B) To ensure that all possible combinations are generated efficiently and systematically.
   (C) To prioritize items that appear more frequently.
   (D) It has no bearing on the algorithm's efficiency, only on presentation.
Q28. According to the lecture, for an itemset (k+1) to be a 'large itemset', what must be true about its 
k-itemset subsets?
   (A) All of its k-itemset subsets must also be 'large itemsets'.
   (B) Only one of its k-itemset subsets must be a 'large itemset'.
   (C) None of its k-itemset subsets can be 'large itemsets'.
   (D) The k-itemset subsets are irrelevant to its 'largeness'.
Q29. Which type of data is the Apriori algorithm particularly suited for in terms of implementation?
   (A) Unstructured text data.
   (B) Image and video data.
   (C) Standard and relational style transactional data.
   (D) Real-time sensor data streams.
Q30. In the example of itemsets {1,3}, {1,5}, and {3,5}, if {1,5} has 9% support and minimum support is 
10%, what can be concluded about the candidate {1,3,5}?
   (A) It must have support greater than 10%.
   (B) It cannot be a large itemset.
   (C) Its support will be exactly 9%.
   (D) It is a strong candidate for a large itemset.
Q31. What is the primary goal of Apriori in the context of Market Basket Analysis?
   (A) To predict the exact next purchase of a customer.
   (B) To identify frequently co-occurring sets of items.
   (C) To calculate the average price of items in a basket.
   (D) To optimize store layout based on item categories.
Q32. If itemset {A, B, C} is a candidate 3-itemset (C_3), but its subset {A, B} was NOT found in L_2 
(large 2-itemsets), what happens to {A, B, C}?
   (A) It is automatically promoted to L_3.
   (B) It is still checked for its support in the database.
   (C) It is immediately pruned from the set of candidates for L_3.
   (D) It is flagged for manual inspection.
Q33. Why is it important to define a minimal support before starting the Apriori algorithm?
   (A) To ensure that only very rare item combinations are analyzed.
   (B) To reduce the computational burden by filtering out infrequent itemsets early.
   (C) To increase the total number of rules generated.
   (D) To guarantee 100% accuracy in the resulting association rules.
Q34. What kind of problem does the Apriori algorithm address that simpler Market Basket Analysis 
versions might struggle with?
   (A) Analyzing only a few hundred transactions.
   (B) Detecting seasonal purchasing patterns.
   (C) The combinatorial explosion of itemset possibilities in large datasets.
   (D) Creating visually intuitive rule representations.
Q35. The term 'candidate' in C_k implies what about the itemsets it contains?
   (A) They have already been confirmed as large itemsets.
   (B) They are potential large itemsets that still need to be verified against minimum support.
   (C) They are itemsets that are guaranteed to form strong association rules.
   (D) They are randomly selected itemsets for initial testing.
Q36. If L_k (the set of large k-itemsets) is empty, what happens to the subsequent generation of 
itemsets?
   (A) The algorithm immediately proceeds to generate L_k+1 by trying new combinations.
   (B) The algorithm halts because no (k+1)-itemsets can be large.
   (C) The minimum support threshold is lowered to find more k-itemsets.
   (D) The algorithm reverts to L_1 to restart the process.
Q37. What is the significance of the example where {a,b,h} has 15% support, and the minimum 
support is 10%?
   (A) It shows that {a,b,h} is not a large itemset.
   (B) It demonstrates that {a,b,h} is considered a large itemset.
   (C) It implies that {a,b,h} cannot form any association rules.
   (D) It illustrates that 15% support is always too low.
Q38. Why is the support of a subset always greater than or equal to the support of its superset?
   (A) Because subsets inherently appear in more transactions than their supersets.
   (B) This is a fundamental property of probability and set theory.
   (C) The Apriori algorithm specifically enforces this rule.
   (D) It's a statistical anomaly observed in retail data.
Q39. When considering combinations for candidate k-itemsets, the algorithm states that it will 'only 
use those products for which one item set was a part of the large category.' What does this refer to?
   (A) Only products that were individual large itemsets will be combined into larger itemsets.
   (B) It refers to including all products, regardless of individual frequency.
   (C) Only products that appear in the first transaction are considered.
   (D) It's a way to randomly select items for combination.
Q40. What would be the most suitable initial minimum support threshold for a very large retail 
database (e.g., 20 million transactions) to manage computational complexity?
   (A) 10 percent
   (B) 5 percent
   (C) 1 percent
   (D) 0.01 percent
Q41. What is the primary outcome of 'stage one' (finding all items with minimal support) in the 
Apriori algorithm?
   (A) A set of final association rules.
   (B) A narrowed-down list of frequent single items.
   (C) The confidence scores for all item pairs.
   (D) A visual representation of market baskets.
Q42. If an itemset {Item A, Item B, Item C} is being considered, and its subset {Item A, Item B} has 
already been pruned because its support was below the minimum threshold, what does the Apriori 
algorithm do with {Item A, Item B, Item C}?
   (A) It calculates the support for {Item A, Item B, Item C} anyway, as it might still be large.
   (B) It immediately discards {Item A, Item B, Item C} without calculating its support.
   (C) It adjusts the minimum support threshold to accommodate {Item A, Item B, Item C}.
   (D) It recursively checks all other subsets of {Item A, Item B, Item C} before deciding.
Q43. Which of the following terms is NOT directly used to define a 'large itemset' in the lecture?
   (A) Minimum support.
   (B) Support percentage.
   (C) Number of occurrences.
   (D) Confidence level.
Q44. The Apriori algorithm primarily focuses on finding:
   (A) Only single-item purchases.
   (B) Association rules with 100% confidence.
   (C) Frequent itemsets.
   (D) Customer lifetime value.
Q45. What is the advantage of generating candidate k+1 itemsets by combining k-itemsets that differ 
only in their last item (lexicographically)?
   (A) It ensures all generated candidates will be large itemsets.
   (B) It simplifies the database structure for easier querying.
   (C) It systematically generates candidates while implicitly respecting the Apriori principle for subset generation.
   (D) It reduces the overall storage requirements for the itemsets.
Q46. If {Item1, Item2, Item3} is a large 3-itemset, what must be true about its 2-itemset subsets 
({Item1, Item2}, {Item1, Item3}, {Item2, Item3})?
   (A) They must all be large 2-itemsets.
   (B) Only one of them needs to be a large 2-itemset.
   (C) They do not need to be large 2-itemsets.
   (D) Their support is irrelevant to the 3-itemset's largeness.
Q47. The lecture implies that Market Basket Analysis rules are ultimately generated from what 
foundational component?
   (A) Customer demographic data.
   (B) Item prices and profit margins.
   (C) Frequently co-occurring itemsets (large itemsets).
   (D) Social media trends and external data.
Q48. Which of the following best describes the role of 'minimal support' in refining candidate 
itemsets?
   (A) It is used to rank itemsets by popularity.
   (B) It acts as a filter to discard itemsets that are not sufficiently frequent.
   (C) It ensures that all itemsets generated have 100% support.
   (D) It determines the maximum size of an itemset.
Q49. What kind of processing does the Apriori algorithm aim to make 'reasonably fast'?
   (A) The initial data loading into memory.
   (B) The generation of candidate rules from large itemsets.
   (C) The calculation of individual item profit margins.
   (D) The real-time prediction of customer churn.
Q50. If you have L_2 (large 2-itemsets) and want to generate candidate 3-itemsets (C_3), which 
combinations would you primarily consider?
   (A) Any three items from the entire product catalog.
   (B) Only combinations where all three 2-itemset subsets are present in L_2.
   (C) Only combinations where at least one 2-itemset subset is present in L_2.
   (D) Randomly selected 3-item combinations.
Q51. What is the direct consequence if a (k-1)-itemset is not found to be 'large' during an iteration of 
the Apriori algorithm?
   (A) It is immediately removed from consideration for forming any k-itemsets.
   (B) It is still used to form k-itemsets, but with lower confidence.
   (C) The algorithm proceeds to check its supersets for support.
   (D) Its support value is increased to meet the minimum threshold.
Q52. The Apriori algorithm uses terminology like L_k and C_k. What does the subscript 'k' primarily 
represent?
   (A) The specific iteration number of the algorithm.
   (B) The minimum support threshold value.
   (C) The number of items in an itemset.
   (D) The total number of transactions in the database.
Q53. The lecture mentions that the generation method 'will still generate some candidates that do not 
have enough support.' What is the purpose of this observation?
   (A) To highlight a flaw in the Apriori algorithm.
   (B) To explain the necessity of a final pruning step to filter invalid candidates.
   (C) To suggest that a higher minimum support should always be used.
   (D) To indicate that these candidates are then manually reviewed.
Q54. Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of the Apriori algorithm as described?
   (A) It is suitable for larger datasets.
   (B) It relies on a minimum support threshold.
   (C) It generates rules directly without finding itemsets first.
   (D) It uses an iterative approach to find frequent itemsets.
Q55. If a market basket analysis using Apriori identifies {milk, bread} as a large itemset, and later 
considers candidate 3-itemsets, which of the following might be a valid candidate to count its 
support?
   (A) {milk, bread, butter}, only if {milk, butter} and {bread, butter} were also large 2-itemsets.
   (B) {milk, bread, butter}, even if {milk, butter} was not a large 2-itemset.
   (C) {milk, eggs, butter}, ignoring the previously found {milk, bread}.
   (D) Only {milk, bread, juice} because 'juice' is a common breakfast item.
Answer Key
Q1: It can handle larger and more complex datasets efficiently.
The transcript explicitly states, 'Now, let us talk about Apriori algorithm which is the advanced version which can be 
used for larger data sets.' (0:34-0:37).
Q2: Identify all items with a specified minimal support.
The lecture states, 'find all items with a specified minimal support. So, first of all you will not do it for everybody, I will 
only find out those guys which have minimal support.' (1:01-1:09).
Q3: A set of k distinct items.
The lecturer defines it: 'For an example, terminology says that there are k itemset let us say, a set of k items. So, it if it is 
a three item set that means a set of three items...' (2:12-2:23).
Q4: The percentage of transactions in the dataset that contain the itemset.
The lecture clarifies: 'But its support an items set has s percentage support that means in the data set, there are s 
percentage transactions, which has these two guys together.' (2:29-2:38).
Q5: To set a lower bound for the frequency an itemset must meet to be considered significant.
The lecture states: 'And what is the minimum support? The Apriori algorithm starts with the specification of a minimum 
support. So, we do not use all itemsets, we only use those itemsets which has a minimum number of 
occurrences.' (2:41-2:57).
Q6: An itemset whose support is at least the specified minimum support.
The lecturer explicitly defines it: 'What is a large item set? Does not mean an itemset, which with many items. It means 
one whose support is at least had the minimum support that is how we use these large itemset definition.' (3:31-3:42).
Q7: L_k is the set of *actual* large k-itemsets, while C_k is a set of *candidate* k-itemsets that *might* be large.
The lecture explains: 'So, L k is the set of all large k item sets in the database. And C k is a set of candidate, so C k is a 
set of candidate large k item sets. In the algorithm we look at it generates this set which contains all k itemsets which 
might be large and then eventually.' (3:42-4:06).
Q8: If an itemset is not large, then none of its supersets can be large.
The core idea is explained through an example: 'if two items are not occurring 10 percent times then three items will not 
occur 10 percent times... if a and b this is not occurring 10 percent times, that means a and b is not a part of the large 
data set, large itemset then a, b, h cannot be a part of the large itemset.' (6:25-6:51). This implies the stated principle.
Q9: 2,000 times
The lecturer calculates this: 'So, 20 million point 01 percent is basically how much? 2000, so 2000 times one items set 
should occur...' (3:16-3:23).
Q10: Find all large one-itemsets (L_1).
The algorithm step is described as: 'it find all large one itemsets that is the first job that means, find out which items are 
occurring at least 10 percent of times if that is your cost of.' (5:59-6:09).
Q11: The set of large (k-1)-itemsets (L_k-1) is non-empty.
The algorithm description states: 'Then for k is equal to 2, while L k minus 1 is non-empty, k plus plus.' (6:13-6:17) and 
later, 'whenever L k minus 1 is non-empty then only you go ahead, otherwise you do not go ahead, otherwise you do 
not increase k.' (10:27-10:37).
Q12: 'b' is a large itemset.
The lecturer gives this example: 'b is occurring 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 is a one item, itemset which is occurring 6 time out of 20 
that means 30 percent, so which is a large item set, so minimums support is 10 percent.' (5:30-5:47).
Q13: The support of an itemset is always less than or equal to the support of any of its subsets.
The lecture explains with examples: 'If 2 has occurred only 5 percent times, then 2 comma 4, can it occur more than 5 
percent times? No, because 2 has occurred... this will be always lower than this, the percentage of this will always be 
lower than this...' (7:51-8:16). Also, 'this guy has to be lower than 9 percent' for 1,3,5 vs 1,5 (9:33-9:50).
Q14: Combining k-itemsets that differ only in their last item, arranged lexicographically.
The lecture advises: 'Only generate k plus one items from k items which differ in the last item, so that is what it is the 
algorithm that they are giving... changing the last item.' (12:31-12:53).
Q15: Because the generation method still produces some candidates that do not have enough support.
The lecture states: 'Will still generate some candidates that do not have enough support, so will still have to check them 
in the pruning step.' (12:59-13:04).
Q16: The algorithm stops, as no larger itemsets can be generated.
The lecture implies this by stating: 'whenever L k minus 1 is non-empty then only you go ahead, otherwise you do not 
go ahead, otherwise you do not increase k.' (10:27-10:37). If L_k-1 is empty, it cannot generate L_k.
Q17: A set of candidate k-itemsets that *might* be frequent.
The lecture explicitly defines C_k: 'And C k is a set of candidate, so C k is a set of candidate large k item sets. In the 
algorithm we look at it generates this set which contains all k itemsets which might be large and then 
eventually.' (3:49-4:06).
Q18: {A, B, C} cannot be a large itemset.
Based on the Apriori principle: 'if a and b this is not occurring 10 percent times, that means a and b is not a part of the 
large data set, large itemset then a, b, h cannot be a part of the large itemset.' (6:42-6:51). Since {A, B} is not large, any 
superset like {A, B, C} cannot be large either.
Q19: It enables reasonably fast processing on large itemsets to generate candidate rules.
The lecture states: 'So, having done stage one, we have considerably narrowed down the possibilities and to can do 
reasonably fast processing on the large itemsets to general candidate rules.' (1:52-2:01).
Q20: A specific set of items that may occur together.
The lecture explains: 'So, item set is just as a specific set of items, like I told when apples and cheese occurred, what 
was the rule if you remember?' (1:11-1:18).
Q21: The antecedent (or itemset for the rule).
The lecture says: 'That apples and cheese occurs, honey also occurs, so apple and cheese is the item set.' (1:18-1:22). 
In an association rule A -> B, A is the antecedent. This question infers from the example.
Q22: It cannot be a large itemset.
The lecture explains: 'Now, if 2 has occurred only 5 percent times, then 2 comma 4, can it occur more than 5 percent 
times? No... So, this will be always lower than this, the percentage of this will always be lower than this, so, I will rather 
will not see any combination which has this.' (7:51-8:19). Since min support is 10%, {2,4} cannot meet it if {2} doesn't.
Q23: To find all large itemsets efficiently in big databases.
The lecture states: 'So, the Apriori algorithm for finding large items item set efficiently in big databases.' (5:47-5:55).
Q24: When L_k-1 (the set of large (k-1)-itemsets) becomes empty.
The algorithm description includes: 'while L k minus 1 is non-empty, k plus plus... So, only for non-empty data sets it is 
saying, whenever L k minus 1 is non-empty then only you go ahead, otherwise you do not go ahead, otherwise you do 
not increase k.' (6:14-6:21, 10:23-10:37). This implies termination when L_k-1 is empty.
Q25: It refers to the process of pruning itemsets that do not meet the minimum support threshold.
The lecture says, 'Now, these use these items is to help generating the rules, so you do not create a rule, you just break 
based on certain conditions.' (1:42-1:52). This breaking refers to filtering out itemsets that don't meet conditions like 
minimum support, before rule generation.
Q26: It cannot have 10% support or more.
The lecture uses this exact example: 'we can easily see that a, b, c, e could not have 10 percent support, because a, b, 
e is not one of our three item sets. See a, b, e was not at all in our three item set, so a, b, e cannot have 10 percent 
support, so then a, b, c, e also cannot have 10 percent support, if a, b, e cannot have 10 percent 
support.' (11:30-11:51). This is a direct application of the Apriori principle.
Q27: To ensure that all possible combinations are generated efficiently and systematically.
The lecture mentions: 'enforce that subsets are always arranged lexicographically and they are already on the left. Only 
generate k plus one items from k items which differ in the last item...' (12:25-12:38). This structured approach helps in 
systematically generating and pruning candidates.
Q28: All of its k-itemset subsets must also be 'large itemsets'.
The lecture emphasizes: 'So, I have to create combinations in such a way such that these guys, so the all the subset of 
the original these combinations are there here, all the possible subsets.' (12:01-12:08). This is the core Apriori principle 
in reverse: if any subset isn't large, the superset can't be large.
Q29: Standard and relational style transactional data.
The lecture explicitly states: 'And it is particularly convenient for implementation in a standard and relational style 
transactional data.' (13:08-13:12).
Q30: It cannot be a large itemset.
The lecture explains: '1, 5 occurs 9 percent, so this guy has to be lower than 9 percent... So, I will practically not 
considered this combination, because this is the combination which is not a large one 10 percent was my cut 
off.' (9:33-9:56). Since its subset {1,5} is not large, {1,3,5} cannot be large.
Q31: To identify frequently co-occurring sets of items.
The entire lecture revolves around finding 'large itemsets' which are frequently co-occurring items, as a precursor to 
generating association rules. The context of Market Basket Analysis implies finding these patterns.
Q32: It is immediately pruned from the set of candidates for L_3.
The Apriori principle dictates that if a subset ({A,B}) is not large, then any superset ({A,B,C}) cannot be large. Therefore, 
it's pruned early without support calculation.
Q33: To reduce the computational burden by filtering out infrequent itemsets early.
'So, first of all you will not do it for everybody, I will only find out those guys which have minimal support.' (1:04-1:09) 
and 'we have considerably narrowed down the possibilities and to can do reasonably fast processing on the large 
itemsets to general candidate rules.' (1:52-2:01) indicates the efficiency gains.
Q34: The combinatorial explosion of itemset possibilities in large datasets.
The lecture introduces Apriori as an 'advanced version which can be used for larger data sets' (0:34-0:37) and focuses 
on how it 'narrows down the possibilities' (1:55), addressing the challenge of scale.
Q35: They are potential large itemsets that still need to be verified against minimum support.
C_k is defined as 'set of candidate large k item sets... which contains all k itemsets which might be large' (3:49-4:03), 
indicating they are awaiting verification.
Q36: The algorithm halts because no (k+1)-itemsets can be large.
The loop condition 'while L k minus 1 is non-empty' (6:14) implies that if L_k becomes empty, the condition L_(k+1)-1 
would also be empty, halting the process.
Q37: It demonstrates that {a,b,h} is considered a large itemset.
The lecture explicitly states: '{a,b,h}... support is 3 by 20, which is 15 percent, here this which is 15 percent. Similarly, a 
comma i is a two item set who support is 0 percent... if I take the cut off as 10 percent then this guy will be a part of my 
large, this guy will be a large item set.' (4:58-5:23).
Q38: This is a fundamental property of probability and set theory.
While the lecture gives examples and uses this property, it's a basic principle: if a transaction contains a set of items, it 
must also contain all subsets of those items. Thus, the count for a subset will always be at least as high as for its 
superset, and consequently, its support will be too.
Q39: Only products that were individual large itemsets will be combined into larger itemsets.
The lecture explains: 'So, you will find further find out item sets for only those guys whose individual items were there in 
the earlier version. That is let us say, when you I am trying to analyse two itemset I will only use those products for 
which one item set was a part of the large category.' (6:51-7:12). This is a direct application of the Apriori principle.
Q40: 0.01 percent
The lecture discusses this for a 20 million database, noting 1% is 'a very big number actually' and suggests '0.01, 0.01 
percent means? ... 2000' occurrences, which is more manageable (3:03-3:23).
Q41: A narrowed-down list of frequent single items.
Stage one involves identifying 'all items with a specified minimal support' (1:01). This filters out infrequent individual 
items, creating the initial 'large 1-itemsets' (L_1) for further processing.
Q42: It immediately discards {Item A, Item B, Item C} without calculating its support.
This is a direct application of the Apriori principle: if a subset is not large, its superset cannot be large. This is the 
pruning mechanism.
Q43: Confidence level.
The lecture defines a large itemset based on its support and minimum support: 'one whose support is at least had the 
minimum support' (3:31-3:42) and 'minimum number of occurrences' (2:51). Confidence is a measure for rules, not 
itemsets themselves.
Q44: Frequent itemsets.
The entire discussion, including 'large itemsets' and 'minimal support', is about identifying itemsets that appear 
frequently together in transactions.
Q45: It systematically generates candidates while implicitly respecting the Apriori principle for subset 
generation.
The lecture says this 'trick guarantees to capture the items that have enough support' (12:53-12:59) and is part of the 
systematic approach to generate candidates from validated frequent subsets.
Q46: They must all be large 2-itemsets.
This is a direct application of the Apriori principle: if a superset is large, all its subsets must also be large.
Q47: Frequently co-occurring itemsets (large itemsets).
The lecture outlines finding 'large itemsets' as the core process before mentioning 'then we try out and find out the rules 
that what kind of rules.' (1:52-2:01, 13:17-13:25).
Q48: It acts as a filter to discard itemsets that are not sufficiently frequent.
The lecturer states: 'first I will choose such kind of combinations, which has a minimum cut off, minimum 
support.' (1:26-1:30) and 'less than 1 percent will not even consider those combination' (1:39-1:42), clearly indicating its 
filtering role.
Q49: The generation of candidate rules from large itemsets.
The lecture states: 'we have considerably narrowed down the possibilities and to can do reasonably fast processing on 
the large itemsets to general candidate rules.' (1:52-2:01).
Q50: Only combinations where all three 2-itemset subsets are present in L_2.
The Apriori principle states that for a (k+1)-itemset to be large, all its k-itemset subsets must be large. Therefore, 
candidates for C_3 are formed by joining two L_2 itemsets that share k-1 items, ensuring their 2-itemset subsets are in 
L_2.
Q51: It is immediately removed from consideration for forming any k-itemsets.
This is the essence of the pruning step based on the Apriori principle. If a subset is not large, its supersets cannot be 
large, so they are not even considered.
Q52: The number of items in an itemset.
The lecture consistently refers to 'k-itemset' as a set of 'k items' (2:12-2:23), and L_k and C_k follow this convention.
Q53: To explain the necessity of a final pruning step to filter invalid candidates.
The lecture directly follows this statement with: 'so will still have to check them in the pruning step.' (12:59-13:04), 
confirming the pruning step's role.
Q54: It generates rules directly without finding itemsets first.
The lecture clarifies: 'you do not create a rule, you just break based on certain conditions' (1:47-1:52) and first finds 
itemsets, then discusses rule generation, indicating it's a two-stage process.
Q55: {milk, bread, butter}, only if {milk, butter} and {bread, butter} were also large 2-itemsets.
This applies the Apriori principle. For {milk, bread, butter} to be a large 3-itemset, all its 2-itemset subsets ({milk, bread}, 
{milk, butter}, {bread, butter}) must also be large. Since {milk, bread} is already large, we only need to ensure the other 
subsets were also large to consider {milk, bread, butter} as a candidate.

Q1. What does 'Support' measure in Market Basket Analysis?
   (A) The probability that a rule is correct.
   (B) The fraction of transactions in which an itemset occurs.
   (C) The ratio by which the confidence of a rule exceeds the expected confidence.
   (D) The total number of items in a transaction.

   (B) Lift
   (C) Confidence
   (D) Frequency
Q3. In Market Basket Analysis, what does 'Lift' represent?

   (B) How many times an item appears in the dataset.
   (C) The ratio by which a rule's confidence exceeds its expected confidence.
   (D) The cost-effectiveness of a marketing campaign.

Q59. Consider the rule `{curd, cereals} -> {whole milk}` with a lift of 3.6. This lift value means that 
buying curd and cereals makes it how many times more likely to buy whole milk, compared to buying 
whole milk normally?
   (A) 0.36 times
   (B) 1 time
   (C) 3.6 times
   (D) 36 times
Q60. What happens if you set a very high `support` threshold (e.g., 0.5) for the `Apriori` algorithm on 
a typical grocery dataset?
   (A) It will generate a very large number of rules.
   (B) It will likely generate very few, if any, rules, as itemsets that frequent are rare.
   (C) It will prioritize rules with high confidence but low lift.
   (D) It will only find rules involving single, extremely popular items.
Answer Key
Q1: The fraction of transactions in which an itemset occurs.
Support is defined as the fraction of transactions where a particular itemset occurs, representing its popularity or 
frequency in the dataset.
Q2: Confidence
Confidence measures the probability that the consequent (C) will be bought given that the antecedent (A and B) has 
already been bought, essentially P(C | A, B).
Q3: The ratio by which a rule's confidence exceeds its expected confidence.
Lift measures how much more likely the consequent is to be bought given the antecedent, compared to its overall 
purchase probability. A higher lift indicates a stronger and more useful rule.
Q4: The rule is more useful and actionable.
The lecturer explicitly states, 'The higher the lift, the better is your rule, the more stronger, more applicable, more 
actionable is your rule.'
Q5: `arules`
The lecture explicitly introduces `arules` (standing for association rules) as the primary library for Market Basket 
Analysis, while `datasets` is mentioned as potentially being for inbuilt data.
Q6: A transaction containing multiple items.
The lecturer states, 'these each of these particular rows is a transaction,' and gives examples of items bought together 
in a single row.
Q7: `whole milk` is already a very popular item on its own.
The lecturer explains that 'Because anyway people buy whole milk, I have shown you 2500 times out of around 10,000 
times at least 40 percent times people anyway by whole milk, almost 40 percent times. So, given that this occurs the 
percentage is 0.91 that is not very high, the lift is 3.6 that means actually, normally people buy whole milk 0.91 divided 
by 3.6 this is the natural probability of buying a whole milk.' Lift accounts for the baseline popularity of the consequent.
Q8: 0.1%
The lecture specifies that `0.001` support means '0.1 percentage times it has to occur'.
Q9: The rule must be correct at least 80% of the time.
The lecturer states, 'And the confidence has to be 0.8 at least. So, at least 80 percent times it has to be correct.'
Q10: 0.25
From the formula, P(C) = P(C | A, B) / Lift. So, P(C) = 0.91 / 3.6 "H 0.2527. The lecturer rounds this to '25 percent times 
people anyway buy whole milk'.
Q11: Support, Confidence, Lift
The lecturer explicitly states, 'So, that is the three things based on which we judge which rule we will act on which will 
discard'.
Q12: `itemFrequencyPlot()`
The lecturer says, 'So, item frequency plot is something that I am creating with top 20.'
Q13: The maximum total number of items (LHS + RHS) in an association rule.
The lecturer explains, 'maxlen is equal to 3 means? The maximum length both side included, right hand and then right 
hand side and left hand side, the length of the rules will be 3 items at max.'
Q14: `appearance = list(rhs = "whole milk")`
The lecture demonstrates setting `appearances = list(lhs = 'default', rhs = 'whole milk')` to achieve this.
Q15: Bundle these products or place them in close proximity.
The lecturer suggests, 'Then I have to put them together or I have to create a bundle or I can put them into different 
ends, so that people can purchase.'
Q16: 0.25
Support is calculated as the fraction of transactions. 2500 / 10000 = 0.25.
Q17: Discover frequent itemsets and association rules.
The `Apriori` algorithm is specifically mentioned and used in the lecture for 'finding out the rules' and generating 
association rules.
Q18: It shows rules in the order they were generated, which might not be based on statistical merit.
The statement 'not based on anything they are randomly created' implies that the initial ordering of rules by `inspect()` 
is arbitrary and doesn't reflect their statistical importance or quality until sorted.
Q19: It demonstrates the rule is not a statistical anomaly or 'a bleep.'
The lecturer emphasizes, 'it has to be supportive that means you the rule is not a bleep, not a statistical error that he 
has enough support for the rule'.
Q20: The items are likely to be bought together anyway, without specific intervention.
A lift of 1 indicates that the purchase of the antecedent items has no effect on the probability of purchasing the 
consequent item. Even with high confidence, if the consequent is frequently bought anyway, the rule might not offer new 
actionable insights.
Q21: `datasets`
The lecturer mentions, 'there are two libraries one is arules and one is datasets that I will use,' although later noting 
`datasets` might be base.
Q22: `summary(rules)`
The lecturer demonstrates this by saying, 'And if I just want to see summary of those rules.'
Q23: `appearance = list(lhs = "whole milk"), minlen = 2`
The lecture demonstrates `appearance = list(lhs="whole milk")` and separately `minlen = 2` for controlling the minimum 
total length of the rule.
Q24: The absolute frequency of the left-hand side itemset appearing in the dataset.
The support value (0.0019) translates to 0.19% of transactions, indicating that the specific combination of items on the 
left-hand side (antecedent) does not occur very often.
Q25: Product discovery and convenience.
Bundling or strategic placement helps customers discover related products they might not have considered, improving 
their shopping convenience and potentially increasing sales of associated items.
Q26: Identifying items frequently purchased together.
The core purpose of Market Basket Analysis is to discover relationships between items that are commonly bought 
together in transactions.
Q27: 10,000
The lecturer states, 'I think I have around 10,000 observations'.
Q28: A and B are independent; buying A does not influence buying B.
A lift of 1 means that the probability of buying B given A is the same as the probability of buying B independently. 
There's no association beyond what's expected by chance.
Q29: `Apriori()`
The lecture clearly states and demonstrates, 'we use the Apriori algorithm with the groceries data set, so the syntax is 
Apriori within bracket data.'
Q30: Confidence
The lecturer says, 'So, I will this thing I will sort them based on confidence. So, rules is, I sort the rules by confidence in 
a decreasing order.'
Q31: Lift, as it shows the strength of association beyond mere popularity.
Lift specifically quantifies how much more likely the consequent is bought due to the antecedent, relative to its baseline 
popularity, making it the best indicator for 'increasing the chances of being bought very high'.
Q32: Whenever the items on the left-hand side are bought, the item(s) on the right-hand side are *always* 
bought.
Confidence of 1 means that in all instances where the antecedent (LHS) occurs, the consequent (RHS) also occurs.
Q33: `support` and `confidence`
Increasing `support` and `confidence` thresholds will filter out less frequent and less accurate rules, respectively, 
resulting in a smaller set of stronger rules.
Q34: Antecedent
The items on the left-hand side of an association rule are called the antecedent, and the items on the right-hand side 
are the consequent.
Q35: Consequent
The item(s) on the right-hand side of the `->` symbol in an association rule represent the consequent.
Q36: Buying X and Y makes it half as likely to buy Z compared to buying Z independently.
A lift value less than 1 indicates a negative correlation. A lift of 0.5 means that the probability of buying Z given X and Y 
is half the probability of buying Z normally.
Q37: Consider bundling rice, sugar, and whole milk or placing them near each other.
Similar to the liquor example, the lecturer suggests that knowing items are bought together should lead to strategies like 
bundling or strategic placement to facilitate purchase.
Q38: `inspect(rules[1:5])`
The lecturer explicitly states, 'So, let us say inspect the top 5 first 5 rules.'
Q39: It can be freely accessed and used for research or practice.
Publicly available data refers to data that can be accessed and used by anyone, often for educational or research 
purposes, without restrictions or payment.
Q40: These rules might not be commercially significant due to their rare occurrence.
Even if a rule is 100% true (confidence=1), if it occurs very rarely (small support), it might not be worth investing 
marketing efforts into, as it wouldn't impact a large segment of customers.
Q41: Analyzing item frequency with `itemFrequencyPlot`.
After loading the data, the lecturer proceeds to 'find out the first thing is to find out the one items which items are more 
prominent' using `itemFrequencyPlot`.
Q42: R
The lecture explicitly refers to R files (`Market Basket analysis.r`) and uses R commands like `install.packages`, 
`library()`, `data()`, `itemFrequencyPlot()`, and `Apriori()`.
Q43: 4 items
The lecture states, 'Four items rules there are 229 such rules', which is the highest count among the rule lengths 
provided (29 for 3 items, 140 for 5 items, 12 for 6 items).
Q44: Lift normalizes confidence by the base probability of the consequent, so a popular consequent naturally 
lowers the lift if the confidence isn't disproportionately higher.
As explained in the lecture with the 'whole milk' example, if an item is bought frequently anyway (high P(C)), then even a 
high conditional probability P(C|A,B) won't result in an exceptionally high lift, because lift = P(C|A,B) / P(C).
Q45: Generate marketing insights and inform decision-making.
The lecturer concludes, 'This part is only a data science to create hypothesis that hypothesis can be tested, can be 
used, once it is tested true to create marketing insights.'
Q46: It's a sample dataset included directly within an R package for demonstration purposes.
In R, 'inbuilt data' typically refers to datasets that come pre-loaded or are part of a package for easy access and use in 
examples or tutorials.
Q47: Rules where the total number of items (LHS + RHS) is less than 2.
The `minlen` parameter specifies the minimum total number of items in a rule (antecedent + consequent). Setting it to 2 
would exclude single-item rules or rules that combine to less than two items (though typically rules have at least one 
LHS and one RHS item).
Q48: Conducting A/B testing with different product placements or bundles.
A/B testing is a common experimental method in retail to test the effectiveness of changes (like product placement or 
bundling suggested by MBA) on actual customer behavior.
Q49: Sorted rules prioritize accuracy (confidence), while default rules show an arbitrary order.
The lecturer explains that the default rules are 'not based on anything they are randomly created' but that sorting by 
confidence allows identification of 'most accurate' rules.
Q50: To perform association rule mining.
The package name `arules` stands for 'association rules', and its explicit purpose in the lecture is to 'find out the rules' 
and perform Market Basket Analysis.
Q51: Left-Hand Side (of a rule)
The lecturer frequently refers to the 'left-hand side' and 'right-hand side' of association rules, denoting the antecedent 
and consequent, respectively.
Q52: 2,500 times
The lecturer states, 'I have around 10,000 observations and out of them 2500 cases whole milk is occurring'.
Q53: It is a rare rule but highly accurate when it occurs.
A support of 0.001 (0.1%) indicates rarity, while a confidence of 0.9 (90%) indicates high accuracy when the antecedent 
is present.
Q54: To ensure rules are both statistically significant (support) and practically useful (lift).
Support ensures the rule isn't a fluke, while lift measures its practical importance beyond baseline probabilities. 
Combining them helps identify rules that are both reliably frequent enough and offer significant insight.
Q55: Personalizing product recommendations and optimizing store layouts.
MBA helps marketers understand which products are bought together, informing strategies like bundling, cross-selling, 
product placement, and personalized recommendations.
Q56: Whole milk
The lecturer states, 'you can see that whole milk is the most common product'.
Q57: Each rule will contain one or two items (total LHS+RHS).
`minlen` and `maxlen` refer to the total number of items across both the left-hand and right-hand sides of a rule.
Q58: They are tested in real-world scenarios to validate their effectiveness.
The lecturer states, 'This part is only a data science to create hypothesis that hypothesis can be tested, can be used, 
once it is tested true to create marketing insights.'
Q59: 3.6 times
Lift directly measures the factor by which the antecedent increases the probability of purchasing the consequent.
Q60: It will likely generate very few, if any, rules, as itemsets that frequent are rare.
Support of 0.5 means an itemset must appear in 50% of transactions. In a diverse grocery dataset, very few, if any, 
combinations of items would reach such a high frequency, leading to very few or no rules being generated.

===== WEEK 9 =====
Quiz: Lecture 49 : Customer Churn and Customer Lifetime Value 
(Contd.)
Q1. According to the lecture, what is the primary definition of Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)?
   (A) The total amount of revenue a company generates from a customer in a single year.
   (B) The total amount of profit a company can generate from a customer over their entire relationship with the company.
   (C) The cost associated with acquiring a new customer.
   (D) The current market value of a company's customer base.
Q2. The lecture explains that 'lifetime of the customer' for CLV calculations refers to what duration?
   (A) The customer's entire biological lifespan.
   (B) The period during which the customer is actively engaged with the company.
   (C) A fixed period of 5-10 years, regardless of customer activity.
   (D) The duration of the customer's first purchase.

core product to generate CLV, similar to how a hotel chain might?

   (B) A fast-food restaurant
   (C) Maruti car manufacturer
   (D) A software as a service (SaaS) provider

   (A) Building new, uniformly designed luxury hotels globally.
   (B) Acquiring and managing historically significant properties with unique cultural identities.

   (D) Franchising its brand to independent hotel owners worldwide.
