Answer Key
Q1: The total amount of profit a company can generate from a customer over their entire relationship with the 
company.
The lecture clearly defines CLV as 'the total amount of profit that a company can generate, from a customer... over the 
lifetime of the customer' (00:44.230 - 00:54.780).
Q2: The period during which the customer is actively engaged with the company.
The lecture clarifies that 'lifetime of the customer means not his whole till his death but till the customer is there with this 
company' (00:54.780 - 01:03.049).
Q3: Maruti car manufacturer
The speaker uses Maruti cars as an example, detailing how they create a service network including 'DPS services,' 
'driving license services,' 'second hand card selling services,' 'insurance company,' 'fuel company and spare parts' to 
generate value over the customer's lifetime (02:23.709 - 02:53.390).
Q4: Acquiring and managing historically significant properties with unique cultural identities.
Redwoods 'very, very good to do properties at various parts of the world and they acquire them. Now these properties 
have their own history, they have their own culture, they have their own...designs' (03:31.930 - 03:52.879).
Q5: Increasing competition from other luxury hotels not focused on heritage.
Redwoods was 'facing increasing competition. So, there were lots of other hotels which were coming up which were not 
focused on this history part or heritage part' (06:23.240 - 06:36.840).
Q6: Corporate chains had low ADR and high occupancy; individually branded had high ADR and low 
occupancy.
The lecture states, for corporate chains, 'their average daily rate is low... and then the occupancy rates are high.' For 
individually branded hotels, they 'have very high daily rate and their occupancy rates are low' (08:53.730 - 09:11.660).
Q7: Ability to cross-sell properties through loyalty programs.
Corporate chains could use loyalty cards to allow customers to redeem points across different hotels in the chain, 
increasing the probability of a customer staying within the network and reducing churn (09:24.079 - 10:10.779).
Q8: Ritz Carlton
The speaker notes, 'Ritz Carlton was always be the leader' (11:16.820 - 11:19.190) in occupancy rate.
Q9: Rosewood
Referring to the lines on the chart for ADR, the speaker states, 'this blue line is for Rosewood, which is the 
highest' (12:03.819 - 12:10.959).
Q10: Revenue generated per available room, accounting for occupancy.
While the speaker struggles to recall the exact full form, the explanation 'revenue that you generate, per occupancy is 
something that we are checking here' (13:00.470 - 13:06.790) points to RevPAR which is a standard metric in hospitality 
combining ADR and occupancy.
Q11: Individual branding, emphasizing the unique character of each property.
The lecture states, 'Rosewood because it was individually branded house it was focusing on individual branding 
strategy' (13:34.220 - 13:39.769).
Q12: To highlight the unique history and culture of each distinct property.
Under individual branding, there was a 'sense of place' and 'service and product was very much flexible' because the 
local manager knew the 'heritage, the culture the history much more than a brand manager at a group level' (19:00.850 
- 19:27.420).
Q13: Low customer retention rate and limited multi-property customers.
The problem was 'the customers were not loyal... So retention rate was only 17 percent very low multi property 
customer... 5 percent' (19:54.560 - 20:27.930).
Q14: 5 percent
The retention rate was 17%, but 'multi property customer... were very low 5 percent' (20:15.470 - 20:27.930).
Q15: Encourages cross-selling and economies of scale.
Under corporate branding, 'it encourages cross sell, it operation economies of scale is high, marketing costs 
reduce' (21:09.830 - 21:18.170).
Q16: Consistency in service experiences across all properties.
Corporate branding 'needs consistency... similar kind of service experiences everywhere' (21:18.170 - 21:24.430), 
which conflicted with Rosewood's existing model.
Q17: 750 dollars
'Average daily spend with branding without branding both was 750' (22:34.220 - 22:36.250).
Q18: 32 percent
'Average gross margin is 32 percent in both cases' (22:46.690 - 22:51.920).
Q19: 150 dollars
The guest acquisition cost is '150 dollars' (24:06.200 - 24:10.970).
Q20: 2 days
'Average number of stays is two days' (22:44.200 - 22:46.690).
Q21: 1.2
'Average number of visits per year per guest... It is 1.2' (22:51.920 - 22:59.660).
Q22: 130 dollars
'First year acquisition So, no other acquisition costs and first year marketing cost is 0, second years marketing cost is 
130' (25:32.490 - 25:41.580). This implies 130 is the recurring marketing cost per customer.
Q23: 6 percent
'With corporate branding, it was growing at a 6 percent rate' (22:36.250 - 22:41.950). This rate was used for the ADS 
(revenue per customer) in the calculation (24:56.120 - 25:06.430).
Q24: 8 percent
'The discounting rate is 8 percent is what I have considered' (23:38.830 - 23:42.920).
Q25: 16.67 percent
The retention rate is '16.67 percent' (26:21.100 - 26:33.220), which was also mentioned as '17 percent' earlier 
(19:56.650).
Q26: Increased from 1.2 to 1.3.
The text states, 'average number of visits per year per guest because cost branding will increase a little bit of number of 
visits. It is 1.2, it has gone up to 1.3' (22:51.920 - 22:59.660).
Q27: 1 million dollars
'The additional marketing cost is almost 10 million dollar, 1 million dollar' (23:29.740 - 23:34.060), with 1 million being 
the correct figure mentioned after correction.
Q28: Increased by 5 percent to 21.67 percent.
'Retention rate also increased by 5 percent as I told So, 21.67 percent' (23:42.920 - 23:48.360).
Q29: It was 1 million dollars divided by the number of unique guests (115,000).
The additional marketing cost was '1 million divided by 115000, these are the number of customers' (28:10.280 - 
28:22.270).
Q30: 345 dollars
The total NPV calculated 'without corporate branding' is '345' (27:54.790 - 27:57.630).
Q31: 401 dollars
The total NPV calculated 'with corporate branding' is '401' (28:50.260 - 28:54.110).
Q32: Corporate branding resulted in a higher CLV, suggesting it was the better strategy.
The additional money generated was 56 dollars per customer (401-345), and the speaker concludes, 'if you are saying 
that your cost of doing the branding is lower than this then you should go for corporate branding. So, that is what we can 
get a generate. So, based on that it is a positive value' (28:54.110 - 29:15.150).
Q33: It increased by approximately 7-8%.
The occupancy rate 'increased from 50 percent or let us say this one is how much 65 percent it increased to 71... So, 
almost 7-8 percent increase in occupancy rate happened' (30:23.700 - 30:47.390).
Q34: It increased from $351 to $429 per night.
ADR 'went up from 351 dollars per night average daily rate to 429 dollars which is very high' (30:59.620 - 31:11.630).
Q35: It was successfully working and showing positive results.
In 2007, 'they could actually say after three years of calculations, they would say that their branding strategy the 
corporate leve,l umbrella branding strategy was working' (32:07.030 - 32:16.090).
Q36: The customer's social media influence.
The lecture focuses on financial aspects like acquisition cost, gross profit, marketing expenses, retention, and 
discounting. Social media influence, while relevant to marketing, is not a direct quantitative component of CLV as 
discussed.
Q37: Limited cross-selling opportunities across properties.
The lecture explains that 'another major problem that individually branded hotels used to face is there is no cross 
selling' (09:17.269 - 09:24.079), leading to customers potentially not staying within the network.
Q38: How to maintain the distinct character of each property while ensuring brand consistency.
The dilemma 'Dilemma with uniqueness and individuality' arises because corporate branding 'needs consistency' but 
Redwoods properties had 'heterogeneous properties' with their own history and culture (21:18.170 - 21:53.840).
Q39: To account for the uncertainty of a customer remaining with the company in future periods.
The 'Probability of retention' reflects the likelihood of a customer staying. Multiplying it with 'Profit if retained' gives the 
anticipated profit, accounting for churn over time (27:05.710 - 27:10.740).
Q40: Customers were unaware of Rosewood, loyal to individual property brands, with awareness primarily 
through travel agents.
Customers were 'Unaware of, they were not knowing about this Rosewood brand. They were loyal to the individual 
brands... and Rosewood brand awareness through the travel agents' (20:27.930 - 20:44.970).
Q41: An experience heavily themed around Rajput history, architecture, and food.
The speaker illustrates that if 'Rajputana is big and that is why it is low... I will actually expect Rajput oriented history. So, 
the expectation will not be services expectation will not be any more the similar kind of stamp' (15:47.949 - 16:07.179).
Q42: It decreases the present value of future profits.
A discount factor represents the time value of money, meaning money received in the future is worth less than money 
received today. Dividing future profits by a factor greater than 1.0 reduces their present value.
Q43: 115,000
'Number of unique guest were around 115000' (22:25.710 - 22:31.730).
Q44: They might lose the local flavor and uniqueness they valued.
Corporate branding 'deters some customers and managers also. Because some customers might want that flavour, the 
local flavour, some managers might be more interested to give the local flavour' (21:57.950 - 22:08.090).
Q45: 5,750 additional customers, of which 10% were multi-property.
'The total number of repeat guests is 5750 number of additional... number of multi property guest is 10 percent of 
this' (23:08.140 - 23:18.680).
Q46: From 5% to 10% of additional customers.
The lecture states it 'was 5 percent that goes up to 10 percent of this [additional 5750 customers]' (23:17.680 - 
23:22.330).
Q47: It reduced a little bit over time as some properties were cancelled and others acquired.
'This is the number of rooms, number of rooms they actually reduced a little bit over time because some of the 
properties they cancel and then then again they acquired some other custom properties' (31:11.630 - 31:22.560).
Q48: 3 percent
The marketing cost of $130 (after year 0) was 'increasing at a rate of 3 percent' (25:41.580 - 25:50.420).
Q49: Whether to change from an individual branding strategy to a corporate branding strategy.
The entire case study revolves around the 'individual branding versus corporate branding issue' and the CLV calculation 
was done to decide 'which one should I grew' (20:53.250 - 22:19.090).
Q50: It accounts for the initial customer acquisition cost.
For year 0, the speaker says 'acquisition cost if you check here it is 150 dollars... that is what is the only cost, there is no 
marketing cost in the first year' (24:06.200 - 24:18.510). This initial outflow makes the profit negative.
Q51: CLV would increase.
A higher retention rate means a customer stays longer, generating profits for more years, thus increasing their lifetime 
value.
Q52: Reduced customer loyalty across properties.
Corporate branding is intended to *increase* customer loyalty and cross-selling across properties, not reduce it 
(21:09.830 - 21:15.840). The other options are listed as challenges (21:18.170 - 22:09.870).
Q53: Luxury segment customers interested in heritage and unique experiences.
Redwoods was 'majorly focusing the luxury segment' and catering to customers who 'want to spend money on this kind 
of a heritage, heritage buildings and heritage stays' (06:23.240 - 06:49.010).
Q54: Costly, flexible, and customized service/product delivery.
Because the local manager had autonomy and flexibility to cater to local heritage, the speaker notes 'because it was 
flexible, it was costly and that is why the margin was low' (19:27.420 - 19:34.450).
Q55: Gross Profit - Marketing Cost.
The 'profit is nothing but the gross profit minus the cost gives me the profit if the customer is retained' (25:57.490 - 
26:11.770).
Q56: Travel agents.
'Rosewood brand awareness through the travel agents' (20:40.960 - 20:44.970).
Q57: 4-8 years
In case of education... 'it can be, let us say 6 years at max 4 years to 6 years, 8 years is the lifespan of the student in an 
educational institute' (01:09.570 - 01:20.259).
Q58: Great Eastern kind of a hotel in Calcutta.
If you 'come to Calcutta to a old school, let us say, let us Great Eastern kind of a hotel which is old hotel, that gives you 
a feeling of the colonial India' (05:01.600 - 05:15.860).
Q59: The rate at which customers switch to another hotel chain.
When discussing corporate chains, the speaker says if the probability of a customer staying within the network is high, 
'the churn comes down' (10:05.110 - 10:11.779), implying churn is customers leaving the network.
Q60: RevPAR
While not explicitly defined by its full name, RevPAR is introduced as a metric related to 'how many occupancy is 
something that they have to calculate' and 'revenue that you generate, per occupancy' (12:54.069 - 13:06.790), linking it 
to both concepts.
Q61: It had 25 years of operation but only 12 properties and 1513 rooms.
It 'has 25 years privately held... it has a small number of properties, 12 properties only they had and there were 1513 
rooms. So not very large chain, small chain and I would say limited number of rooms' (06:05.070 - 06:15.430).
Q62: $400
Expected profit = Profit if retained * Probability of retention = $500 * 0.8 = $400.

---
Quiz: Lecture 50 : Customer Churn and Customer Lifetime Value 
(Contd.)
Q1. Which of the following best defines a 'survival probability curve' in the context of customer 
churn?
   (A) The likelihood that a customer will make a purchase in the next time period.
   (B) The probability that a customer will be 'alive' (i.e., retained) at or beyond a certain point in time.
   (C) The rate at which customers are leaving the service at a given time.
   (D) The total number of active customers at any given moment.
Q2. In survival analysis, what does the 'expected lifetime' represent for a customer?
   (A) The maximum possible duration a customer can stay with a company.
   (B) The average time a customer is predicted to remain with the company, derived from the survival probability curve.
   (C) The probability of a customer staying for more than 10 years.
   (D) The financial value a customer brings over their entire engagement period.
Q3. A company wants to determine how many years, on average, their customers stay without any 
intervention. Which aspect of survival analysis would directly answer this?
   (A) Hazard rate calculation.
   (B) Comparison of hazard ratios between customer groups.
   (C) The expected survival time derived from the overall survival curve.
   (D) Predicting the exact churn date for individual customers.
Q4. Which of the following is NOT a typical question that survival analysis helps answer in the 
context of customer churn?
   (A) How long do male customers stay compared to female customers?
   (B) Is our understanding of customer lifecycle accurate with reality?
   (C) What is the exact reason for every single customer's churn?
   (D) How the likelihood of customer churn changes over time?
Q5. The lecture mentions that survival analysis can be applied to diverse fields. In a manufacturing 
context, what might it be used to analyze?
   (A) The market share of a product line.
   (B) The lifetime of machine components.
   (C) Customer satisfaction scores for manufactured goods.
   (D) The efficiency of the production line.
Q6. What is a primary advantage of using survival regression techniques (like Cox Proportional 
Hazard) over simple descriptive statistics for churn analysis?
   (A) It allows for the creation of more visually appealing graphs.
   (B) It can only analyze two customer groups at a time.
   (C) It models the relationship between churn time and multiple customer characteristics simultaneously.
   (D) It guarantees 100% accuracy in predicting churn events.
Q7. Which statement accurately describes the Kaplan-Meier estimation technique's assumption 
about survival probability?
   (A) The probability of surviving a future time period is entirely dependent on all previous time periods.
   (B) The probability of surviving a given time period is independent of survival in previous time periods.
   (C) It only applies to continuous survival data, not discrete.
   (D) It assumes a constant hazard rate over time.
Q8. According to the Kaplan-Meier formula, S(t) = S(t-1) * (1 - d_j/n_j), what does 'd_j' represent?
   (A) The total number of customers who survived until time j.
   (B) The number of 'deaths' (churn events) that occurred at time period j.
   (C) The total number of customers at the start of the study.
   (D) The probability of survival at time period j.
Q9. What is the primary purpose of calculating a 'hazard rate' in survival analysis?
   (A) To determine the total cumulative number of events over time.
   (B) To quantify the instantaneous risk of an event (e.g., churn) occurring at a given time, given that it hasn't occurred 
yet.
   (C) To find the average survival time directly.
   (D) To calculate the total revenue generated by surviving customers.
Q10. When comparing two customer groups using Kaplan-Meier curves, what would indicate that the 
groups are significantly different in their survival patterns?
   (A) Their survival curves are exactly identical.
   (B) The p-value associated with the comparison is less than 0.05.
   (C) One group has a consistently higher number of total customers.
   (D) The curves cross multiple times.
Q11. In the context of the Kaplan-Meier summary output, if `S2 = S1 * (11/12)`, what does the term 
`11/12` represent?
   (A) The cumulative survival probability from time 0 to time 2.
   (B) The probability of an event (death/churn) occurring between time 1 and time 2.
   (C) The conditional probability of surviving from time 1 to time 2, given survival up to time 1.
   (D) The total number of individuals at risk at time 2.
Q12. Which method allows for the inclusion of multiple customer characteristics (covariates) like 
age, gender, and income to predict churn time?
   (A) Kaplan-Meier estimator.
   (B) Descriptive statistics only.
   (C) Cox Proportional Hazard Model.
   (D) Simple linear regression.
Q13. In a Cox Proportional Hazard Model, if the hazard ratio for a particular customer characteristic 
(e.g., 'high income' vs. 'low income') is found to be 0.5, what does this imply?
   (A) High-income customers are twice as likely to churn as low-income customers.
   (B) High-income customers have half the hazard rate of churning compared to low-income customers.
   (C) The churn rate for high-income customers is 50% higher than for low-income customers.
   (D) There is no significant difference in churn likelihood between the two groups.
Q14. A marketing manager wants to know if a recent loyalty program (intervention) has significantly 
reduced the churn rate for a specific customer segment. Which survival analysis technique would be 
most appropriate, and what would they look for in its results?
   (A) Kaplan-Meier; comparing the mean survival time of the two groups.
   (B) Cox Proportional Hazard Model; checking if the loyalty program covariate has a significant hazard ratio less than 1.
   (C) Kaplan-Meier; observing if the survival curves for the two groups visually diverge.
   (D) Descriptive statistics; comparing the absolute number of churned customers before and after the program.
Q15. What is the primary reason for taking the 'log' of likelihood or hazard rates in survival analysis, 
as mentioned in the lecture?
   (A) To make the numbers larger and easier to interpret.
   (B) To always ensure the rates are positive.
   (C) To transform very small probabilities into more 'handleable' numbers, preventing underflow issues.
   (D) To comply with regulatory statistical requirements.
Q16. Consider a Kaplan-Meier plot with two curves, C1 and C2. If C1 is consistently above C2, what 
does this generally imply?
   (A) C1 represents a group with a higher hazard rate than C2.
   (B) C2 represents a group with a higher survival probability than C1.
   (C) C1 represents a group with a higher survival probability (lower hazard) than C2.
   (D) Both groups have identical survival patterns.
Q17. In the R example, what does the `Surv(time, event)` function create?
   (A) A linear regression model for survival.
   (B) A Kaplan-Meier plot directly.
   (C) A survival object that combines time-to-event and event status (censoring).
   (D) A table of descriptive statistics for the ovarian data.
Q18. What is 'censoring' in the context of survival analysis?
   (A) Excluding certain customers from the analysis due to missing data.
   (B) The process of stopping the study before all subjects have experienced the event of interest.
   (C) Randomly assigning customers to treatment and control groups.
   (D) Adjusting for seasonal variations in churn rates.
Q19. If a `fit.coxph` summary shows a p-value of 0.8 for the 'ecog' variable, what can be concluded 
about its effect on the hazard rate?
   (A) Ecog has a very strong and significant impact on the hazard rate.
   (B) Ecog significantly reduces the hazard rate.
   (C) Ecog significantly increases the hazard rate.
   (D) Ecog does not have a statistically significant effect on the hazard rate at conventional levels (e.g., 0.05).
Q20. A customer churn analyst is comparing the survival curves of two treatment groups, A and B. 
They find that the p-value for the comparison is 0.3. What should be their conclusion?
   (A) Treatment group A is significantly better than B.
   (B) Treatment group B is significantly better than A.
   (C) There is no statistically significant difference in survival patterns between group A and group B.
   (D) More data is needed to make any conclusion.
Q21. Which of the following is an example of an 'event' in a customer churn survival analysis?
   (A) A customer updating their profile information.
   (B) A customer making a purchase.
   (C) A customer cancelling their subscription.
   (D) A customer logging into their account.
Q22. Why is customer lifetime value (CLV) calculation relevant to survival analysis, as per the lecture?
   (A) Survival analysis determines the exact monetary value of each customer.
   (B) Survival analysis provides the expected customer lifetime, which is a key input for CLV calculation over future 
periods.
   (C) Survival analysis directly calculates future revenue streams.
   (D) CLV is only applicable to short-term customer relationships.
Q23. The lecture discusses using survival analysis for 'potential intervention points.' What does this 
mean in practice for a business?
   (A) Identifying the precise moment to legally intervene in customer disputes.
   (B) Determining the optimal time to offer incentives or support to retain customers before they churn.
   (C) Calculating the financial cost of various customer interventions.
   (D) Analyzing the impact of external market events on customer behavior.
Q24. What is the main difference between Kaplan-Meier and Cox Proportional Hazard models in 
terms of what they can analyze?
   (A) Kaplan-Meier can handle multiple covariates, while Cox cannot.
   (B) Cox Proportional Hazard only works with categorical variables, while Kaplan-Meier handles continuous ones.
   (C) Kaplan-Meier primarily describes survival for groups, while Cox Proportional Hazard models the effect of multiple 
specific factors on the hazard rate.
   (D) Kaplan-Meier is a parametric model, and Cox Proportional Hazard is non-parametric.
Q25. A hazard ratio of 4.25 for 'resid.ds: yes' means that customers with residual disease (yes) have 
a hazard rate of churn that is how many times higher than those without residual disease (no)?
   (A) 0.25 times higher.
   (B) 1 time higher.
   (C) 4.25 times higher.
   (D) 42.5 times higher.
Q26. If the `ggforest` plot shows a hazard ratio for 'age group: young' as 0.11, with 'old' as the 
reference, what is the interpretation?
   (A) Young people have a hazard rate 11 times higher than old people.
   (B) Young people have a hazard rate 0.11 times (or about 9 times lower) than old people.
   (C) Age group has no significant impact on hazard rate.
   (D) Old people are 11% more likely to experience the event than young people.
Q27. In the R example, how was a new variable 'age group' (old/young) created from the 'age' 
variable?
   (A) By manually assigning each customer to a group.
   (B) Using a complex machine learning algorithm.
   (C) By applying a conditional statement (e.g., if age > X, then 'old', else 'young') using the `mutate` function.
   (D) It was an inherent variable in the original dataset.
Q28. What does a 'hazard ratio' in a Cox model fundamentally compare?
   (A) The survival probability of two different time points.
   (B) The cumulative number of events in two groups.
   (C) The hazard rate of one group or level of a covariate to a reference group or level.
   (D) The average age of customers in different segments.
Q29. If a company observes a steeper drop in the survival curve for its non-loyal customers 
compared to loyal customers, what does this indicate about their hazard rates?
   (A) Non-loyal customers have a lower hazard rate of churn.
   (B) Loyal customers have a higher hazard rate of churn.
   (C) Non-loyal customers have a higher hazard rate of churn.
   (D) Both groups have similar hazard rates.
Q30. Which industry application for survival analysis was NOT explicitly mentioned in the lecture?
   (A) Telecommunication.
   (B) Insurance.
   (C) Healthcare.
   (D) Education.
Q31. When interpreting the `summary(fit.coxph)` output, what information helps determine the 
statistical significance of a covariate?
   (A) Only the beta coefficient's value.
   (B) The hazard ratio alone.
   (C) The standard error of the coefficient.
   (D) The Z-value and its corresponding p-value.
Q32. A marketing team wants to analyze if a premium subscription tier leads to longer customer 
lifetimes than a basic tier. Which statistical test would be appropriate after fitting Kaplan-Meier 
curves for the two groups?
   (A) A t-test for independent samples.
   (B) A Chi-square test.
   (C) A log-rank test (or its equivalent p-value from survfit summary).
   (D) An ANOVA test.
Q33. What does a 'plus' sign (e.g., '150+') next to a time value in the `surv` object output signify?
   (A) The event occurred at exactly that time.
   (B) The subject was censored at that time, meaning the event had not yet occurred.
   (C) The subject's data is incomplete and should be removed.
   (D) The time is an estimated value, not exact.
Q34. According to the lecture, why is the analysis of 'customer lifecycle' important in survival 
analysis?
   (A) To categorize customers into different demographic groups.
   (B) To ensure the company's internal understanding of customer progression aligns with actual customer behavior 
and retention patterns.
   (C) To calculate the average transaction value over time.
   (D) To predict short-term fluctuations in sales.
Q35. Which type of variable is `fustat` (event status) typically in a survival analysis dataset?
   (A) Continuous numerical variable.
   (B) Categorical variable indicating event (e.g., 1 for event, 0 for censored).
   (C) Ordinal variable representing severity.
   (D) Date-time variable.
Q36. What happens if a factor (like 'ecog') is found to be not significant in a Cox Proportional Hazard 
model (p-value > 0.05)?
   (A) It means the factor significantly increases the hazard rate.
   (B) It means the factor significantly decreases the hazard rate.
   (C) It suggests that this factor, within the model, does not have a statistically discernible impact on the hazard rate.
   (D) It implies that the model itself is incorrectly specified.
Q37. A telecommunications company wants to reduce customer churn. Using survival analysis, they 
discover that customers who rarely use data services have a significantly higher hazard rate. What 
would be a logical intervention strategy based on this finding?
   (A) Increase the price of data services for all customers.
   (B) Offer data bundles and promotions to customers with low data usage to encourage engagement.
   (C) Eliminate data services entirely from their offerings.
   (D) Focus marketing efforts solely on customers with high data usage.
Q38. Which of these scenarios is an appropriate use case for survival analysis?
   (A) Predicting the exact next purchase item for a customer.
   (B) Forecasting the total sales volume for the next quarter.
   (C) Determining factors that influence the time until a patient recovers from a specific illness.
   (D) Segmenting customers based on their demographic characteristics.
Q39. If a hazard rate for a group is 'much lower' than another group, as described in the lecture with 
income groups, how would their survival curves visually compare?
   (A) The group with the much lower hazard rate would have a curve that drops much steeper.
   (B) The group with the much lower hazard rate would have a curve that drops much flatter (stays higher).
   (C) Both curves would be identical, only shifted horizontally.
   (D) The curves would frequently intersect each other.
Q40. What is the primary role of a 'covariate' (like age, gender, or treatment group) in a Cox 
Proportional Hazard Model?
   (A) To serve as the dependent variable.
   (B) To be the event status indicator.
   (C) To explain variation in the hazard rate.
   (D) To define the time scale of the analysis.
Q41. The lecture mentions 'log likelihood.' Why is this concept important in statistical modeling, 
especially when dealing with probabilities?
   (A) It directly provides the p-value for hypothesis testing.
   (B) It converts products of small probabilities into sums of more manageable log-probabilities, simplifying calculations 
and avoiding numerical underflow.
   (C) It normalizes the data to a standard distribution.
   (D) It measures the overall fit of the model to the data, regardless of probability values.
Q42. If a business wanted to identify *which* specific interventions (e.g., discounts, personalized 
emails, customer service calls) effectively reduce churn and by how much, which survival analysis 
technique would be most suitable?
   (A) Kaplan-Meier curves for each intervention, compared visually.
   (B) Calculating the simple average customer lifetime for each intervention group.
   (C) A Cox Proportional Hazard model including different interventions as covariates.
   (D) A logistic regression model predicting churn within a fixed period.
Q43. What is the key issue that survival analysis helps address in the context of customer churn, as 
highlighted in the lecture?
   (A) Determining the most profitable customer segments.
   (B) Understanding how the likelihood of customer churn changes over time and identifying intervention points.
   (C) Forecasting seasonal demand for products.
   (D) Optimizing advertising spend across different channels.
Q44. In the summary of a Kaplan-Meier fit, what does the column `n.event` represent?
   (A) The total number of individuals at risk at that time point.
   (B) The cumulative number of events that have occurred up to that time point.
   (C) The number of events (deaths/churns) that occurred specifically at that time point.
   (D) The number of individuals who were censored at that time point.
Q45. An insurance company uses survival analysis to study policyholder retention. If they find that 
younger policyholders have a hazard ratio of 0.7 compared to older policyholders, what can they 
infer?
   (A) Younger policyholders are 70% more likely to churn.
   (B) Younger policyholders are 30% less likely to churn.
   (C) Older policyholders are 70% less likely to churn.
   (D) There is no significant difference in churn likelihood based on age.
Q46. Which characteristic makes the Kaplan-Meier method 'non-parametric'?
   (A) It assumes a specific underlying distribution for the survival times.
   (B) It does not make assumptions about the underlying distribution of survival times.
   (C) It only works with parametric data types.
   (D) It requires a very large sample size to be accurate.
Q47. A retail company wants to use survival analysis to understand customer behavior. Specifically, 
they want to know the 'time till food customer starts purchasing nonfood.' What would be the 'event' 
in this analysis?
   (A) The first purchase of food items.
   (B) The last purchase of any item.
   (C) The first purchase of a non-food item by a customer previously only buying food.
   (D) The total revenue generated from food items.
Q48. What is meant by the 'proportional hazards' assumption in the Cox model?
   (A) It assumes that the hazard ratios between groups change over time.
   (B) It assumes that the effect of covariates on the hazard rate is constant over time (i.e., the hazard ratios are 
constant).
   (C) It assumes that all subjects have the same baseline hazard.
   (D) It assumes that hazards are directly proportional to survival times.
Q49. Why did the lecture mention 'log likelihood' as opposed to just 'likelihood' when discussing 
hazard rates?
   (A) Log likelihood is easier to compute derivatives for during optimization.
   (B) The likelihood values are always negative, so log transforms them to positive.
   (C) It simplifies interpretation of the hazard rates.
   (D) Log likelihood is a more common term in probability theory.
Q50. A bank is using survival analysis to model mortgage default. They want to compare the default 
rates of customers who received financial counseling versus those who didn't. Which survival 
analysis technique would be appropriate for a preliminary visual comparison?
   (A) Simple bar charts of default numbers.
   (B) Kaplan-Meier survival curves.
   (C) Linear regression.
   (D) Decision trees.
Q51. What is the primary output of the `ggforest` function in the R example, in the context of the Cox 
Proportional Hazard model?
   (A) A table of raw data points.
   (B) A visual representation of hazard ratios, their confidence intervals, and p-values for each covariate.
   (C) A Kaplan-Meier survival plot.
   (D) A scatter plot of age vs. survival time.
Q52. If a covariate's confidence interval for its hazard ratio in a Cox model includes 1, what does this 
typically suggest?
   (A) The covariate has a very strong and significant effect.
   (B) The covariate significantly increases the hazard.
   (C) The covariate significantly decreases the hazard.
   (D) The effect of the covariate on the hazard rate is not statistically significant.
Q53. The lecture concludes by emphasizing that churn management is 'immediate management' 
while lifetime increases need 'something much ago.' How does survival analysis support this idea?
   (A) By identifying factors that prevent churn proactively, thus extending customer lifetime.
   (B) By focusing only on immediate customer cancellations.
   (C) By ignoring the impact of past interventions.
   (D) By suggesting that all customers have a fixed lifetime.
Q54. What kind of data structure is represented by the `ovarian` dataset used in the R example?
   (A) Time-series data.
   (B) Cross-sectional data.
   (C) Panel data (repeated measures over time for the same subjects).
   (D) Survival data (time-to-event with censoring).
Q55. If a survival curve for customer group A drops much more gradually than for group B, what 
does this imply about their likelihood of churn?
   (A) Group A customers are more likely to churn than Group B.
   (B) Group B customers are more likely to churn than Group A.
   (C) Both groups have an equal likelihood of churn.
   (D) The data is insufficient to compare churn likelihood.
Q56. In the context of the Kaplan-Meier formula, if `d_j/n_j` is the probability of 'death' at time `j` 
(given survival until `j`), what does `1 - d_j/n_j` represent?
   (A) The cumulative probability of death up to time `j`.
   (B) The conditional probability of survival at time `j`.
   (C) The total number of individuals censored at time `j`.
   (D) The hazard rate at time `j`.
Q57. The lecture highlights that survival analysis can identify factors that 'drive churn.' Why is this 
more valuable than just knowing *when* customers churn?
   (A) It allows for precise prediction of individual customer churn dates.
   (B) It enables businesses to develop targeted strategies and interventions based on the identified drivers to prevent 
churn proactively.
   (C) It provides a clear financial value for each customer.
   (D) It confirms that churn is an unavoidable business phenomenon.
Q58. What would be the effect of a very high hazard rate on the overall survival probability curve?
   (A) The curve would stay very high for a long time.
   (B) The curve would drop very steeply and quickly.
   (C) The curve would show no change over time.
   (D) The curve would oscillate frequently.
Q59. Which of these is a valid application of survival analysis in the public sector, as mentioned?
   (A) Optimizing public transportation routes.
   (B) Predicting time intervals to critical events (e.g., reoffending, facility failure).
   (C) Analyzing public opinion polls.
   (D) Measuring the effectiveness of government advertising campaigns.
Q60. If a Kaplan-Meier plot shows two survival curves, and one is significantly above the other, what 
statistical interpretation can be made?
   (A) The group with the upper curve has a statistically higher survival probability.
   (B) The group with the lower curve has a statistically higher survival probability.
   (C) Both groups have identical survival characteristics.
   (D) The difference between the curves is due to measurement error.
Q61. A company wants to assess the impact of different customer support channels (phone, chat, 
email) on customer retention time. Which approach would be most effective using survival analysis?
   (A) Run a separate Kaplan-Meier for each channel and visually compare them.
   (B) Develop a Cox Proportional Hazard model with 'support channel' as a categorical covariate and compare hazard 
ratios.
   (C) Calculate the average retention time for each channel using simple means.
   (D) Use linear regression to predict retention time from channel type.
Answer Key
Q1: The probability that a customer will be 'alive' (i.e., retained) at or beyond a certain point in time.
The survival probability curve plots the probability that a subject (or customer, in this context) survives beyond a certain 
time point 't'. It represents the cumulative probability of retention over time.
Q2: The average time a customer is predicted to remain with the company, derived from the survival probability 
curve.
The expected lifetime is calculated by integrating (or summing, for discrete time) the survival probability function over 
time, giving the average duration a customer is expected to stay.
Q3: The expected survival time derived from the overall survival curve.
The expected survival time (or expected lifetime) directly answers the question of how long customers stay on average, 
assuming no specific interventions or distinguishing factors are applied.
Q4: What is the exact reason for every single customer's churn?
Survival analysis helps understand the *likelihood* and *factors* associated with churn over time, and compare groups. 
While it identifies significant drivers, it doesn't pinpoint the exact, specific reason for *every* individual customer's churn, 
which often involves qualitative data or deep individual analysis.
Q5: The lifetime of machine components.
The lecture explicitly states that in manufacturing, survival analysis has an application in 'lifetime machine components', 
predicting how long parts will last.
Q6: It models the relationship between churn time and multiple customer characteristics simultaneously.
Survival regression, particularly Cox proportional hazard, is powerful because it allows us to include multiple covariates 
(customer characteristics) to understand their combined impact on churn time.
Q7: The probability of surviving a given time period is independent of survival in previous time periods.
Kaplan-Meier assumes that the probability of staying in time period 't' is independent of whether one survived previous 
time periods, meaning the transition probability from t-1 to t is standalone.
Q8: The number of 'deaths' (churn events) that occurred at time period j.
In the Kaplan-Meier formula, 'd_j' refers to the number of events (deaths or churns) observed at time 'j', while 'n_j' is the 
number of individuals at risk of experiencing the event at time 'j'.
Q9: To quantify the instantaneous risk of an event (e.g., churn) occurring at a given time, given that it hasn't 
occurred yet.
The hazard rate measures the instantaneous rate at which an event occurs at a specific time, given that the individual 
has survived up to that time.
Q10: The p-value associated with the comparison is less than 0.05.
A p-value less than a conventional significance level (e.g., 0.05) indicates that the observed differences in survival 
between the two groups are statistically significant, and unlikely to have occurred by chance.
Q11: The conditional probability of surviving from time 1 to time 2, given survival up to time 1.
The term `(1 - d_j/n_j)` or in this specific case `11/12` represents the proportion of individuals who survived the current 
interval, given they were at risk at the beginning of that interval.
Q12: Cox Proportional Hazard Model.
The Cox Proportional Hazard Model is designed to analyze the effect of multiple covariates on the hazard rate, allowing 
for a more comprehensive understanding of churn drivers.
Q13: High-income customers have half the hazard rate of churning compared to low-income customers.
A hazard ratio of 0.5 means that the group in question (high income) has 0.5 times the hazard (or risk) of the reference 
group (low income), implying a reduction in risk by 50%.
Q14: Cox Proportional Hazard Model; checking if the loyalty program covariate has a significant hazard ratio 
less than 1.
The Cox model is suitable for assessing the impact of specific interventions (as covariates) on the hazard rate. A 
significant hazard ratio less than 1 for the intervention variable would indicate a reduction in the hazard of churn.
Q15: To transform very small probabilities into more 'handleable' numbers, preventing underflow issues.
The lecture explains that probabilities can be very small (e.g., 0.000001), and taking a log transforms them into more 
manageable numbers (e.g., -6 for 10^-6), which is better for computation and interpretation.
Q16: C1 represents a group with a higher survival probability (lower hazard) than C2.
A curve that stays higher on a survival plot indicates a higher probability of survival (i.e., lower risk or hazard of the 
event) over time. Therefore, C1 has better survival.
Q17: A survival object that combines time-to-event and event status (censoring).
The `Surv()` function in R's `survival` package creates a 'survival object' which is the fundamental data structure used 
by `survfit()` and `coxph()`. It encodes the time until the event and whether the event actually occurred or was censored.
Q18: The process of stopping the study before all subjects have experienced the event of interest.
Censoring occurs when the event of interest (e.g., churn) has not happened for a customer by the end of the study, or 
they drop out for reasons unrelated to the event. Their survival time is known for at least a certain duration.
Q19: Ecog does not have a statistically significant effect on the hazard rate at conventional levels (e.g., 0.05).
A p-value of 0.8 is much higher than 0.05, indicating that we cannot reject the null hypothesis that 'ecog' has no effect 
on the hazard rate. Therefore, its effect is not statistically significant.
Q20: There is no statistically significant difference in survival patterns between group A and group B.
A p-value of 0.3 is greater than the common significance level of 0.05, meaning that any observed differences between 
the groups are not statistically significant and could likely be due to random chance.
Q21: A customer cancelling their subscription.
In churn analysis, an 'event' is typically the occurrence of churn, which is represented by a customer cancelling their 
subscription or discontinuing service.
Q22: Survival analysis provides the expected customer lifetime, which is a key input for CLV calculation over 
future periods.
The expected lifetime from survival analysis tells you 'how long' to project the value for, making it crucial for accurately 
calculating CLV over a customer's anticipated relationship with the company.
Q23: Determining the optimal time to offer incentives or support to retain customers before they churn.
Identifying intervention points means understanding when customers are most at risk of churning, allowing businesses 
to proactively engage with them through targeted offers or support to increase retention.
Q24: Kaplan-Meier primarily describes survival for groups, while Cox Proportional Hazard models the effect of 
multiple specific factors on the hazard rate.
Kaplan-Meier is excellent for visualizing and comparing survival curves between distinct groups. Cox is more advanced, 
allowing you to include and assess the impact of various continuous and categorical covariates on survival.
Q25: 4.25 times higher.
A hazard ratio directly indicates how many times higher (or lower, if less than 1) the hazard is for one group compared 
to the reference group. So, 4.25 means 4.25 times higher.
Q26: Young people have a hazard rate 0.11 times (or about 9 times lower) than old people.
A hazard ratio of 0.11 means the young group's hazard is 0.11 times that of the old group. This is equivalent to saying 
their hazard is about 1/0.11 "H 9 times lower.
Q27: By applying a conditional statement (e.g., if age > X, then 'old', else 'young') using the `mutate` function.
The transcript states, 'mutate means if you change and put something here per age group is if the age group is higher 
than 15 than its old or otherwise it is young.' This is a conditional assignment.
Q28: The hazard rate of one group or level of a covariate to a reference group or level.
The hazard ratio is a ratio of hazard rates. It quantifies how much a particular covariate (or a level within a covariate) 
changes the hazard of the event compared to a baseline or reference level.
Q29: Non-loyal customers have a higher hazard rate of churn.
A steeper drop in the survival curve signifies that a higher proportion of customers are experiencing the event (churn) in 
that time period, which directly translates to a higher hazard rate.
Q30: Education.
The lecture mentioned Telecommunication, Insurance, Mortgages, Mail order catalogue, Retail, Manufacturing, Public 
Sector, and alluded to healthcare (ovarian cancer example), but did not explicitly list 'Education' as an application.
Q31: The Z-value and its corresponding p-value.
The Z-value tests the hypothesis that the coefficient is zero, and the p-value derived from it directly indicates the 
statistical significance of the covariate's effect on the hazard rate.
Q32: A log-rank test (or its equivalent p-value from survfit summary).
The log-rank test is commonly used to compare the survival curves of two or more groups in Kaplan-Meier analysis, 
providing a p-value to assess statistical significance.
Q33: The subject was censored at that time, meaning the event had not yet occurred.
The 'plus' sign denotes censoring, meaning the individual was observed for at least that amount of time, and the event 
of interest had not happened by the end of their observation period.
Q34: To ensure the company's internal understanding of customer progression aligns with actual customer 
behavior and retention patterns.
The lecture states, 'is our understanding of customer lifecycle accurate with reality. So, this is something that I can also 
try to find out...'
Q35: Categorical variable indicating event (e.g., 1 for event, 0 for censored).
The `fustat` variable (like other event status indicators) is a binary categorical variable, usually coded 1 for event 
occurrence (e.g., death/churn) and 0 for censoring.
Q36: It suggests that this factor, within the model, does not have a statistically discernible impact on the hazard 
rate.
A non-significant p-value for a factor indicates that we do not have enough evidence to conclude that it has a real effect 
on the hazard rate, suggesting it might be removed from a parsimonious model.
Q37: Offer data bundles and promotions to customers with low data usage to encourage engagement.
If low data usage correlates with higher churn hazard, an intervention aimed at increasing data engagement (like 
offering bundles) would be a logical strategy to try and reduce that hazard.
Q38: Determining factors that influence the time until a patient recovers from a specific illness.
Survival analysis is perfectly suited for 'time-to-event' data, where the 'event' is recovery and the 'time' is the duration 
until recovery, and one might want to understand influencing factors.
Q39: The group with the much lower hazard rate would have a curve that drops much flatter (stays higher).
A lower hazard rate means a lower instantaneous risk of the event, so more individuals survive for longer, resulting in a 
survival curve that stays higher and drops less steeply.
Q40: To explain variation in the hazard rate.
Covariates are independent variables whose effect on the hazard rate is being investigated within the Cox model.
Q41: It converts products of small probabilities into sums of more manageable log-probabilities, simplifying 
calculations and avoiding numerical underflow.
Multiplying many small probabilities can lead to 'numerical underflow' (numbers becoming too small for computers to 
handle accurately). Taking logs converts these products to sums, which are much easier to compute and handle.
Q42: A Cox Proportional Hazard model including different interventions as covariates.
The Cox model is ideal for this as it can simultaneously assess the impact (and significance) of multiple interventions 
(as covariates) on the hazard rate of churn, providing hazard ratios for each.
Q43: Understanding how the likelihood of customer churn changes over time and identifying intervention 
points.
The lecture explicitly states, 'The key issues are, so how the likelihood of customer churn changes over time and 
determine the potential intervention points.'
Q44: The number of events (deaths/churns) that occurred specifically at that time point.
As explained in the R output walkthrough, `n.event` refers to `dj`, the number of deaths (events) that occurred at that 
specific time period.
Q45: Younger policyholders are 30% less likely to churn.
A hazard ratio of 0.7 means younger policyholders have 70% of the hazard of older policyholders, which translates to a 
30% reduction in their hazard (1 - 0.7 = 0.3 or 30%).
Q46: It does not make assumptions about the underlying distribution of survival times.
A key feature of non-parametric methods is that they do not assume a particular form (e.g., normal, exponential) for the 
underlying data distribution.
Q47: The first purchase of a non-food item by a customer previously only buying food.
The event of interest is explicitly defined as 'starts purchasing nonfood', so the first non-food purchase marks the 
occurrence of this event.
Q48: It assumes that the effect of covariates on the hazard rate is constant over time (i.e., the hazard ratios are 
constant).
The proportional hazards assumption is critical for the Cox model; it posits that the ratio of hazard rates for any two 
individuals (or groups) remains constant over time, even if the hazards themselves change.
Q49: Log likelihood is easier to compute derivatives for during optimization.
While the lecture primarily stressed numerical 'handleability' for small numbers, a deeper statistical reason for using log-
likelihood (especially in maximum likelihood estimation) is that it transforms products into sums, making differentiation 
for optimization much simpler.
Q50: Kaplan-Meier survival curves.
Kaplan-Meier curves are ideal for visualizing and comparing the survival (or in this case, non-default) probabilities over 
time for distinct groups.
Q51: A visual representation of hazard ratios, their confidence intervals, and p-values for each covariate.
The `ggforest` plot provides a forest plot, a specialized visualization for Cox model outputs, showing the estimated 
hazard ratios and their confidence intervals, along with significance levels for each factor.
Q52: The effect of the covariate on the hazard rate is not statistically significant.
If the 95% confidence interval for a hazard ratio includes 1, it means that a hazard ratio of 1 (no effect) is a plausible 
value, hence the effect is not statistically significant.
Q53: By identifying factors that prevent churn proactively, thus extending customer lifetime.
Survival analysis, especially Cox models, helps identify covariates that influence the hazard rate. Understanding these 
factors allows businesses to implement proactive strategies ('something much ago') to reduce churn risk and increase 
overall customer lifetime, rather than just reacting to imminent churn.
Q54: Survival data (time-to-event with censoring).
The `ovarian` dataset is used for survival analysis, containing `futime` (follow-up time or survival time) and `fustat` 
(event status), which are characteristic components of survival data.
Q55: Group B customers are more likely to churn than Group A.
A gradual drop in a survival curve indicates a higher probability of survival (i.e., lower likelihood of the event, or churn, 
occurring). Conversely, a steeper drop means a higher likelihood of churn.
Q56: The conditional probability of survival at time `j`.
If `d_j/n_j` is the conditional probability of experiencing the event, then `1` minus that value must be the conditional 
probability of *not* experiencing the event, i.e., surviving that period.
Q57: It enables businesses to develop targeted strategies and interventions based on the identified drivers to 
prevent churn proactively.
Knowing the drivers (the 'why' or 'what') allows for actionable insights – businesses can then manipulate these factors or 
intervene to mitigate the risk, which is more powerful than just knowing the 'when'.
Q58: The curve would drop very steeply and quickly.
A high hazard rate means a high instantaneous risk of the event, leading to a rapid decline in the proportion of 
individuals surviving over time, hence a steep drop in the survival curve.
Q59: Predicting time intervals to critical events (e.g., reoffending, facility failure).
The lecture states, 'in public sectaries, time intervals to critical events.'
Q60: The group with the upper curve has a statistically higher survival probability.
The higher curve on a Kaplan-Meier plot visually represents better survival. If the difference is statistically significant 
(based on a p-value), then this higher survival probability is not due to chance.
Q61: Develop a Cox Proportional Hazard model with 'support channel' as a categorical covariate and compare 
hazard ratios.
A Cox model allows for the comparison of multiple categories within a covariate (like different support channels) and 
provides statistically robust hazard ratios to quantify their differential impact on retention (survival).

Q1. Which of the following is NOT typically considered a characteristic of a service, as described in 
the lecture?
   (A) It is intangible.
   (B) It can be stored for later consumption.
   (C) It is highly dependent on human-to-human interaction.
   (D) Consumption often involves 'moments-of-truth'.
Q2. According to the lecture, why is maintaining customer relationships particularly important for 
service providers like doctors or hospitals?
   (A) They generate all their revenue from a single interaction.
   (B) Services are typically tangible and easily re-sold.
   (C) They do not make all their money from a single interaction, relying on multiple 'moments-of-truth'.
   (D) Customer preferences for medical care are highly stable over long periods.
Q3. The lecture uses the example of a customer with two mobile SIM cards to illustrate which 
challenge in customer churn detection?

   (B) The issue of customer loyalty programs failing to prevent switching.
   (C) A customer reducing consumption with one provider but shifting it to another, making churn harder to identify.
   (D) The challenge of tracking customer movement across geographical regions.

shortly after receiving it. If Bank X relies on Average Monthly Balance (MAB) to identify 'alive' 
customers, what is the likely outcome?

   (B) Bank X will perceive the customer as highly profitable due to the large initial deposit.

usage being elsewhere.
   (D) Bank X will immediately close the account due to insufficient activity.

   (D) The role of product packaging in service industries.
Q42. Why is 'conjoint' analysis mentioned in the recap of previous weeks?
   (A) To study customer perceptions of product attributes and new product development.
   (B) To analyze the effectiveness of advertising campaigns.
   (C) To predict stock market fluctuations.
   (D) To measure employee satisfaction.
Q43. Which of the following describes a 'service encounter' based on the lecture?
   (A) A purely automated transaction with no human involvement.
   (B) A long-term contract signed between a customer and a service provider.
   (C) A specific instance of human-to-human interaction or consumption where a service is delivered and experienced.
   (D) The initial phase of market research for a new service.
Q44. The banking example assumes a salary of 1 lakh rupees per month. If the salary is received on 
the 1st and transferred out on the 10th of a 30-day month, what is the calculated mean account 
balance presented in the lecture?
   (A) 1 lakh rupees
   (B) 50,000 rupees
   (C) 33,000 rupees
   (D) 0 rupees
Q45. Why is a clear 'definition' of churn emphasized as the first step in managing it?
   (A) To simplify data collection by ignoring complex scenarios.
   (B) To ensure consistent and accurate identification of customers who are actually leaving.
   (C) To meet regulatory requirements for customer data.
   (D) To standardize the amount of money spent by churning customers.
Q46. The lecture suggests that customer segmentation helps in understanding that 'what customers 
want' differs. This implies that:
   (A) All customers have the same needs and preferences.
   (B) Customer needs are constant over time.
   (C) Different customer groups may have distinct needs and preferences.
   (D) Segmentation is only useful for tangible products.

   (A) Course administrator
   (B) Teaching assistant
   (C) Professor taking this course for you
   (D) Guest speaker

   (A) Advertising models
   (B) Customer demand forecasting
   (C) What customers want
   (D) Retail marketing
Q49. The lecturer considers 'retail' as a type of service. This classification is likely due to retail's 
emphasis on:
   (A) Storing physical goods in large warehouses.
   (B) The intangible experience of shopping, customer interaction, and delivery of value.
   (C) Its unique pricing strategies not found in other industries.
   (D) Its reliance on manufacturing processes.
Q50. In the context of the course recap, the lecturer stated that 'quite a bit of extensive pricing

   (A) Theoretical economic principles of pricing.

   (C) Historical pricing trends in various markets.

Q51. If a customer in the banking example is identified as 'alive' based on MAB but uses another 
bank for primary transactions, what is the 'old bank' missing?
   (A) The customer's bank statements from the new bank.
   (B) The complete picture of the customer's transactional loyalty and primary banking relationship.
   (C) Information about the customer's investment portfolio.
   (D) Data on the customer's credit score.
Q52. The lecture explicitly mentions 'professional and non professional services'. What does this 
distinction primarily refer to?
   (A) The legal status of the service provider.
   (B) The level of specialized knowledge, training, or certification required for the service.
   (C) Whether the service is offered for profit or non-profit.
   (D) The geographical reach of the service.
Q53. What is the ultimate benefit derived from successfully controlling customer churn and making a 
customer stay longer, as indicated at the end of the lecture?
   (A) Reduced operational costs.
   (B) Increased market research opportunities.

after noticing a drop, why might this offer fail to retain the customer's data consumption?
   (A) The customer is unwilling to try new offers.
   (B) The customer's reason for shifting was not necessarily cost-related, and the offer doesn't address the underlying 
issue.
   (C) The competitor's network coverage is superior.
   (D) The customer prefers not to use data at all.
Q57. The lecture implies that accurately identifying churn allows companies to:
   (A) Reduce their overall marketing budget by cutting all promotions.
   (B) Focus retention efforts on customers who are genuinely at risk of leaving.
   (C) Increase their customer acquisition cost.

   (B) They could lose out on revenue from the customer's primary transactions and relationship, despite appearing to 
retain them.
   (C) Their ATM network might become overutilized.
   (D) They will be unable to process international wire transfers.
Q60. The lecture mentions that 'marketing mix models' were covered previously. These models 
typically integrate analysis of:
   (A) Only product and price.
   (B) Only promotion and place.
   (C) The combined effect of multiple marketing variables (Product, Price, Place, Promotion).
   (D) Competitor advertising expenditure.
Q61. If a customer stops taking a service 'before' their expected average lifespan, what state are they 
considered to be in, according to the lecture?
   (A) Satisfied
   (B) Loyal
   (C) Churned (not alive)
   (D) Temporarily inactive
Q62. The ability to 'identify a switching properly' is crucial because:
   (A) All customer switching is illegal.
   (B) It allows companies to understand the true reasons for customer disengagement and respond effectively.
   (C) It helps in forcing customers to stay with the current provider.
   (D) It's primarily for internal audit purposes.
Answer Key
Q1: It can be stored for later consumption.
The lecture explicitly states that services 'cannot be stored' and 'you have to consume then and there,' making 'It can be 
stored for later consumption' an incorrect characteristic.
Q2: They do not make all their money from a single interaction, relying on multiple 'moments-of-truth'.
The lecture explains that service providers like doctors or hospitals 'do not make money from a customer or patient in 
one single interaction' and aim for 'multiple moments-of-truth' to provide value and generate revenue.
Q3: A customer reducing consumption with one provider but shifting it to another, making churn harder to 
identify.
The mobile phone example highlights a customer reducing data services with one provider and shifting consumption to 
another, making it difficult for the first provider to accurately detect true churn versus partial switching.
Q4: Bank X might mistakenly consider the customer 'alive' because the MAB meets the threshold, despite 
active usage being elsewhere.
The lecture explicitly states that even with a large portion of the month showing zero balance, the initial salary deposit 
could result in an MAB that makes the bank mistakenly believe the customer is still 'alive' and profitable, despite all 
transactions happening in Bank Y.
Q5: To develop new product features to attract non-customers.
The lecture focuses on understanding and managing existing customer relationships to prevent churn and assess their 
value, not on developing new products for non-customers.
Q6: Customer demand and pricing exercises.
The lecture states, 'Then we have discussed about customer demand and based on customer demand, we have done 
various kinds of pricing, quite a bit of extensive pricing exercises has been done.'
Q7: They represent critical junctures of human-to-human interaction where service quality is experienced.
The lecture describes services as 'highly dependent on human to human interaction and moments-of-truth,' indicating 
these are key interaction points for service experience.
Q8: To demonstrate that even non-profit entities require sustained customer relationships for perceived value.
The lecture applies the concept of 'all round service' and multiple interactions to government, stating 'whether it is a 
profit making or non profit making' and 'it is not based on one single interaction,' implying the importance of sustained 
value for non-profits too.
Q9: To provide specific services or offerings to convert them back or stop their switching behavior.
The lecture states, 'If you can identify that in a better way... then you can target those customers particularly so that you 
can give them certain kind of service, certain kind of offerings so that they can be converted back or their switching 
behavior can be stopped.'
Q10: The duration for which a customer remains actively engaged and uses a service provider.
The lecturer defines lifespan as 'that is the lifespan, that is something that if a customer stays up to that time period, 
then he is alive, his relationship is alive.'
Q11: Product
The lecturer says, 'And in the product is actually something that we discussed a little bit in conjoint and new product 
development is something that we will discuss later also.'
Q12: Can be stored and used later, while services cannot.
The lecture states, 'you buy a product... you can keep it somewhere, you can store it somewhere and you can use it 
later. But services is something that you cannot use later, you have to consume then and there.'
Q13: The customer has stopped all internet activity.
The lecture asks, 'So do you think that this guy has stopped using data service at all? He has stopped doing any 
internet connection, any internet related activity at all? No, he has shifted the consumption... from one service provided 
to other service provider.'
Q14: To understand why customers leave and develop strategies to prevent it.
The lecture aims to find out 'what are the various nuances that can predict a customer churning behavior' and 'their 
switching behavior can be stopped.'
Q15: Varies significantly depending on the type of business or service.
The lecture states, 'how much time is vary depending on which kind of business you are in,' giving examples like mobile 
phone service vs. educational service providers.
Q16: Sustained engagement through multiple interactions generates more value over time.
The lecture emphasizes 'keeping this particular relationship alive, so that multiple moments-of-truth can be created,' 
leading to 'more amount of money' or better value exchange over time.
Q17: The difficulty in getting a complete 360-degree view of a customer's total consumption across 
competitors.
This statement highlights the information asymmetry between service provider 1 and the customer's overall 
consumption patterns, which include a competitor.
Q18: The point of customer churn.
This directly defines what it means for a customer to no longer be considered an active, 'alive' customer, which is the 
definition of churn.
Q19: Because it was covered in the context of demand forecasting.
The lecture states, 'We, in the demand forecasting also we have talked about how new product diffusion happens.'
Q20: Perishability (cannot be stored)
The lecture explicitly states, 'A service is something which is, which cannot be stored.' This is known as perishability.
Q21: They might waste marketing resources targeting an unengaged customer, failing to address the actual 
reason for disengagement.
The lecture explains that the bank 'do not care about you actually, they do not think that you are a customer who can 
switch,' suggesting they won't take appropriate retention actions, potentially wasting effort on an already lost customer.
Q22: Churn directly impacts the duration of a customer relationship, which in turn affects their total lifetime 
value.
The lecture implies this connection by stating that if a customer can be made to 'stay for a certain period of time, then 
what kind of money that I can make,' linking retention (avoiding churn) to monetary value (CLV).
Q23: The provider only sees reduced consumption and doesn't understand the underlying reason for the shift.
The lecturer states, 'he just sees that okay, the consumption dropped by 40%. ... But he is not tracking down exactly why 
is the reason this guy have shifted.'
Q24: Revenue generation often occurs over multiple interactions rather than a single transaction.
The lecture emphasizes 'you do not get all your money in one single interaction,' and the goal is to create 'multiple 
moments-of-truth' to maximize value.
Q25: VG SOM, IIT Kharagpur
The lecturer introduces himself as 'Doctor Swagato Chatterjee from VG SOM, IIT Kharagpur'.
Q26: To understand how to precisely predict customer churning behavior and its financial implications.
The lecture concludes by stating, 'we will actually find out that what are the various nuances that can predict a customer 
churning behavior. And... if I can control customer churn... then what kind of money that I can make... and all those 
details can be found out.'
Q27: Cannot be touched or physically possessed.
The lecture clarifies, 'It is intangible, you cannot touch a service, you cannot feel a service you can only consume a 
service.'
Q28: Because one provider offers better deals for specific needs (e.g., data) and another for other needs (e.g., 
calls).
The lecture explains, 'Because sometimes some particular service provider gives some offer which is more suitable to 
your needs X and some other service provider... gives some other offers, which is more suitable to your need Y.'
Q29: Promotion
The lecturer states, 'In advertising models, we majorly focused on the promotion part.'
Q30: Manufacturing of consumer electronics
Airlines, hospitality, and restaurants were given as examples of services. Manufacturing is a product-based industry, 
contrasting with services.
Q31: Churn definition can be complex and context-dependent.
The statement 'depending on situations also how you measure that whether a customer is alive or not alive, also 
changes' directly supports the idea of context-dependency.
Q32: Understanding what customers want.
The lecturer says, 'So we have understood that what customers want after the R coding and etc, we started with that, 
that what customers want.'
Q33: To maximize profit by ensuring long-term customer engagement and repeat business.
The lecture links sustained relationships to 'multiple moments-of-truth' that can 'give you better value and in turn can 
get, in exchange they can get more amount of money.'
Q34: It means service quality can be highly variable and influenced by individual interactions.
While not explicitly stated as 'heterogeneity,' the reliance on human interaction implies variability, as human 
performance is not perfectly consistent. This is a common service characteristic.
Q35: Segmentation analysis (implied through 'divided into multiple groups')
The lecturer states, 'we actually also have seen that how customers can be divided into multiple groups based on their 
preferences, based on their behavior.' This describes market segmentation.
Q36: By converting needs, wants, and desires into a willingness and ability to purchase.
The lecturer states, 'what is the needs, wants and desires for those particular customers and how you can convert them 
to a demand.' This implies that demand is a step beyond just having needs/wants/desires, involving the ability to act on 
them.
Q37: Retail marketing and retail related analytics.
The lecture states, 'After that, we are focused on retail marketing and retail related analytics quite a bit.' This comes 
before the introduction to services.
Q38: misinterpretation
The bank thinks the customer is 'alive' and 'profitable' due to MAB, but the customer is actually shifting all activity 
elsewhere, leading to a misinterpretation of their true engagement.
Q39: Corporate training typically involves a shorter, more intense engagement period.
The lecturer contrasts 'years' for mobile service with 'six months at max' for a corporate trainer, implying the 
engagement period for the trainer is shorter-term.
Q40: It enables targeted retention efforts to prevent loss of valuable customers.
The lecture states, 'If you can identify that in a better way... then you can target those customers particularly so that you 
can give them certain kind of service... so that they can be converted back or their switching behavior can be stopped.'
Q41: The financial returns ('what kind of money that I can make') from such retention.
The lecturer says, 'if I can make a customer stay for a certain period of time, then what kind of money that I can make, 
which type of customer should I target and all those details can be found out.'
Q42: To study customer perceptions of product attributes and new product development.
The lecturer states, 'And in the product is actually something that we discussed a little bit in conjoint and new product 
development is something that we will discuss later also.' Conjoint analysis is used for product design and feature 
valuation.
Q43: A specific instance of human-to-human interaction or consumption where a service is delivered and 
experienced.
The lecture uses terms like 'moments-of-truth based service encounter or consumption encounters' and emphasizes 
human-to-human interaction in service delivery.
Q44: 33,000 rupees
The lecturer calculates, '1 lakh for 10 days, that means 33,000 for 30 days if you do just the maths... So, mean account 
balance is 33,000.'
Q45: To ensure consistent and accurate identification of customers who are actually leaving.
The lecture explicitly states, 'the definition has to be clear. Once the definition is clear, you have to find out how I can 
correctly identify who are the people who are has higher probability of churning.'
Q46: Different customer groups may have distinct needs and preferences.
The lecturer states, 'And then within those groups, how the, what customers want, that differs, we have seen that.' This 
is a core purpose of segmentation.
Q47: Professor taking this course for you
Dr. Swagato Chatterjee introduces himself as 'who is taking this course for you'.
Q48: What customers want
The lecturer says, 'So we have understood that what customers want after the R coding and etc, we started with that, 
that what customers want.'
Q49: The intangible experience of shopping, customer interaction, and delivery of value.
Although retail involves tangible products, the 'retail is one service actually' statement implies recognition of its service 
aspects (e.g., customer service, shopping experience, convenience), aligning with the described characteristics of 
services like human interaction and moments-of-truth.
Q50: Practical application and analytical methods for setting prices.
The phrase 'pricing exercises' in an 'Analytics course' suggests a focus on practical, analytical methods rather than 
purely theoretical or historical aspects.
Q51: The complete picture of the customer's transactional loyalty and primary banking relationship.
The lecture explains that the customer 'is not the customer of Bank A, you are actually shifting all your money to bank B 
and then doing all the transactions there,' indicating Bank A misses the actual primary relationship.
Q52: The level of specialized knowledge, training, or certification required for the service.
While not directly defined, in common business parlance, 'professional services' (e.g., legal, medical, consulting) imply 
specialized expertise, while 'non-professional' (e.g., cleaning, general repairs) typically do not require extensive formal 
training or certification.
Q53: Greater financial returns or 'money that I can make'.
The lecture states, 'if I can control customer churn and if I can make a customer stay for a certain period of time, then 
what kind of money that I can make'.
Q54: Marketing advertising models.
The lecture clarifies, 'In advertising models, we majorly focused on the promotion part.'
Q55: Demand creation
The lecturer asks 'how you can convert them to a demand.'
Q56: The customer's reason for shifting was not necessarily cost-related, and the offer doesn't address the 
underlying issue.
The lecture notes, 'Probably the cost is not the reason, probably something else is the reason. So until and unless you 
can identify that properly, you can identify a switching properly.'
Q57: Focus retention efforts on customers who are genuinely at risk of leaving.
The lecture states, 'If you can identify that in a better way, in a reasonably good way, then you can target those 
customers particularly so that you can give them certain kind of service... so that they can be converted back or their 
switching behavior can be stopped.'
Q58: Week 9
The lecturer states, 'Today we are in week 9 and this is session 1 in week 9.'
Q59: They could lose out on revenue from the customer's primary transactions and relationship, despite 
appearing to retain them.
The lecture clearly indicates that while the bank considers the customer 'alive,' the customer is actually shifting all their 
money and transactions to another bank, meaning the 'old bank' loses the associated revenue and active relationship.
Q60: The combined effect of multiple marketing variables (Product, Price, Place, Promotion).
The lecturer explicitly mentions the 4 Ps, stating, 'So out of 4 Ps, price, place, promotion, we have talked about in 
marketing mix, we have talked about all of them.'
Q61: Churned (not alive)
The lecture states, 'If he comes goes out of the customer base before that or if he stops taking service before that then 
this customer is not alive.' This is the definition of churn.
Q62: It allows companies to understand the true reasons for customer disengagement and respond effectively.
The lecture states, 'So until and unless you can identify that properly, you can identify a switching properly... Probably 
the cost is not the reason, probably something else is the reason.' Understanding 'why' is key.

   (D) Marketing Campaign Response Data

to:
   (A) Increase advertising spend to attract new customers.
   (B) Understand why customers churn to implement retention strategies.
   (C) Segment customers for personalized marketing offers.
   (D) Optimize product pricing for maximum profit.

   (B) Partner status
   (C) Senior Citizen status
   (D) Dependent status

   (C) Approximately 26%
   (D) Approximately 10%

   (C) Normalization
   (D) Outlier removal

Q35. Which of the following describes the Telco customer churn data, based on the initial 
description?
   (A) It contains only demographic information.
